There is no sign of targeted positive reinforcement toward high contributors or toward those who appear prosocial in other ways. The most parsimonious interpretation is that they value cooperation but treat reward as an unnecessary or inefficient expense, preferring to let their own contribution be their main prosocial act. Whether they refrain from rewarding due to cost–benefit calculation, indifference to expressive gratitude, or lack of belief in reward as an effective signal is unknown.

They never spend on rewards at any point, even though rewarding is sometimes used extensively by others. This suggests a strong reluctance to incur any direct cost for social signaling or norm reinforcement. They do not selectively reward high contributors, consistent helpers, or those who reward them; in fact, they do not reward anyone at all. They therefore do not use the reward mechanism strategically to build alliances, to encourage cooperation, or to express approval. Their behavior fits a “reward-averse observer”: they rely on the presence of others’ rewards but do not participate, preserving their own payoff instead. This indicates low willingness to sacrifice for indirect, reputational, or future-oriented gains in this environment, at least given the observed group dynamics. Whether this stems from skepticism about the efficacy of rewards, indifference to social signaling, or simple payoff-maximization is unknown, but their consistent non-use of rewards shows that any such motives are dominated by the desire to avoid these costs.

This player is an active but selective user of the reward mechanism. They are willing to incur non-trivial costs to reward others across many rounds, but do not reward in every opportunity. Their rewarding is not blanket generosity; instead, it targets specific players whose behavior they likely interpret as especially cooperative, constructive, or supportive of their own interests. They do not use reward as a purely efficiency-based tool (since the mechanism here is cost-neutral in aggregate). Instead, they treat reward as a signaling device and a way to shape norms: they repeatedly spend to recognize high contributors in the group and, at times, to support or acknowledge particular individuals, even when those individuals are not the absolute top contributors. This pattern suggests they may be trying to stabilize cooperative clusters and encourage allies rather than maximize short-run payoffs. They also appear to react to their own payoff history: after periods of heavy reward expenditure and low personal returns, their willingness to spend drops sharply for a while, then returns in more modest doses. This implies they monitor the cost-benefit balance of rewarding and adjust intensity accordingly, rather than sticking dogmatically to a moral rule. Overall, they use reward strategically for norm reinforcement and relationship management, not as indiscriminate altruism.

This player almost never spends on rewards and is effectively a non-rewarder. In nearly all rounds they pay nothing to influence others via the reward mechanism, even when there are clear high contributors and clear low contributors in the group and even though rewarders are visible. On the rare occasion they do spend on reward, the spending is very small and not repeated, and there is no clear pattern of using rewards to build alliances, reinforce cooperation, or signal gratitude. Because they do not systematically target high contributors or reciprocate prior rewards, it is difficult to infer a principled reward rule beyond possible ad hoc experimentation. They behave as if rewards are not a tool they wish to use: they keep the cost savings for themselves, even though the tech is efficient in a narrow sense (one-to-one). This pattern suggests low willingness to incur direct costs for social shaping, very low use of positive reciprocity as an instrument, and a largely myopic focus on immediate payoff rather than on strategic investment in others’ future behavior. Any strategic use of reward, if it exists, is weak and inconsistent; the safe inference is that they are a “reward-averse” type who largely ignores the reward option.

This player never spends on rewards in any round, despite the presence of clearly identifiable, often generous contributors and repeated opportunities to reinforce them. Their willingness to incur any personal cost to shape others’ behavior appears effectively zero in this environment. They do not use rewards as strategic tools, neither to build alliances with high contributors nor to encourage cooperation from low or unstable contributors. This holds even when others spend heavily on rewards and when the reward technology is transparent. The absence of any attempt to reward suggests that they either (a) do not believe rewards will substantially change others’ behavior, (b) do not value reciprocal or norm-enforcing relationships enough to pay for them, or (c) adopt a narrowly self-regarding strategy focused on immediate material payoff. They also do not use rewards as expressions of gratitude or as symbolic gestures, given that some others clearly behave in ways that could warrant such social recognition. They never spend any coins to reward others, despite having multiple opportunities and seeing others do so. This shows a clear reluctance to incur personal costs to reinforce cooperation, even when they themselves benefit from the cooperative environment. Their stance on reward is purely passive: they accept rewards directed toward them but do not reciprocate or reward others in turn. This non-use of reward is not paired with any justification or discussion, so the motive is uncertain. It is consistent with several possible interpretations: a payoff-maximizing reluctance to sacrifice earnings, an expectation that the group will sustain cooperation without extra incentives, or simple inattention or low engagement with the reward mechanism. Given that they clearly understand and verbally endorse the collective benefit of contribution, the most direct inference is that they conceptually support cooperation but prefer not to pay to enforce or encourage it. There is no evidence that they use or view rewarding as a strategic tool to shape others’ behavior; they treat it as a feature to ignore.

They never spend any coins on rewarding others, despite having multiple opportunities and despite many group members using rewards to support cooperators. Even when others reward them on at least a couple of occasions, they do not reciprocate. This shows no observable inclination toward gratitude signaling, reputation-building via reward, or strategic reinforcement of high contributors. Given the relatively favorable reward technology (slightly socially efficient), their strict non-use points to a strong preference for keeping private payoff over investing in group-level incentives. They do not use reward instrumentally (e.g., to encourage everyone to go “all in”), nor do they selectively reward those who cooperate or who reward others. Their type thus involves ignoring the reward channel altogether, behaving as if such second-order cooperation mechanisms are not worth the personal cost.

They never spend on rewards at any point. This is consistent across both cooperative and free-riding phases. Their abstention from rewarding persists even when they personally benefit from others’ rewards and when many others are clearly rewarding high contributors. This suggests either a strong reluctance to incur any cost for social enforcement, a belief that rewards are not worth the cost in this environment, or a preference to let others bear the cost of norm enforcement. There is no indication of strategic reward use to shape future behavior or to reciprocate those who reward them. Overall, they are a pure non-rewarder: they rely on the public good mechanism and on others’ enforcement, but do not actively reinforce cooperation themselves.

The player is willing to spend on rewards early, then stops rewarding entirely for the rest of the game. Initially they invest a nontrivial amount of their income to reward others, despite already having chosen to defect in that same early stage. This reveals two things: they understand and use the reward mechanism, and they are willing to incur personal cost to influence or acknowledge others’ behavior. Because rewarders are anonymous in the mechanism, their spending cannot credibly build personal reputation; this implies their early rewards are not obviously self-branding but rather aimed at supporting cooperation in general or expressing approval toward high contributors. After these initial uses, they completely cease rewarding, even in later rounds when many players contribute fully or when some appear to “come back” to cooperation. This discontinuation may indicate disappointment in the stability of cooperation, learning that rewarding has little discernible effect on group behavior, or a recalibration toward conserving payoffs. There is no later resurgence of reward aimed at late-round cooperators. Overall, they are a short-lived rewarder: they test or employ prosocial incentives early, then pragmatically abandon them when returns (in terms of group behavior) appear limited or unclear.

They never spend any coins to reward others, despite frequent opportunities and repeated observation that other players do reward cooperators. This holds both when they themselves have cooperated and when they have free-ridden; in all cases, they refrain from using the reward mechanism. This consistent non-use suggests that, for them, the small positive return from rewards is not worth the cost, or that they do not prioritize shaping others’ behavior via incentives. They show no sign of gratitude signaling: even when they benefit heavily from others’ contributions and from an overall cooperative environment, they do not reciprocate with rewards. Their refusal to reward persists even when most reward activity clearly targets high contributors and cooperative behavior. They remain a pure second-order free-rider with respect to the reward system: they benefit from the cooperation that other players try to sustain through rewards but contribute nothing to that institutional maintenance.

They never spend any coins on rewarding others, despite many opportunities and despite often being a high contributor among mixed cooperators and free-riders. However, they do receive rewards from others multiple times, which increases their payoffs, and they continue to cooperate after receiving such rewards. This suggests that external rewards are not necessary for them to sustain cooperation, and that they do not reciprocate reward behavior materially. Their reluctance to reward is not obviously tied to contribution patterns (they see clear examples where generous cooperators could be rewarded), so the most parsimonious interpretation is that they prioritize keeping their own payoff over incurring costs to shape others’ behavior, even when reward has a positive efficiency multiplier. That said, because they maintain high contributions without rewarding, their lack of reward use is best viewed as “reward-frugal cooperator” rather than a self-interested defector. There is no evidence they experiment with low-level rewards, target specific contributors, or strategically use rewards to counter free-riding. Their strategy appears to be: cooperate through contributions, but conserve resources by not spending on costly social incentives.

They never spend any coins on rewarding others, despite consistently high contributions themselves and repeated opportunities. They do, however, receive rewards at least once, and this incoming reinforcement does not change their own reward behavior; they remain a non-rewarder. This indicates that they are willing to cooperate without using costly positive incentives to shape others’ behavior. The absence of reward use is not clearly tied to any visible frustration; it appears as a stable strategic or dispositional choice. Possible motives (all low confidence): they may see the reward mechanism as inefficient, unnecessary, or not worth the personal cost; they may believe that direct contribution is their main cooperative duty and that others’ choices are their own responsibility; or they may simply be inattentive to or uninterested in the reward phase. There is no evidence of targeted or reciprocal rewarding (no “reward back those who reward me” behavior). Overall they are a pure cooperator, not a conditional or strategic rewarder.

They never spend resources to reward anyone, even when many others reward generously and when they themselves receive rewards. This suggests they either do not value the reward mechanism, judge the cost as not worth the marginal benefit, or deliberately choose to focus on contribution as their main cooperative act. At the same time, they do accept rewards: others repeatedly allocate positive reward units to them, implying their contributions are visible and appreciated. The player does not reciprocate this kindness with their own reward spending, so their generosity is asymmetric: they are willing to incur the cost of cooperation (by contributing) but not the additional, smaller cost of rewarding others. Strategically, this fits a “cooperate but don’t pay for meta‑incentives” type: they are willing to sacrifice for the public good, but not willing to further sacrifice to shape others’ incentives via rewards. They do not use rewards tactically (e.g., to encourage hesitant cooperators or to differentiate between high and low contributors). Their stance appears stable: no exploratory or one‑off experiments with reward spending are observed. Motivationally, this may indicate a belief that the primary cooperative decision is the contribution choice and that reward is either inefficient, unnecessary, or too small to matter. It could also reflect a more self‑regarding stance about second‑order costs: they will help on the main dimension but avoid additional, arguably more “optional,” sacrifices. Confidence about their underlying reasoning is low because they do not verbalize it; only the behavioral pattern is clear.

They never spend any coins on rewards, even though they consistently cooperate and often find themselves surrounded by other cooperators. This suggests either a strong payoff-maximizing stance with respect to the costly reward mechanism, a belief that rewards are ineffective or unnecessary, or a preference to express approval and disapproval via chat and contribution choices rather than through formal incentives. They do not appear to use rewards strategically to encourage or stabilize cooperation, despite having multiple opportunities where rewarding consistent full contributors could have reinforced norms. There is no evidence that they engage in selective reciprocity via rewards—neither gratitude to high contributors nor retaliation against free-riders through withholdings of positive incentives. Their stance toward the reward tool is effectively passive: they ignore it and rely entirely on their own contributions and occasional verbal norms to shape group behavior.

They are a light and selective rewarder. They only spend on rewards in a few rounds, and the amounts are modest relative to what some peers spend. When they do reward, it coincides with phases where many others also contribute fully, suggesting they use reward as a positive reinforcement tool when group cooperation is high or improving. They never reward every cooperator; rather, they appear to target a subset, but because identities of rewarders and detailed mappings are hidden in the interface, their precise target rule is unknown. They never reward defectors or consistent low contributors as far as can be inferred from outcome patterns, indicating that rewarding is contingent on perceived cooperation, not on equalizing payoffs. They also do not use reward as a compensatory mechanism for victims of free-riding; there is no clear pattern of “rescuing” those who lose out. Overall, they value the reward mechanism but are cautious with its cost, using it intermittently rather than as a systematic strategy.

They never spend any coins on rewarding others, even though they are themselves repeatedly rewarded by others when they contribute fully. This indicates an asymmetric stance: they cooperate via contribution, but they do not use the costly reward mechanism, neither to incentivize high contributors nor to thank those who reward them. They therefore behave as a “pure provider” rather than an “institutional enforcer” or “reciprocal rewarder.” Possible interpretations (with low confidence): they may see reward as an unnecessary cost, may be focused on maximizing their own payoff conditional on high contributions, or may be uncertain about whom to reward and thus choose inaction. There is no evidence that their willingness to contribute depends on receiving reward; they continue to fully contribute even in periods where they receive little or no reward. Overall, they are a non‑rewarding cooperator: generous in contributions, frugal in costly positive sanctioning.

They are a moderate, selectively prosocial rewarder. They spend on rewards in several rounds but not consistently; at times they give nothing, at other times they spend a modest amount, and very rarely they spend at a slightly higher but still limited level. They never engage in extreme or indiscriminate rewarding. Their reward behavior appears to target cooperators or those aligning with the cooperative norm rather than defectors, consistent with conditional cooperation and norm reinforcement. They do not seem to use rewards to “buy” personal payoff, as the reward technology only offers a small net efficiency gain; instead, they appear to use it as a signal of approval or encouragement toward cooperative partners. They accept rewards from others but do not publicly reference them, suggesting that gratitude is not a prominent overt motive, although they likely register who is supporting cooperation.

They never spend any resources on reward. Yet they repeatedly receive reward from others, sometimes substantial amounts, even while not contributing. Their refusal to reward has several implications: - They value their own payoff enough to avoid any costly prosocial action, even when reward is relatively efficient. - They do not use reward strategically to try to shape others’ future contributions, despite being in a group where many others do reward. - They show no “gratitude signaling” via reciprocal reward toward those who have rewarded them; any such reciprocity is absent. This positions them as a pure beneficiary of others’ generosity, not as a participant in the reward norm. Whether they consciously exploit the fact that rewarders cannot be identified is unclear, but behaviorally they act as if anonymity removes any reputational pressure to reward in return.

They never spend any coins on reward throughout the game, despite the mechanism being available and despite receiving rewards themselves at several points. This suggests a strong reluctance to incur personal cost for positive reciprocity or indirect enforcement through rewards. Their strategy appears to be purely contribution-based and communication-based rather than relying on the reward mechanism. The absence of reward use is consistent: they do not selectively reward high contributors, do not attempt to “buy” cooperation, and do not use rewards as thanks or as a signal. This could indicate that they (a) discount the effectiveness of rewards as a coordination tool, (b) strongly prefer to keep their payoff rather than pay for social incentives, or (c) perceive explanation and norm-talk as their primary enforcement instrument. Their refusal to reward persists even when others reward them, suggesting that responding in kind is not part of their strategy. They behave as a “non-rewarding cooperator”: willing to contribute heavily to the public good, but not willing to spend additional resources to differentially incentivize others.

The player never rewards anyone, despite many opportunities and despite facing others who contribute consistently and sometimes reward or punish in ways that could be interpreted as attempts at coordination. They do not use rewards to encourage high contributors or to reciprocate when they themselves are rewarded. This suggests that they treat the reward mechanism as strictly dominated from their perspective: they are unwilling to sacrifice even small amounts of payoff to positively reinforce others. There is no evidence of selective rewarding (e.g., supporting cooperators while withholding from defectors); instead, there is complete abstention. This supports an interpretation of the player as highly self-regarding and uninterested in building cooperative “alliances” via positive incentives, even though rewarders are identifiable and could, in principle, become useful partners.

This player shows that they can be extremely generous with rewards, but this generosity is sporadic and short-lived. When they choose to reward, they are willing to spend very large amounts to transfer substantial payoffs to others, even at great personal cost, including driving their own round payoff negative. At least once, they reward multiple peers simultaneously and at high intensity, indicating a willingness to create large positive differentials in others’ favor. Their reward behavior early on is fairly aligned with peer contributions: they reward others who contribute non-zero amounts, including those who are contributing more than them and those who are around or above the group average. This looks like an attempt to reinforce cooperation and signal approval for contributing. However, the reward technology is symmetric (1:1), so these choices are not payoff-maximizing and serve more as social signaling than incentive-efficient behavior. Over the game, reward usage fades almost entirely. After some early episodes of intense rewarding, they largely stop rewarding despite many opportunities where others contribute cooperatively. This suggests either learning (deciding that rewards are not effective or satisfying) or a shift in affect (moving from hopeful encouragement to frustration and punitive focus). They do not systematically use rewards to build alliances or to maintain mutual support once initial attempts are made.

This player is willing to reward, but in a restrained, targeted, and sometimes strategically ambiguous way. They reward when others contribute at high or improving levels or when they appear aligned with this player’s emerging coalition or norm preferences. They occasionally reward in periods of low overall cooperation, which may function as an attempt to encourage desirable partners rather than to enforce a strict contribution threshold. They never use reward as broadly or as intensely as some peers use punishment; their rewards are small, selective, and often paired with punishment of others in the same phase, consistent with a “carrot for allies, stick for violators” pattern. Given that rewards are payoff-neutral at the dyadic level in this mechanism, their use of reward appears more about social signaling (gratitude, alliance-building, or recognition of “good types”) than about pure payoff engineering. They do not systematically reward all high contributors, only certain ones, which suggests they incorporate history and identity (who it is) rather than acting purely on current contributions.

This player uses rewards, but more sparingly than punishment. When they do reward, it is targeted and consistent with reinforcing cooperative or improved behavior. They choose high or clearly cooperative contributors as recipients when they are rewarding, and they also occasionally reward a player who appears to have shifted toward more helpful behavior after turmoil. They never use rewards indiscriminately. They do not systematically reward every high contributor each period, but instead pick spots where a reward can plausibly signal approval or encouragement. This makes their reward usage look strategic rather than purely altruistic. Given the technology (reward is not more efficient than direct giving), their willingness to pay for rewards indicates some preference for expressing approval and possibly for influencing future cooperation, even at a personal cost. However, the overall lower frequency of rewards compared to punishments suggests they lean more toward negative than positive sanctions as a means of shaping the group.

They never spend on rewards either, even in situations where the group is highly cooperative and some members expend substantial resources to reward or support others. This suggests that they do not see positive reinforcement as worth its cost, at least at the given reward technology. There is no sign of gratitude signaling via rewards, nor of attempts to strategically strengthen cooperation by rewarding high contributors. Their cooperative episodes—where they contribute a lot—are not accompanied by rewarding others, indicating that their cooperation is expressed only through contributions, not through secondary prosocial tools. In combination with zero punishment, this makes them a pure “non-intervenor” in the sanction/reward layer: they exploit or join cooperation purely through their own contribution choices, not by trying to reshape the incentive environment for others.

The player never uses the reward option, even when others make very high or improving contributions and even when some peers spend substantial amounts rewarding. They receive rewards in several rounds but do not reciprocate, suggesting they are not motivated by gratitude signaling or by building reciprocal reward relationships. Given that the reward technology is inefficient for the giver relative to punishment, their behavior is consistent with a cost-effectiveness focus in sanctioning: they are willing to spend on punishment (which is powerful per unit) to deter or correct free-riding, but they decline to spend on the weaker, more expensive positive reinforcement mechanism. There is no evidence that they deploy reward strategically to stabilize high cooperation; their cooperation is offered regardless, and they appear to rely solely on punishment plus their own example rather than on formal rewarding.

They use reward selectively but in a highly nonstandard way. In most rounds they refrain from rewarding despite many others contributing at or near the maximum and despite reward being available from the start. When they do reward, they spend very substantial amounts on one specific low-contributing player who has been heavily punished by others. These rewards are not aligned with that player’s contribution level at the time; instead, they often arrive after that player’s contributions remain poor or only marginally improved. This pattern suggests they use reward primarily as a counterweight to what they might perceive as excessively harsh punishment directed at a single group member, rather than as a standard reinforcement for prosocial behavior. The focal player effectively transfers a large portion of their own payoff to the ostracized player, absorbing a big personal cost. This indicates strong concern for equity or compassion for a scapegoated member, even at the expense of efficiency and their own payoff. They also provide a small amount of reward once to a more cooperative peer, but this is minor compared to the repeated, substantial rewards directed to the previously low-contributing, heavily punished player. Overall, their reward behavior looks more like “protective support” for the marginalized than like systematic incentive management.

They never reward anyone, despite many others using the reward mechanism and despite frequent high contributions by peers. This suggests either that they view the reward technology as too inefficient to justify use, that they believe high contribution is merely the expected norm and not something to be positively reinforced, or that they reserve all sanctioning effort for punishment rather than positive incentives. They also do not reciprocate rewards they receive; receiving small rewards does not trigger any visible change toward rewarding others. Overall, they rely exclusively on negative incentives to enforce cooperation and appear unwilling to spend resources on positive reinforcement.

They are willing to use reward, but in a sparse and idiosyncratic way. They sometimes reward players who contribute very little, as well as players who contribute very highly. This implies that reward is not a straightforward “payback for high contribution” tool. Their rewarding of low contributors suggests a possible inclination toward inclusion or encouragement, or an attempt to cultivate allies among those at risk of being punished by others. It might also reflect sympathy for heavily punished players or a desire to moderate the group’s harshness toward deviants. However, these motives are speculative; all that can be stated confidently is that reward is not tightly aligned with contribution levels. They also occasionally give small rewards to high contributors, which could be interpreted as gratitude signaling or reinforcement of cooperative behavior. Still, relative to punishment, reward plays a minor role in their strategy: they spend far more, and more often, on punishment than on reward. They thus use reward selectively and symbolically, while punishment is their primary social tool.

They never use rewards either, despite having many opportunities to positively reinforce high contributors. Given that reward is very inefficient, this could reflect either an implicit cost-benefit awareness (reluctance to waste payoff on weak incentives) or a more general preference not to intervene in others’ outcomes at all. Combined with their refusal to punish, their pattern is consistent with a “non-interfering altruist”: they contribute fully to the public good but do not attempt to steer or evaluate others via secondary mechanisms. They do not appear to use rewards as gratitude signals or as a strategy to consolidate a cooperative coalition.

They never use the reward mechanism, despite many opportunities and despite witnessing others occasionally use it. This holds even when some peers contribute the maximum consistently and might be “deserving” from a reciprocity standpoint. Given that reward is structurally inefficient in this game (it costs more to give than the recipient gains), their behavior suggests they evaluate reward as not worth the cost, even for norm reinforcement. Their enforcement style is thus one-sided: they rely entirely on costly punishment to shape behavior, and do not invest in positive reinforcement or gratitude signaling. Any satisfaction they derive from cooperation enforcement seems to come from sanctioning defectors, not from actively subsidizing cooperators.

The player never uses the reward mechanism. This holds even when others contribute at very high levels or correct earlier low contributions. They appear unwilling to pay a personal cost to provide small material rewards, possibly because the reward technology is highly inefficient, because they prioritize punishment as the primary tool of norm enforcement, or because they believe that high contributions are baseline obligations that do not merit extra compensation. There is no evidence that they ever attempt to strategically reinforce good behavior via rewards or to express gratitude monetarily.

The player uses reward selectively, less frequently than punishment, and always to recognize above-average or consistently high contributors. When they do reward, they spread small bonuses across multiple strong contributors rather than concentrating on a single individual, indicating a desire to broadly reinforce cooperative behavior. They never reward obvious free riders or people contributing far below the group, and they do not appear to reward in response to others’ punishment/reward spending (e.g., they do not “thank” punishers or compensate victims). Reward seems to function as a positive counterpart to their punishment: it is aimed at clear cooperators, especially when contributions are high and aligned with their full-cooperation norm. They accept the poor efficiency of the reward technology; they still spend significantly to give relatively small payoffs to others, suggesting an intrinsic preference for acknowledging good behavior rather than strictly maximizing their own earnings. Over the game, they intermittently switch between rewarding and not rewarding as the group’s cooperation and punishment environment evolves, but they never abandon reward entirely. Their reward behavior appears strategic in a social sense (reinforcing norms), not in a narrow monetary sense.

They never spend on rewards either, despite repeated instances where others make high or even perfect contributions and where some players lavish large positive transfers on cooperators. This reveals that they are not inclined to use costly prosocial incentives as a coordination or gratitude device. They appear content to let the public good mechanism itself do the rewarding, and they separate their cooperative contribution decisions from the more targeted, interpersonal incentive stage. Combined with their lack of punishment, this suggests a “non-interventionist” stance in the secondary mechanism: they participate in the core public good but deliberately stay out of interpersonal reallocations, possibly viewing them as inefficient, noisy, or strategically opaque.

They never spend on rewards, even though they repeatedly receive rewards from others. Their zero-reward pattern is stable across high- and low-cooperation environments and across times when many others reward versus when almost no one does. This suggests that they do not use the reward tool either as reciprocity (thanking those who reward them or who cooperate) or as strategic reinforcement of prosocial behavior. It is unknown whether this is due to (a) strict payoff maximization regarding the reward mechanism, (b) a principle against manipulating others’ incentives, or (c) simple disinterest or confusion about the mechanism. However, the consistency across many opportunities makes confusion less likely and instead points to a simple rule: contribute fully but do not pay to reward. They implicitly separate their cooperative norm (contributing) from costly social sanctioning or thanking (rewarding), and keep the latter at zero.

They never spend any coins on reward despite having many opportunities and seeing others reward generously. This suggests a strong reluctance to incur personal cost for positive reinforcement, even toward consistent cooperators and even late in the game when they are wealthy. Their stance appears closer to “cooperate myself but let others handle rewarding,” rather than fully strategic rewarding. They nonetheless notice and appreciate when they receive rewards, but do not reciprocate materially. The absence of any reward behavior, even when cooperation is nearly universal, indicates that they do not use rewards as a tool to shape behavior or to express gratitude; their preferred way of encouraging cooperation is through communication and consistent own contributions, not through direct incentive payments.

They are an extremely active rewarder, repeatedly spending substantial portions of their payoff to give small, dispersed rewards to many or all other active players. They tend to give rewards even in rounds where they themselves contribute nothing, which shows that their rewarding is not contingent on their own cooperative behavior. Instead, they appear to use rewards as a tool to stabilize or encourage others’ cooperation: rewards are typically directed widely across the group, often favoring participants who contribute, and rarely if ever self-serving in a narrow sense. This pattern fits a “strategic rewarder–free-rider” hybrid: they invest in others’ satisfaction and cooperative inclination while continuing to maintain a personal contribution schedule that yields them high net payoffs. They do not use reward in a selective, reputation-building manner (e.g., favoring allies or punishing defectors indirectly); instead, their pattern looks like broad positive reinforcement of the group’s participation, even when they personally deviate. This suggests they accept significant personal cost to sustain a cooperative climate from which they can sometimes free-ride. Their willingness to spend repeatedly on rewards, despite never receiving corresponding rewards of similar magnitude, indicates they are not simply reciprocating; they appear comfortable acting as a unilateral reward provider to shape group behavior.

The player never spends any coins on rewarding others, despite having substantial accumulated earnings and facing multiple opportunities. They do accept and appreciate rewards when others give them, but they do not reciprocate materially. Their chat does not reference any intent to reward; instead, when discussing sanctions, they focus on punitive or exclusionary mechanisms that are not available in this game. This pattern suggests that they view reward as unnecessary or inefficient, even toward high contributors, and would prefer a direct punishment or exclusion mechanism for defectors. Their strategy appears to be: contribute fully, use communication to enforce norms, but preserve all of their own payoff rather than paying to positively reinforce others. Any internal gratitude they may feel is not expressed via the reward mechanism. Their lack of rewarding persists even when others regularly reward them and when everyone is contributing fully, indicating this is a stable preference and not a reaction to specific individuals.

The player never spends any coins on rewarding others, despite multiple opportunities and clear visibility that some others frequently give out rewards. Their own reward balance steadily increases due to others rewarding them, but they do not reciprocate in kind. This indicates either a deliberate strategy to avoid the cost of rewarding or a low valuation of the reward mechanism relative to direct contribution. They do not appear to use rewards to signal gratitude, encourage cooperation, or build alliances, even when cooperation is high and clearly attributable to specific players. Their earlier expressed desire to “target” low contributors is not implemented via the reward system; since they cannot punish with negative transfers, and reward is the only lever, they simply abstain. Overall, they act as a pure contributor who relies on contribution norms rather than supplementary incentive tools. Their reward behavior suggests a separation in their mind between “doing your part” (contributing fully) and actively managing others’ behavior through costly social incentives.

They never spend any of their own coins on reward, despite having many opportunities and being in a group where at least one player frequently uses rewards. They are a pure non-rewarder in this environment. At the same time, they frequently receive small rewards from others, especially when they contribute. There is no evidence they reciprocate reward to those who reward them, or that they use reward to signal approval or gratitude. This suggests a type that relies on others’ use of positive incentives rather than investing in such mechanisms themselves. It is unclear whether this is due to indifference to social signaling, a desire to maximize personal payoff by avoiding extra costs, or uncertainty about the value of rewarding. What is clear is that their prosociality, when it appears, is expressed via direct contribution to the public good, not via secondary mechanisms like reward. They do not appear to treat reward decisions as a tool for strategic reinforcement or coalition-building.

The player never spends any coins on rewards. Across all opportunities, their reward cost is always zero. This is notable because they consistently contribute fully, and other cooperators frequently reward contributors in some periods, yet this player does not reciprocate via the reward mechanism. They accept rewards from others but do not pay to reinforce desired behavior, suggesting they either (a) place very high weight on preserving their own payoff relative to the benefit of rewarding, (b) do not view rewarding as instrumental or necessary given already-high cooperation levels, or (c) do not pay much attention to, or do not value, the reward mechanism at all. There is no evidence that they use rewards strategically (e.g., to encourage borderline cooperators or to retaliate against free riders by withholding rewards). Instead, they behave as a “pure contributor but non-rewarder”: they are willing to provide the public good itself, but unwilling to invest further in costly social incentives.

They almost never spend on rewards. Across the game they only once pay a small, isolated amount to reward someone, and there is no clear pattern of them using rewards strategically or reciprocally. They instead function primarily as a passive recipient of rewards from highly prosocial others. Their chat suggests they conceptually like the idea of punishment and negative sanctions far more than positive reinforcement: early on they explicitly wish for a mechanism to take coins from others rather than to give them more, and later they question the logic of a player who heavily rewards while not contributing, treating that pattern as suspicious or “non‑human.” That reaction implies they view costly rewarding without contributing as atypical and perhaps as a sign of an artificial or experimental agent. Their own minimal rewarding behavior is consistent with a payoff‑concerned cooperator who is willing to invest in the public good but reluctant to incur extra individual costs for social signaling beyond contribution itself. They appear to treat full contribution as the main and sufficient cooperative signal, rather than using the reward stage as an additional lever.

They never spend on rewards despite having repeated opportunities and often being part of a highly cooperative majority. At the same time, they repeatedly receive small rewards from others, indicating that their cooperative behavior is recognized and valued, but they do not reciprocate in kind. The consistent absence of reward spending, even when everyone else cooperates and a pro-social reward norm emerges, suggests one of the following (true motive is unknown): (a) a strictly payoff-maximizing attitude toward the reward mechanism (reward is exactly cost-1 for benefit-1, so it is individually neutral and thus not worth the effort), (b) a belief that rewards are unnecessary when cooperation is already high, or (c) inattention or low salience of the reward step. Given their steady engagement with contributions and occasional chat, inattention to the mechanism seems less likely but cannot be ruled out. They do not use rewards strategically to shape others’ behavior, to express gratitude, or to coordinate against free-riders. They appear content to let others bear the cost of reinforcing cooperation and simply benefit from the public good and incoming rewards. This pattern is consistent with a “cooperative non-rewarder”: they invest heavily in the public account but treat the reward stage as irrelevant or inefficient, even when social norms around rewarding emerge.

They almost always refrain from giving rewards, even when they are themselves rewarded and even when others use the mechanism actively. When they do spend on reward, the spending is rare and small relative to others’ extensive rewarding. Because the mechanism is symmetric and visible, their general non‑use indicates that they see rewarding as personally costly with limited upside. They do not appear motivated by strong gratitude or reciprocal rewarding: receiving rewards does not reliably trigger them to reward back, and they never state an intention to “reward cooperators” or “thank” anyone. On a couple of occasions they do spend on rewards. The pattern around those spends is ambiguous: it could reflect a brief attempt to conform to an emerging norm of mutual rewarding, or a one‑off experiment to see if rewarding changes how others treat them. There is not enough evidence to attribute a stable reciprocal strategy; confidence about their underlying reward rule is low. They do not try to weaponize rewards strategically (e.g., concentrating rewards on specific borderline contributors or on themselves in ways that would be obviously self‑signaling), and they never discuss the reward mechanism in chat. Overall, they act as a reluctant, low‑engagement rewarder: they understand the option, mostly decline to use it, and occasionally dabble without making it central to their strategy.

The player is willing to spend their own payoff to reward others, despite never contributing themselves. They direct rewards only to high contributors and never to fellow non-contributors, indicating that reward is used as a positive reinforcement for cooperative behavior rather than as a general prosocial gesture or an attempt to build alliances with similar payoff-maximizing players. They sometimes allocate substantial total cost to reward, and sometimes only a small cost; the specific pattern suggests a selective rather than automatic rewarding rule. They do not appear to use reward to “pay back” someone who rewarded them, since they never receive reward. Instead, they appear to reward based on observed contribution behavior alone, not on reciprocal exchange. Overall, their reward use is consistent with a “moral free-rider” pattern: they personally do not contribute to the public good but are willing to privately subsidize or encourage those who do.

They are willing to spend on rewards and do so selectively rather than uniformly. They never reward defectors; rewards are only directed to players who contribute to the public fund. They use rewards for two main purposes: to recognize consistent cooperators and to reinforce or encourage cooperative behavior in others. Their willingness to pay for rewards persists even when they themselves receive little or no reward in return, indicating prosocial rather than purely strategic reciprocity motives. At the same time, their reward pattern is not mechanically equal across all contributors: some cooperators receive rewards in certain periods and not others, suggesting they attend to recent behavior (e.g., a drop in contributions) or perceived reliability when deciding whom to reward. They are comfortable sacrificing some payoff to shape the group environment in a positive direction, but they do not escalate reward spending excessively; their total reward outlays are moderate relative to what would be possible, consistent with a boundedly “prosocial but still somewhat payoff-aware” type.

They never spend anything on rewards, even when others are clearly using rewards to encourage cooperation. They also abstain from rewarding those who reward them. This pattern suggests that they do not use the reward mechanism either as a strategic tool to shape others’ behavior or as an expression of gratitude or norm enforcement. Their guiding incentive appears to be strict payoff maximization within each round, with no willingness to invest current resources in potential longer-term group benefits or reputational gains. Whether they conceptually understand the potential strategic value of rewarding is unknown.

They never spend on rewards despite clear opportunities and visible cooperative partners. This strongly suggests that they treat the reward mechanism as not worth the cost, at least under the given parameters, or that they prefer to keep their decisions purely on contribution rather than on secondary incentives. They do not use rewards to reinforce high contributors, to thank rewarders, or to steer group norms. There is no evidence that they strategically invest in reward to induce future cooperation or to cultivate allies. Whether this is due to a purely payoff-maximizing calculus, skepticism about influencing others, or simple indifference to the mechanism is unknown. What is clear is a consistent pattern of non-use: they separate their own contribution decisions from any form of costly social reinforcement.

They never spend any coins on rewards, even when clear high contributors exist and even when they themselves are directly rewarded by others. This indicates a strong preference for keeping payoff rather than engaging in costly positive sanctioning, despite the neutral cost–benefit ratio of the reward mechanism in this setup. Their stance suggests that, for them, cooperation via contribution is sufficient, and additional reinforcement via reward is unnecessary or not worth the complexity or risk. There is no observable pattern of selective rewarding; absence of use across different contribution environments points to a generally non-rewarding, non-enforcing type rather than a nuanced, contingent reward strategy. They do not appear to use rewards to build alliances, express gratitude, or shape others’ behavior.

They show a limited but nonzero willingness to reward others. When they do reward, they target clear high contributors rather than non-contributors, which indicates a straightforward “reward cooperation” heuristic rather than strategic manipulation. Their reward use is sparse and not persistent; they reward in some cooperative phases but not in others, even when high contributors are present. This pattern suggests that rewards are not central to their strategy and may be used more as a one-off recognition or experiment than as a consistent tool to shape group behavior. There is no evidence that they weigh the cost-benefit of rewarding very carefully; the fact that the cost and benefit of reward are equal and they use it only occasionally suggests low salience of efficiency considerations rather than strong economic optimization.

They never spend any coins on rewarding others, even when there are clear high contributors and visible generous rewarders whose behavior could be reinforced at no net gain or loss (reward here is cost-neutral per unit but still involves a choice). This indicates that they follow a “non-rewarding cooperator” profile: they provide the public good but do not invest in second-order cooperation. They do not use reward either as gratitude signaling, as a reciprocity tool, or as a device to stabilize cooperation. Their non-use of reward persists even after others show willingness to reward them and other cooperators, suggesting that the absence of rewarding is not due to lack of information. Possible motives (all low confidence): they may be narrowly payoff-focused beyond their fixed contribution rule (avoiding extra complexity), indifferent to the reward institution, or simply defaulting to inaction on the reward screen. There is no evidence that they condition reward on others’ contributions or on being rewarded; they simply abstain.

They occasionally use rewards, but not consistently, and at notable personal cost. When they do reward, they expend a nontrivial portion of their payoff, indicating a willingness to sacrifice to reinforce behavior they view positively. However, they do not reliably reward all high contributors, nor do they maintain a stable pattern of rewarding over time. This suggests either exploratory or uncertain use of the mechanism rather than a clear long-term strategy. Their reward decisions appear loosely aligned with cooperation (they do not reward clear free-riders), so their trigger seems to be “contributed to the public good” rather than reciprocity for personal gain. They do not exploit the fact that reward is cost-neutral in aggregate (cost equals benefit), which implies they either misunderstand or ignore the strategic equivalence and use it more as a normative “pat on the back” tool than an efficiency-maximizing lever.

They never spend on rewards at any point, despite having multiple opportunities and despite being embedded in a group where several others actively use the reward option. This is not a borderline case: their reward expenditure is consistently zero. Given that contributing is privately costly and they already choose low-to-moderate contributions, it is consistent that they also avoid the added cost of rewarding. They do not seem to use rewards instrumentally to sustain high contributions by others, even though others around them are strongly rewarding high contributors. This suggests either they do not believe their individual reward choices matter for group behavior, or they place very high weight on preserving their own payoff relative to any social or strategic benefits of rewarding. Their lack of rewarding does not change when they personally receive reward from others; they remain non-rewarding even after being targeted. Thus they do not appear motivated by reciprocal gratitude in this mechanism, nor by an urge to “participate” in the emergent reward norm. They also do not selectively reward either consistent high contributors or exceptional changes in behavior. Overall, they behave as a pure non-rewarder, driven mainly by the immediate cost of rewarding rather than by long-term cooperative reinforcement.

This player uses the reward mechanism sparingly and in a highly conservative, self-focused way. Across the game, they either spend nothing or a very small amount on rewards relative to what is possible. They occasionally incur the minimum possible cost to give reward, and they do so only in situations where they themselves are also receiving some reward from others in that same period. There is no evidence they ever make large, costly reward investments. Given that buying one unit of reward is exactly cost-neutral (they pay the same amount as the recipient gains), their reluctance to spend suggests either (a) a preference to conserve their own payoff rather than actively shaping others’ behavior, or (b) a belief that reward will not meaningfully change others’ contributions. They do not adopt the “altruistic rewarding” pattern seen in some other players who spend heavily on others regardless of self-gain. Their small, occasional use of reward when they themselves are being rewarded is consistent with weak reciprocity or gratitude signaling: they may be mirroring the cooperative climate or reciprocating prosocial signals in a limited way. However, because they never escalate to strong, targeted rewarding of top contributors, they do not appear to use rewards strategically to reinforce specific high contributors or to build coalitions. Instead, their type is “light, reactive rewarder”: they mostly keep their coins, with only minimal, situational use of the reward tool.

This player almost never uses the reward option. For most of the game they spend nothing on rewarding anyone, even when there are consistently very high contributors whose behavior other players visibly reward. They only use the reward mechanism once, and at a moderate level, which shows that they understand the tool and are capable of using it but perceive little reason to do so. Their rare use of reward coincides with a situation where many others are actively rewarding. In that instance, they both give and receive some reward. This suggests they may be responding to a momentary surge of pro-social signaling: they are willing to participate in a collective “recognition” phase when that norm is salient, but they do not initiate or maintain such a norm on their own. The fact that they do not continue rewarding afterwards, even when others still reward heavily, implies that they regard reward as optional, not integral to sustaining cooperation. They do not selectively target or withhold reward in any systematic way across high and low contributors, because they almost never reward at all. Thus there is insufficient evidence to say they strategically reinforce specific partners or punish low contributors indirectly via omission. The pattern is more consistent with a “cooperate, but don’t pay extra to manage others” stance. They seem to rely on contributions alone as their main social signal, and treat reward as a costly luxury rather than a necessary coordination tool. Gratitude signaling from their side is minimal: even after receiving nontrivial reward in later rounds, they do not reciprocate with outbound reward. This indicates they are not strongly reciprocity-driven at the reward stage; they accept benefits without feeling compelled to incur symmetric costs in response. Their strategy with reward is thus best characterized as passive and sparing.

This player is unusually willing to spend heavily on rewards and treats the reward stage as their primary lever for influencing the group. They repeatedly invest substantial amounts of their private payoff in rewarding others, even to the point of severely reducing their own earnings in some rounds. This is not consistent with pure payoff maximization and indicates a real preference for reinforcing behaviors they value or for shaping the social environment, even at significant personal cost. Their reward behavior appears selective and strategic rather than random. They do not reward in every round; instead, they concentrate reward spending in periods where cooperation is high and where others are also actively rewarding. This pattern suggests they are attempting to reinforce perceived cooperators and maintain a high-contribution equilibrium. When they later switch to free-riding, they simultaneously stop rewarding, indicating that in their mind, rewarding and contributing are part of the same “cooperative effort package”: once they disengage from one, they disengage from both. They sometimes receive rewards themselves, but their own reward behavior does not obviously track direct reciprocity; there is no clear evidence that they only or primarily reward those who rewarded them. This leans toward a more group-focused or norm-maintenance motive rather than dyadic tit-for-tat. However, because identities of rewarders are hidden and the mapping from their choices to realized receipts is opaque, their exact targeting rule is unknown. Confidence is higher that they reward high contributors and central cooperators than that they reward based on personal gain. The combination of high reward expenditure and later cessation suggests a player who initially uses reward as a way to “buy” or support a cooperative climate, then later views continued rewarding as wasted effort once they perceive the system as self-sustaining or as no longer worth the extra cost.

They almost never spend on rewards and, when they do, they use it sparingly. This reveals a strong reluctance to pay personal costs to influence or thank others. Reward is not a central tool in their strategy. Their rare use of reward coincides with them receiving substantial rewards from others. This suggests their own rewarding is more likely a reactive gesture—possibly signaling gratitude or reciprocity to someone perceived as generous—rather than a standing commitment to reinforcing high contributors in general. However, because identities of rewarders are hidden, any targeted reciprocity would be at best probabilistic; the low frequency and volume of their rewarding indicate that even probabilistic reciprocity is weak. They do not appear to use reward instrumentally to steer group behavior. High contributors and heavy rewarders exist in the group, yet this player does not systematically support them. They retain the option to free-ride not only on others’ contributions but also on others’ costly rewarding efforts. In summary, they treat the reward mechanism as something to mostly ignore, occasionally engaging with it in a limited way when they personally benefit, but without a consistent norm of rewarding cooperators or rewarders.

This player is very sparing with rewards and does not use them as a primary strategic tool. Most of the time they refrain from rewarding altogether, even when others clearly contribute at or near the maximum and even when others are actively rewarding high contributors. When they do spend on rewards, it is in small, isolated instances rather than as part of a sustained reinforcement pattern. The evidence does not allow clear identification of their exact targets, but the minimal and sporadic use of the mechanism suggests one of two main possibilities: either they place low value on reward as an instrument of cooperation management, or they are uncertain about how to use it effectively and thus default to non‑use. They do not appear to systematically “buy” better group behavior by supporting top cooperators, nor do they seem to use rewards to reciprocate being rewarded themselves. Their choices are not consistent with a strong “peer reward manager” or “strategic investor in reputation” type; instead, they resemble a player who relies on contribution norms alone and treats reward as optional, marginal, or secondary. There is no evidence of spite via the reward channel (e.g., deliberate non‑reward of low contributors as a meaningful “signal”), only a generally low engagement with the reward technology.

The player never spends anything on rewards, despite being repeatedly eligible to do so. They receive rewards multiple times but never reciprocate in kind, even toward clearly generous and/or highly rewarding peers. This indicates that they do not use reward as an active tool—neither to reinforce cooperation nor to build reciprocal ties. Their behavior is consistent with viewing reward as individually neutral at best and not worth the attention cost, since it is one-to-one and does not amplify payoffs. They seem uninterested in shaping others’ behavior via costly signaling; instead, they rely purely on their contribution choices to express their cooperative stance. Gratitude, if present, does not translate into costly rewarding. They are therefore a “non-rewarding high contributor”: strongly cooperative in contribution, but strategically or emotionally disengaged from the reward mechanism itself.

This player is extremely restrained in using rewards. They sometimes spend a small, non-escalating amount on rewards and often spend nothing, even when many other players reward extensively. Their reward use does not track obvious shifts in others’ contribution levels or payoff imbalances in a systematic way, and there is no clear evidence that they use rewards either as strong positive reinforcement or as a reciprocal “thank you” for receiving rewards themselves; they sometimes reward when they have not just been generously rewarded, and sometimes abstain even after receiving substantial rewards. This suggests that they do not treat the reward mechanism as a core strategic tool. Instead, they appear to view rewards as optional, low-frequency signals rather than as a continuous management of group behavior. They do not appear to be motivated by competitive status via paying out large visible rewards, nor by “keeping up” with more generous rewarders. Overall, they are a high contributor but a minimal, somewhat indifferent rewarder, with low inclination to pay private costs to differentially shape others’ incentives.

The player is a heavy, targeted user of the reward mechanism, but not a consistent one: they either spend nothing or very large amounts, creating a pattern of “bursts” of generous rewarding. They accept substantial personal payoff losses to provide rewards, indicating that they value influencing others’ behavior or expressing approval more than maximizing their own earnings. Their rewards are not random: they concentrate on others who contribute highly to the public good, and they never reward clear low contributors. They also sometimes reward multiple high contributors in the same stage, which suggests an attempt to broadly reinforce cooperation rather than form an exclusive alliance. They do not systematically reciprocate every reward they receive, nor do they clearly “buy” future rewards by paying others. Their willingness to spend far more on rewarding than they receive back implies that they are not primarily using reward as a strict tit-for-tat exchange. Instead, their use of rewards fits a prosocial or norm-enforcing type: they selectively encourage behavior they approve of (high contributions) and ignore or withhold support from low contributors, even when low contributors have high payoffs. Their pattern shows limited concern for the direct personal return on each reward unit; they act more like a “moral investor” in cooperation than like a narrow strategist.

This player uses the reward mechanism heavily at first, then abandons it entirely in later stages. Early on, they spend substantial amounts on rewarding others even though each unit of reward is privately costly and neutral in terms of direct net transfer. This strong initial use suggests they treat reward as a tool for shaping norms, encouraging high contributions, or expressing approval, rather than as a purely self-interested payoff tool. They also reward in at least one round where they themselves contribute relatively little, implying they are willing to pay to support or reinforce others’ cooperative behavior even while not fully matching it in their own contribution. Over time, however, their willingness to reward drops to zero and remains there. This shift coincides with a stable environment of relatively high contributions and active rewarding by others. The pattern suggests they come to view their own rewards as no longer necessary or not payoff-effective once a cooperative and reward-rich environment is established. They do not use reward to reciprocate those who reward them in any consistent, sustained way; any reciprocity that may occur is short-lived and not systematically maintained. This points to a primarily strategic, environment-shaping use of reward early on, followed by a free-riding stance on the reward mechanism itself once others continue to supply it. They do not use reward as a form of indirect punishment (e.g., rewarding low contributors less or not at all while continuing to support high contributors in a nuanced gradient). Instead, their pattern is coarse: an initial period of substantial positive reinforcement, followed by complete withdrawal. Their gratitude signaling is limited and short-lived; they do not adopt a “reward those who rewarded me” identity as a stable strategy. Overall, they treat reward as a front-loaded investment in cooperation rather than an ongoing, relational signal.

The player is an unusually heavy user of the reward mechanism and routinely spends substantial amounts to reward others. They clearly do not treat the reward channel as a rare or marginal tool: they often invest a large share of their payoff into rewarding. Given that reward is exactly cost-neutral and anonymous, this suggests they see it as a norm-enforcing or gratitude-signaling device rather than a way to change material payoffs. The main inferred trigger for rewarding is high or above-average contribution to the public good; there is no observable correlation between receiving rewards and later self-interested restraint in giving rewards, so reciprocity directed at personal benefactors is at best weakly supported and remains low confidence. They show a willingness to incur costs to reinforce cooperation even when they themselves are not directly rewarded in that period, indicating they prioritize supporting cooperative partners over preserving their own immediate payoff. They do not use rewards selectively to “buy” future help from specific targets in any clear, targeted way; instead, their pattern better fits diffuse, pro-social reinforcement of cooperators. They never reward low contributors or free-riders as far as can be inferred, indicating they try to keep the signal “clean”: rewards are reserved for those behaving cooperatively. Strategic sophistication is present but likely bounded: they understand that rewarding can encourage cooperation, but they do not seem to optimize the cost–benefit ratio of their reward spending and are willing to overinvest from a narrow payoff-maximizing perspective.

This player is willing to spend nontrivial amounts on rewards, but not consistently across all rounds. Their reward behavior is selective and appears tied to perceived contribution levels and perhaps to patterns of others’ rewarding, though precise targets cannot be inferred because identities of rewarders are hidden. They sometimes spend a meaningful portion of their payoff on rewards, and other times spend nothing even in similar contribution environments. This on–off pattern suggests they use rewards as an occasional tool rather than a constant strategy. The direction of spending aligns more with supporting cooperation than with self-enrichment: the reward technology is neutral in payoff terms, so any spending on rewards indicates a social or strategic motive rather than direct monetary gain. Their use of reward is compatible with several possible triggers: - reinforcing high or very high contributors; - signaling approval or gratitude to others who seem to reward or cooperate strongly; - experimenting to see whether increased rewarding stabilizes group cooperation. They do not appear to systematically “buy” personal favor; there is no pattern of always spending to secure their own incoming rewards, and at times they accept receiving rewards while giving none. This suggests they are not purely exchange-oriented or tit-for-tat in the reward stage. Instead, they seem to treat rewards as a discretionary, prosocial instrument, used when they judge it worthwhile to recognize or encourage certain behaviors, and withheld when they either feel it is unnecessary or too costly relative to the perceived benefit. Overall, they are a conditional rewarder: capable of generous reinforcement, yet willing to refrain from rewarding when signals from the group do not strongly justify the cost. Their focus appears more on supporting cooperative norms than on micromanaging every deviation.

This player never spends on rewards despite having many opportunities to do so, including toward consistent high contributors who reward others. They do, however, receive substantial rewards from several group members in multiple periods, sometimes quite concentrated, yet this does not lead them to begin rewarding in return. Thus they do not use reward as a tool for reciprocity, gratitude signaling, or strategic reinforcement. Their stance is best characterized as “non-rewarding cooperative”: they are willing to bear the cost of full contribution but not the additional cost of rewarding others, even when such rewards are cost-neutral in expected value terms. Possible reasons (inferred with low confidence) include: a preference for simplicity (focusing on contribution decisions only), a belief that rewarding is unnecessary or inefficient, or indifference to actively shaping others’ behavior via incentives. There is no evidence of selective or targeted rewarding; all-zero reward behavior suggests a consistent internal rule: contribute maximally but do not spend further resources on others after the redistribution stage.

This player never purchases reward in any round, despite having multiple opportunities to do so in a setting where many others actively use the reward mechanism. At the same time, they accept and appear to benefit from reward granted by others without reciprocating. This combination indicates a “non-rewarder beneficiary” type: they are open to positive reciprocity in principle (they continue to cooperate even after being rewarded) but do not themselves expend resources to reinforce others’ cooperation. Their non-use of the reward tool is stable rather than situational; they refrain from rewarding in environments with both high and low contributions, during periods when they themselves are low relative contributors and periods when they contribute at or above the norm. This suggests that the absence of rewarding is a general preference or heuristic rather than a tactical response to any specific pattern of others’ behavior. There is no observable pattern of targeted gratitude signaling: they do not use reward to thank those who contribute a lot, nor to respond to those who reward them. Likewise, they do not engage in strategic reinforcement of high cooperators, even when such reinforcement might help sustain beneficial group behavior. Overall, their stance toward reward is “exploit passive benefits, avoid active costs”: they let others bear the burden of any incentive provision while focusing their own decisions on contribution choices.

This player never spends on rewarding others, despite having repeated opportunities and clear evidence that many others are using the reward mechanism extensively, often in ways that look like attempts to support high contributors or build alliances. Their own behavior shows a complete avoidance of that cost. This suggests that they treat the reward technology as individually unprofitable “noise” rather than as a tool to strategically shape behavior. They do not use reward to reciprocate high contributions, to express gratitude, or to signal themselves as a cooperative partner. They also do not use it to join existing informal coalitions of rewarders. Either they doubt that anonymous, one-shot rewards can meaningfully influence future behavior, or they simply prioritize retaining their own payoff over any long-term coordination benefits. Because they never reward in any context—neither when others are very generous nor when they themselves benefit from group cooperation—the most consistent interpretation is that they have a strictly individualistic cost–benefit threshold for prosocial moves: costly positive actions that do not yield clear, immediate, private gains are avoided. They may still value public-good contributions on their own, but they do not see additional value in layering rewards on top of that.

This player uses reward selectively and sparingly to positively reinforce cooperative behavior. They only reward clear contributors who match their own cooperative standard; they never reward non‑contributors or marginal contributors. Their rewards are low in magnitude and infrequent, suggesting that they are cost‑sensitive or see reward as a symbolic signal rather than a main strategic tool. They show some variation in whom they reward among the consistently cooperative players, implying that they may be sampling or rotating recipients rather than forming a tight bilateral alliance. They do not appear to condition immediate rewards on recent deviations (for instance, they do not counter‑reward past defectors who later cooperate), which points to a relatively simple heuristic: “reward some cooperators, ignore defectors.” The use of reward seems more like prosocial norm signaling than calculated investment in reciprocity, although whether they expect long‑term indirect returns is unknown.

The player never uses costly rewards, even though they repeatedly interact with many high contributors and occasionally with unanimous full cooperation. They do not selectively reward especially cooperative others, nor do they attempt to reinforce high cooperation levels through positive incentives. Their inaction is consistent across varying group contribution patterns and does not change after they themselves receive rewards. This suggests that, although they value cooperation enough to always contribute, they do not see costly interpersonal transfers as part of how they want to manage the group. Possible reasons (all uncertain) include: seeing rewards as redundant when cooperation is already high, preferring to let the public good mechanism itself “reward” everyone, or preferring to conserve resources. In any case, their type is “non-rewarder” in practice.

This player never uses the reward option, even in environments with high cooperation where many others match their own contribution level. They do not provide positive reinforcement to high contributors, and they do not appear to respond strategically to received rewards by reciprocating. Their non-use of rewards persists even when rewarding would be cost-neutral in expectation for the pair (same cost and benefit), and when there are obvious candidates whose behavior is highly cooperative. This suggests they prioritize keeping all available coins rather than spending on interpersonal signaling or social reinforcement. They do not appear to invest in alliance-building or gratitude signaling via formal rewards. The motive could be a narrow payoff focus with respect to transfer decisions (willing to contribute to the public good but not to bilateral transfers), a reluctance to engage in interpersonal interventions of any kind, or simple indifference to the reward mechanism; the precise reason is unknown. Behaviorally, they are a pure contributor but a non-rewarder.

The player is notably reluctant to use the reward mechanism, despite being a consistent high contributor themselves and despite being in groups where many others also cooperate. They never pay to reward anyone, even when almost all others behave cooperatively and even after receiving rewards themselves. This indicates they do not treat positive reinforcement as worthwhile or necessary, perhaps because they view cooperation as a baseline obligation that does not merit extra payment. They also do not appear to use rewards strategically to build alliances or to stabilize cooperation. Compared with their willingness to punish, their use of rewards is effectively zero, suggesting an asymmetric norm‑enforcement style that relies on negative, not positive, incentives.

This player does not voluntarily reward others at any point, despite frequent high contributions by many peers and the availability of a one-to-one reward technology. They appear unwilling to incur certain personal cost merely to express gratitude or reinforce cooperation. This is consistent with a payoff-oriented mindset: if reward is neutral or negative in expected personal return, they avoid it. Their use of the reward mechanism is purely passive: they receive rewards from others but never initiate them. They therefore are not a “positive reciprocity” type and do not invest in building alliances or reputations through rewards, even when many around them behave cooperatively enough to justify such actions on normative grounds. In summary, they behave as a non-rewarder by default: they rely on contributions themselves as their main pro-social act and leave costly positive sanctioning to others.

This player never uses the reward option, even when others behave very cooperatively and even when they themselves receive rewards. They therefore reveal a strong preference to avoid paying for positive sanctions, despite the group’s high levels of cooperation and despite rewarders being identifiable. This suggests they do not see reward as necessary for sustaining norms given already high contributions, or they consider the cost of rewarding unjustified for the marginal benefit. Their enforcement style is thus one-sided: they rely on occasional punishment rather than on positive reinforcement. There is no evidence that they strategically reward especially cooperative others to secure alliances or future benefits, nor that they use rewards to reciprocate or signal gratitude. Their stance toward the reward mechanism appears to be that of a non-user: they either discount its effectiveness or simply prioritize conserving personal payoff over investing in relational or motivational capital.
