This player does not use the reward mechanism at all, despite being a consistent high contributor and observing other high contributors. Their behavior suggests they do not see costly rewards as necessary for sustaining cooperation when a strong norm is already in place, or they view the cost–benefit ratio of reward as unattractive relative to punishment. They may implicitly rely on punishment and on their own example of full contribution as sufficient tools for norm enforcement. The absence of selective positive reinforcement, even toward especially cooperative or possibly allied players, indicates a preference for negative incentives when sanctioning is needed, and neutrality otherwise. Their stance on rewards as a strategic tool beyond this experiment is therefore uncertain, but in this environment they effectively ignore the reward option.

They are quite willing to spend on rewards and do so repeatedly, even though the reward technology is efficiency-neutral (they pay as much as they give). Their rewards consistently target players who contribute fully or who sustain or re-join high cooperation, including both consistently cooperative peers and at least one case of a prior low contributor who later cooperates. This pattern suggests that they use rewards both as gratitude for reliable cooperators and as positive reinforcement for (re-)alignment with the cooperation norm. They allocate multiple small, evenly distributed rewards rather than concentrating on a single ally, indicating a broad, group-oriented prosocial style rather than narrow coalition-building. They do not systematically reward every cooperator, implying some selectivity; they seem to highlight either especially salient cooperators or those whose behavior change or reliability stands out to them. Overall, they accept a personal payoff cost to support and stabilize group cooperation through positive incentives.

They are quite willing to use rewards and generally direct them toward players who contribute fully, especially those who consistently cooperate. Rewarding behavior is purposeful rather than random: they reinforce both a standout cooperator and, at one point, a broad subset of high-contributing peers. They sometimes reward a particularly consistent cooperator even when everyone else is also contributing fully, suggesting they may recognize and support especially reliable partners or those they see as “pillars” of the group norm. They also show willingness to spread rewards across multiple cooperators in one decision, indicating an inclusive orientation and possibly a desire to strengthen overall group cohesion. Because the reward technology is payoff-neutral to the giver (cost equals benefit), their use of rewards does not sacrifice own income, but it still reveals preferences: they expend decision effort to send positive signals and to acknowledge cooperative behavior. The pattern of pairing punishment of defectors with rewards to cooperators in some periods implies a dual strategy: discourage free-riding and simultaneously encourage and recognize norm-following. This is consistent with a prosocial, institution-builder type who tries to shape the social environment in both directions rather than relying only on negative sanctions.

They show a clear willingness to use reward to reinforce cooperative partners, even though rewarding is financially neutral (no social multiplier). They direct rewards toward a consistently high-contributing peer, apparently treating this player as a “model cooperator” deserving positive reinforcement. This indicates a preference for affirming and supporting reliable contributors as a social norm, rather than using reward purely as a profit-seeking tool. They are selective: only one partner is rewarded repeatedly, which suggests they either (a) view that partner as especially trustworthy or exemplary, or (b) believe reinforcing one salient cooperator is enough to stabilize cooperation. They do not reward everyone, even when nearly all contribute, so their reward strategy is not egalitarian; it is targeted recognition. The timing of rewards, especially when cooperation levels dip in the group, suggests an attempt to buttress and encourage stable cooperation under pressure. Overall, they function as a focused positive-reinforcer of group norms rather than a broad “reward for all” type.

This player never uses the reward option, even when nearly all other players contribute fully and at least one peer rewards them. They do not engage in positive reinforcement of high contributors, nor do they reciprocate specific rewards received. Given that reward in this game is exactly cost-equal to its benefit, their non-use suggests they see it as at best neutral and at worst a distraction from maximizing their own payoff. It is unclear whether they conceptually value reciprocity; they simply do not spend coins on rewarding others, even in highly cooperative environments. Their revealed preference is to rely on their own stable cooperation rather than attempt to shape others’ behavior via costly prosocial incentives. Thus they appear “reward-averse or reward-indifferent,” favoring self-contained cooperative behavior over explicit positive sanctioning.

The player never spends resources on rewarding others despite frequently benefiting from high contributions and occasionally receiving a reward themselves. This indicates that they do not invest in positive reinforcement or gratitude signaling, even when such actions would be cost-neutral for the group and could potentially build goodwill. Their social strategy is therefore one-sided: they accept rewards and the benefits of cooperation but do not reciprocate with prosocial transfers. This supports a characterization as payoff-focused and minimally other-regarding in the reward domain. They do not appear to use rewards strategically to influence future behavior or to cultivate allies; either they do not value such longer-term positioning or they discount it in favor of conserving their own payoff.

The player is willing to use rewards, but only in a specific, concentrated way. Most of the time they do not reward anyone, even when many others contribute fully. On one occasion, when virtually everyone cooperates, they spend heavily to reward almost all other players, including both consistent cooperators and prior defectors who have moved to cooperation. This mass rewarding is unconditional with respect to individual histories in that moment and appears to celebrate or reinforce a highly cooperative group state. They do not use small, targeted rewards to shape individual behavior; instead, they use an all-encompassing reward burst when group cooperation aligns with their apparent norm. Given that the reward technology is payoff-neutral for them, this pattern suggests they are willing to sacrifice a substantial share of that round’s net payoff to positively reinforce widespread cooperation and perhaps to signal approval and inclusiveness. They do not show evidence of using rewards strategically to build specific alliances.

They never reward anyone, despite being in groups where others sometimes behave cooperatively and where they themselves are occasionally rewarded. This indicates that positive reciprocity via the reward tool is not part of their strategy. They do not use rewards to encourage high contributors, build alliances, or reciprocate kindness. Their non-use persists even after experiencing a reward, suggesting their abstention is not due simply to ignorance of the mechanism. The most parsimonious interpretation is that they view the reward tool as unnecessary or wasteful, preferring to support the public good solely through contribution rather than side-payments. Whether they consider rewards unfair, strategically ineffective, or simply not worth the cost is unknown.

They never use the reward mechanism, despite being a consistent high contributor and receiving rewards themselves in some cooperative periods. This strongly indicates that they are not inclined to spend their own resources to positively reinforce others, even when they might appreciate being rewarded. Their non-use of rewards suggests a focus on own-payoff preservation once their contribution decision is made: they are willing to cooperate by contributing, but not to further reduce their payoff through additional prosocial transfers. It also implies they do not attempt to build explicit alliances or reciprocity loops via the reward tool. Their behavior fits a “pure contributor, not a meta-enforcer” profile: cooperation through their own contributions, but no extra-cost involvement in social engineering of others’ behavior, whether via carrots or sticks.

The player is willing to use rewards, but does so in a nuanced and somewhat unconventional way. They reward both clear cooperators and, at least once, a clear defector. This indicates that they do not use rewards purely as backward-looking praise for high contributions; instead, they may also use them as forward-looking strategic tools—either to encourage improvement, to experiment with how others respond, or to build relational capital. Their overall reward frequency is low, suggesting that they value their own payoff and do not see constant rewarding as worthwhile when it is exactly cost-equal (no multiplier advantage). The fact that they still spend on rewards at all, despite no efficiency gain from the mechanism, reveals a social or strategic motive beyond pure payoff maximization: they may want to shape norms, express approval or disapproval, or test who is responsive. They selectively choose whom to reward rather than distributing rewards broadly. This selectivity, along with the mix of recipients (both cooperative and non-cooperative), supports the view that they use rewards as targeted signals—sometimes reinforcing cooperation, sometimes signaling goodwill or offering a “carrot” to a previous defector.

They never use the reward option, even when cooperation is high and others reward them. This suggests they do not rely on positive incentives to shape behavior and instead view punishment as the primary enforcement tool. The symmetric cost–benefit of rewarding (no social multiplier) may also make it seem inefficient relative to punishment, which has a stronger impact per coin spent; their behavior aligns with this cost-effectiveness difference. They do not engage in gratitude signaling via rewards, even after being rewarded themselves, which implies they are not focused on building alliances through material reciprocation. Their social enforcement style is thus one-sided: they are willing to incur costs to punish defectors but abstain from spending on rewarding cooperators.

The player very rarely uses rewards, deploying them only once, and again toward the same peer they have previously punished. This reward occurs at a time when that peer is currently defecting while others cooperate, and the player themselves is still fully contributing. Rewarding a present defector is strategically costly and does not clearly reinforce good behavior. The most plausible interpretation (though low confidence) is that the reward is an attempt at complex signaling: it may be an effort to soften a prior harsh punishment, to “reset” relations, or to experiment with a carrot after using a stick. Another possibility is mis-click or confusion about the mechanism, but there is no definitive evidence of that. What is clear is that this player does not systematically reward cooperators or use rewards to maintain high contributions in general. Reward is not part of their core enforcement toolkit; it appears as a single, idiosyncratic act rather than a stable strategy. They primarily rely on high contribution and occasional strong punishment, not on positive reinforcement.

The player never uses costly reward, even when others contribute fully or return to full cooperation after having defected. They thus do not engage in positive reinforcement of cooperative behavior, despite consistently benefiting from group cooperation. This may indicate that they view full contribution as a baseline obligation rather than something that merits extra compensation, or that they are generally averse to spending coins on social incentives. Overall, they show no inclination toward gratitude signaling or using rewards to stabilize norms; they rely solely on their own consistent cooperation and a single early verbal message.

The player never uses the reward option, including in long stretches where everyone cooperates and where positive reinforcement of high contributors could be strategically or normatively natural. This suggests that they do not engage in costly prosocial signaling via rewards and do not attempt to engineer better group outcomes through positive incentives. Given that they receive a sizable reward transfer from someone else after they defect, their continued refusal to reward even then indicates that they are not motivated by gratitude‑based reciprocity or by symmetric “returning favors” logic within the reward mechanism. They accept windfalls but do not mirror the behavior. Taken together with their non‑punishment, their overall sanctioning profile is one of complete passivity: they neither punish nor reward. They rely on their own contribution decisions rather than shaping others’ payoffs through the incentive tools the game provides.

They also never use the reward mechanism, even in long stretches of perfect mutual cooperation. This indicates they are not inclined to spend on positive reinforcement, gratitude, or strategic rewarding to stabilize cooperation. Given that they actively value cooperation in their chat and contribution choices, their non-use of rewards is more plausibly about the perceived inefficiency or personal cost of rewarding, rather than indifference to others’ cooperative behavior. They appear to believe that verbal encouragement plus their own consistent full contributions are sufficient signals, and that paying extra to reward is not worth it. They thus rely on contribution behavior and minimal communication rather than monetary incentives to influence others, behaving as a “non-incentivizer” on both the punishment and reward sides.

The player never uses reward, even when others fully cooperate alongside them. They do not employ positive reinforcement to encourage high contributions, despite repeated cooperative group outcomes. This suggests that, for them, cooperation is expected and does not merit extra incentive payments, or that they regard the reward mechanism as economically inefficient and therefore avoid it. Their behavior is consistent with a neutral stance toward reward rather than a desire to build reciprocal alliances or signal gratitude through the mechanism. They appear to prefer that the payoff structure of the PGG itself do the work of incentivizing cooperation, instead of layering additional, costly rewards on top.

This player frequently uses the reward mechanism and consistently spends some of their payoff to reward others. They are willing to incur real cost to support and reinforce cooperation, even though the reward technology is exactly cost-equal (no multiplier), so the act cannot increase total surplus. This indicates a preference for social reinforcement and possibly gratitude or reciprocity rather than pure efficiency maximization. The pattern suggests they are not purely altruistic toward everyone at all times: they vary the amount they spend across rounds, which likely reflects selective rewarding of perceived cooperators, though exact targets and rules are unknown because reward recipients are not identified. They also receive rewards from others and do not verbally reference them, so there is no explicit gratitude signaling, but their continued cooperation and ongoing use of rewards are consistent with a generalized, indirect reciprocity strategy: support the cooperative norm, reward contributors, and maintain group cohesion. Their willingness to give up coins for equal-value rewards suggests they may value the psychological or normative function of reward—encouraging group-wide cooperation and acknowledging others—over strictly maximizing individual earnings.

They never spend on rewards, despite repeatedly receiving rewards from others and despite clear information on the reward mechanism. This strongly suggests a non-rewarding cooperative type: they contribute fully but do not engage in costly social reinforcement, even when it is normatively encouraged by others’ behavior. They do not appear to reciprocate received rewards, nor do they use rewards to shape others’ contributions, indicating either a strong concern for keeping all residual payoff, skepticism about the value of rewarding when it has no social multiplier, or simple disinterest in that mechanism. Their stance fits a “pure contributor” profile: they generate public benefits through contributions alone while abstaining from extra costly prosocial actions like rewarding.

They make substantial use of the reward mechanism, including in rounds where they themselves contribute nothing. At multiple points, they spend nontrivial amounts on rewards, even when the reward technology is break-even at best, indicating that they are not using reward to increase their own direct material payoff. Instead, they appear willing to sacrifice some earnings to influence or acknowledge others, though the exact targets are unobservable. Their pattern suggests a few possibilities, all with low to moderate confidence: they may be rewarding high contributors to sustain group cooperation that they can free-ride on; they may be selectively rewarding particular players (e.g., for social reasons or perceived deservingness); or they may use rewards in the end-game to redistribute for idiosyncratic reasons unrelated to future incentives. They never use rewards to punish, as punishment is unavailable, and they do not use the absence of punishment to adjust their exploitation of the group. Overall, they are a non-cooperative contributor but an active, sometimes heavy, spender on rewards, implying a type that free-rides on the public good while still valuing the ability to transfer or acknowledge payoffs at personal cost.

They never spend any resources on rewarding others, even though they consistently cooperate and repeatedly receive rewards themselves. This indicates that they separate their own contribution norm from any notion of reciprocal reward or social reinforcement. They do not appear to use the reward mechanism instrumentally (to encourage group cooperation) or expressively (to signal gratitude or approval), despite being a beneficiary of others’ rewards. This could reflect a preference to conserve private payoff once the contribution decision is made, a belief that rewards are unnecessary or inefficient, or simple inattention or disinterest in the extra mechanism. Since they never reward anyone, their criteria for rewarding (e.g., reciprocity, conditionality on others’ cooperation, or targeting defectors vs. cooperators) remain unknown.

The player is an active but selective rewarder whose behavior changes across the game. Early on, they invest heavily in rewards, then sharply reduce this spending in the mid-game, and finally ramp it back up at the end. This non-monotonic pattern suggests exploratory, strategic, or mood-driven use of rewards rather than a rigid rule. Because specific targets are unobserved, the exact triggers are unknown, but the timing implies they may initially try to encourage group cooperation, then conserve resources once high contributions appear established, and later use rewards more freely as the game concludes. The player shows clear willingness to sacrifice private payoff to provide rewards, indicating some combination of altruistic preferences, norm reinforcement, or a desire to reciprocate perceived kindness. At one point, they give no rewards despite continued full cooperation, implying they do not feel obligated to constantly reward others and are capable of withholding relational investment while still contributing. Their reward decisions seem decoupled from short-term material self-interest, and instead aligned with loose, evolving intuitions about when positive reinforcement is “worth it,” rather than with precise, outcome-based calculations.

They are highly willing to use the reward mechanism and do so at significant personal cost, especially when cooperation in the group is strong. They appear to treat reward as a social and strategic tool to reinforce high contribution norms and possibly to maintain goodwill, despite the fact that the reward technology is cost-neutral and does not increase joint surplus. Their use of rewards is not purely self-protective or narrowly profit-maximizing; they spend notably more on rewarding others than they receive back and accept lower immediate payoffs in exchange for supporting the cooperative environment. They do not publicly specify who they reward or why, but the timing suggests they are more inclined to reward when group cooperation is high or improving, using reward as positive reinforcement rather than as compensation for personal grievances. They do not reward in every round, indicating some sensitivity to cost and context and a willingness to suspend rewarding when it no longer aligns with their short-term payoff goals. Overall, they behave like a generous but selective rewarder who is prepared to invest in reciprocity and norm support, even when it is not strictly payoff-dominant in the short run.

They never spend on rewards, despite being a consistent high contributor and a frequent recipient of rewards from others. This suggests a strong focus on conserving personal payoff once cooperation is already high, or a belief that costly rewarding is unnecessary given existing group behavior. There is no indication that they use rewards as a strategic tool to reinforce cooperation or to reciprocate kindness; they accept rewards but do not mirror them. Their non-use of rewards persists even in environments of near-universal cooperation, where positive reciprocity might be expected, implying either low intrinsic value placed on rewarding, a more self-interested stance regarding side-payments, or simple inattention to the mechanism. Their specific triggers for rewarding or thresholds for action remain unknown, as they never cross them in this game.

This player never spends any of their own coins to reward others, despite consistently receiving positive rewards from the group. They do not reciprocate reward behavior materially, even after being singled out as a recipient of broad, group-wide rewarding from at least one other cooperator. This pattern suggests that they treat reward as a passive bonus rather than a tool they actively use for norm reinforcement or gratitude signaling. They appear to rely on others to provide incentive structures (cooperation guidance, rewards) rather than initiating such structures themselves. Their non-use of reward is not clearly hostile or exploitative; it may reflect a preference for keeping their own payoff, a lack of attention to or understanding of the strategic purpose of rewards, or simply a low inclination to manage others’ behavior. Overall, their “reward type” is best described as a non-rewarding cooperator: they cooperate strongly once convinced, but they do not invest in pro-social incentives for others, even when they personally benefit from those incentives.

This player is a consistent and relatively heavy user of the reward mechanism. They repeatedly spend substantial amounts on rewarding others across the game, including when they themselves do not receive much or any reward in return. Their willingness to pay for reward remains high even when it is not clearly benefiting them monetarily, and it does not taper off toward the end of the game; if anything, they intensify use before the final payoff is realized. This pattern suggests that they treat reward primarily as a tool to support and encourage cooperation rather than as a calculated investment in personal profit. They appear comfortable accepting the cost-symmetry of the mechanism (it costs as much as it gives) and still choose to use it repeatedly, indicating either intrinsic satisfaction from reinforcing “good” behavior, a desire to sustain a positive group norm, or both. There is no evidence that they use reward in a narrowly strategic manner (e.g., only rewarding when it clearly increases their future payoffs, or selectively targeting specific swing players); the exact targeting pattern is unknown, but the aggregate cost levels imply broad, non-grudging generosity. Gratitude signaling or attempts to reciprocate received rewards specifically are possible but not directly inferable from the transcript; what is clear is a stable disposition toward costly, positive reinforcement of the group.

The player is willing to spend substantial resources on rewarding in some situations and completely withholds rewards in others, indicating that they view rewarding as discretionary rather than obligatory. When they do reward, they spend a sizable portion of their available payoff, consistent with a readiness to incur meaningful personal cost to reinforce behavior they see as desirable. However, the exact trigger for rewarding is partially opaque because the specific targets are not visible. The timing suggests that they are more inclined to reward when they may be trying to establish or reinforce cooperative norms early on, and less inclined once widespread cooperation is already established or when they do not see additional benefit from reinforcing it. They exhibit no visible pattern of retaliatory or corrective rewarding (e.g., selectively excluding defectors) from the transcript alone; whether they strategically differentiate between high and low contributors in their reward allocations is unknown. They do not verbally thank others for rewarding them nor signal gratitude through chat, so any reciprocity in their reward behavior is implicit rather than explicitly communicated. Overall, they are a high-cooperation player who treats rewards as a flexible, context-dependent tool rather than as a consistent part of their cooperative identity.

This player is an active and generous user of rewards, willing to spend substantial resources to incentivize and reinforce cooperation. They repeatedly incur nontrivial costs to give rewards to others, indicating that they are not using the reward tool sparingly or only in extreme cases. Their reward behavior is clearly strategic and norm-expressive: they direct rewards broadly to cooperators and explicitly withhold reward from at least one persistent non-contributor, calling attention to that exclusion in chat. This shows they treat rewards both as positive reinforcement and as a soft, non-punitive way to sanction free-riding by omission. They also reward while simultaneously encouraging lagging players to change, suggesting they see rewards as part of a package of persuasion and norm-building. Their willingness to keep rewarding late in the game, and to reward many players at once, indicates low aversion to the personal cost of rewarding and a strong orientation toward shaping a cooperative environment, even when individual material returns from those rewards are uncertain or limited.

This player is a heavy and highly active rewarder, frequently spending large amounts of their payoff to transfer coins to others. They use reward in a distinctly prosocial, not profit-maximizing, way: they sometimes give away so much that their own earnings drop substantially, revealing that reinforcing cooperation and relationships matters more to them than maximizing personal income. Their reward targeting is nuanced rather than purely proportional to contributions. They often give substantial rewards to those who have under-contributed in previous rounds but then increase contributions, consistent with a “rehabilitative” or encouragement approach: they appear to reward positive change or effort rather than only absolute high contribution levels. They also sometimes reward wide swathes of the group, including already high contributors, as if to maintain a broad cooperative climate and express appreciation. They do not use reward strictly as a narrow strategic investment (e.g., only to those most likely to reciprocate materially). Instead, they seem to treat it as a social signal and motivational tool: rewarding to say “I see your effort,” “you’re still part of the group,” or “thank you for cooperating,” even when there is little evidence that specific individuals will reliably pay them back. They are willing to spend extremely high amounts on rewards relative to any reward they personally receive. This identifies them as a strong positive reciprocator and moralist: they behave as though increasing the group’s cooperative atmosphere is intrinsically valuable, even when net costly.

The player also uses rewards frequently and systematically, focusing almost exclusively on those who contribute at or near the high group standard. They tend to distribute small, equal-sized rewards across many high contributors, suggesting a desire to positively reinforce cooperative norms and to acknowledge prosocial behavior broadly rather than forming narrow alliances. Rewards often co-occur with punishment in the same period, implying a dual strategy of “carrot and stick”: sanctioning defectors while simultaneously encouraging or thanking cooperators. At times they intensify rewards specifically for especially reliable or standout contributors, which may serve to stabilize high-cooperation coalitions or maintain morale when others are slipping. Since rewards are costly and yield no net material gain for the group, the player’s consistent willingness to spend on them points to an intrinsic valuing of fairness, reciprocity, or mutual encouragement over personal payoff. Rewarding is clearly conditional: there is no sign of random generosity; it tracks cooperation levels closely.

They are highly willing to reward others and do so in a diffuse, inclusive manner. Early and mid-game, they spend substantial amounts of their own payoff to distribute rewards widely, rather than concentrating them on a few top cooperators. They sometimes even reward players who have contributed less than the apparent group norm, indicating that they do not use reward as a strict proportional-pay scheme. Instead, they tend to reward almost everyone who is participating at all, with only limited differentiation by contribution level. This looks more like a prosocial, morale-building or “friendliness-signaling” strategy than a tightly strategic reinforcement of only the highest contributors. They also occasionally suspend rewarding entirely, even when others are highly cooperative, indicating that they do not see reward as an obligatory tit-for-tat rule but as an optional tool. Over time, their use of rewards becomes less frequent, which may reflect learning about its limited impact on behavior or a desire to conserve payoff; this interpretation is tentative. Overall, they act as a generous, broadly affiliative rewarder who spends real resources to make others better off, without using reward in a fine-grained, performance-contingent way.

This player uses reward frequently and selectively, but with a lower intensity per target than their punishment. They are willing to incur direct costs to transfer coins positively to several different peers. Reward is often directed at players who either (a) contribute consistently high amounts, (b) modestly improve after earlier mixed behavior, or (c) appear neutral or friendly in the broader conflict structure. This points to a desire to reinforce supportive or cooperative others and perhaps to cultivate allies. They sometimes combine reward and punishment in the same opportunity set, rewarding some and punishing others, which indicates a differentiated social map: they are not in a “pure hostility” or “pure generosity” mode, but use both tools in parallel for distinct relationships. Reward is never used as aggressively as punishment (in terms of magnitude), and they do not appear to use it primarily for strategic profit: the 1:1 reward technology is weak, yet they still incur this cost, suggesting a genuine preference for expressing approval or gratitude, even when it is not monetarily advantageous. Overall, their rewarding behavior appears to serve two functions: relational signaling (“you are on my good side”) and positive reinforcement of contributors they deem cooperative or sympathetic. There is no clear evidence that they systematically reward former punishers to repair relationships; instead, reward and punishment appear to define separate in‑group and out‑group categories.

Rewarding is central to this player’s type. They reward in almost every round, often at very large personal cost, including extreme expenditures that significantly lower or even make their round payoff negative. They show no concern for optimizing their own earnings when choosing reward levels. Triggers for reward are broadly cooperative behavior: they repeatedly distribute rewards widely across many or all peers, typically including both very high contributors and moderate contributors, and at times even those who contribute little. This indicates that rewards are not tightly calibrated to exact contribution levels; instead, they appear to use rewards to maintain goodwill, encourage group cohesion, and perhaps signal appreciation for ongoing participation more generally. At a few points, they concentrate large rewards on a single peer who is consistently cooperative, suggesting willingness to form or signal especially strong support for highly cooperative partners. However, because they also distribute rewards even when some peers have recently under‑contributed, they are not using a strict “reward only the best” strategy; their threshold for “deserving” is low. They do not combine reward with punishment in the same round; they seem to treat reward and punishment as distinct tools and overwhelmingly favor the positive tool. The sheer scale and persistence of their rewarding implies prosocial or altruistic motives: they value others’ payoffs and the cooperative atmosphere highly, even at large expense to themselves, and may believe that generous rewards can stabilize or improve group cooperation over time.

They are an extremely active and generous rewarder, frequently spending large amounts to send positive transfers to many other players. They focus rewards on those who contribute substantially, regularly reinforcing high contributors and often including moderately contributing players as well. They largely ignore or minimally reward those who contribute nothing or very little, but they still do not punish them. Their reward pattern is selective and structured rather than random: higher contributors tend to receive more units, and consistently cooperative co-players are repeatedly supported. This indicates a clear attempt at strategic positive reinforcement of prosocial norms in the group. They appear willing to incur substantial personal costs to encourage and sustain a cooperative atmosphere, even when this clearly reduces their own material payoff. The behavior is consistent with a “positive peer enforcer” who substitutes reward for punishment as their main social tool.

This player is an active and consistent user of reward. They frequently spend on positive transfers and do so across many rounds, indicating a real willingness to incur personal costs to benefit others. Their reward pattern is selective and nuanced rather than uniformly directed only at top contributors. They often reward players who deviate downward from very high contributions but still participate, or who move upward from very low contributions, suggesting a focus on encouraging improvement and inclusion rather than just celebrating perfect cooperation. They sometimes reward individuals who are contributing far less than the dominant full-contribution norm, including near-zero contributors. This indicates that they may be using reward as a tool to “pull in” peripheral or struggling participants, perhaps to prevent social fracture or to encourage gradual norm compliance. They also recurrently reward the same individuals across time, implying that they track social histories and attempt to sustain relationships or reinforce trajectories they perceive as positive. Their reward behavior does not appear narrowly profit-maximizing: they spend in situations where there is no clear immediate return and where recipients are not the main drivers of the public good. This suggests intrinsic valuation of supporting others, or a belief that positive reinforcement is better than punishment for sustaining cooperation. Overall, they act as a “supportive rewarder”: they reliably use small rewards as social signals and as a low-intensity coordination device, not merely as payment for strict high contributions.

This player is an extremely active rewarder who relies on positive incentives as their primary strategic tool. They routinely spend a sizable portion of their payoff on rewarding others, often distributing many small rewards to multiple high contributors. Their rewards are clearly conditional on perceived cooperation: they consistently favor those who contribute fully or near-fully and omit or downweight clear free-riders and very low contributors. Within the subset of high contributors, they show some differentiation, occasionally giving larger rewards to particular peers who appear especially reliable. They appear to use rewards in three ways: (1) to reinforce and stabilize high contributions, (2) to acknowledge and support a core of “allies” in cooperation, and (3) to express approval of adherence to a cooperative norm rather than absolute fairness in payoffs. When overall contributions drop substantially, they temporarily stop rewarding, suggesting they treat rewards as a contingent privilege that depends on a broadly cooperative environment. They do not use reward as a simple tit-for-tat of what they personally receive; their giving is more strongly tied to others’ contributions than to incoming rewards.

The player makes extremely limited use of the reward mechanism. Despite many peers contributing at or near the maximum for most of the game, they rarely provide positive reinforcement. When they do reward, it is minimal and directed at an already consistently cooperative partner, not at a marginal improver or someone whose behavior they need to encourage. This looks more like a token acknowledgment than a systematic strategy to cultivate cooperation. They do not appear to use rewards as a main tool for shaping group behavior; instead they rely on punishment to correct low contributions and personally model full cooperation. Their low use of rewards, despite frequent opportunities, suggests a preference for sanction-based norm enforcement and a belief that cooperation is an obligation, not something that merits frequent bonus payments.

They never use the reward option, despite frequently being in a position to acknowledge high-contributing peers. This holds even though reward is symmetric in cost and benefit and thus not intrinsically inefficient. Their consistent abstention implies that, for them, positive sanctioning is not part of their cooperative toolkit. Several interpretations are possible: they may view reward as unnecessary given already-high contributions; they may wish to conserve resources and keep the game “clean” of side-payments; or they may not attend strongly to targeted reciprocity mechanisms at all, focusing instead on their own contribution choices. What is clear from behavior is that they do not engage in selective reinforcement of others’ cooperation via rewards, even when others reward them. This positions them as a “non-rewarding cooperator”: they contribute to the public good but do not layer on extra, targeted positive incentives for others.

The player uses rewards more frequently and more flexibly than punishment, indicating a preference for positive reinforcement over sanctions. They reward both selectively and, at times, broadly. In some instances, they direct small rewards to specific cooperators or moderate contributors, which aligns with a strategy of encouraging desirable behavior and showing appreciation. In other instances, they distribute rewards widely across many players, including some who have not been especially cooperative at that moment. This broad gifting suggests a willingness to invest in goodwill or to “reset” group relationships positively after tension, rather than purely to incentivize contribution levels. They also at one point reward players who have relatively low contributions alongside high contributors, which implies that reward is not purely merit-based. Possible motives include signaling friendliness, diffusing conflict, or building generalized reciprocity, rather than strict conditional reward for high input. They sometimes abstain from rewarding when cooperation is already high, indicating that they do not feel compelled to constantly spend on rewards; their use is episodic and situational. Strategically, the reward profile suggests a player who values social cohesion, is willing to pay to express approval or solidarity, and is more comfortable shaping the group through positive means than through harm. They show some awareness that reward can be used as a tool, but they do not apply it with tight calibration to individual behavior; instead, they mix targeted reinforcing rewards with more symbolic, group-wide generosity.

They also never reward anyone, even in periods of near-perfect group cooperation or when others are clearly engaging in large-scale reward behavior. This indicates they do not use positive incentives as a coordination or gratitude tool. Their consistent non-use of rewards, combined with non-use of punishment, paints a picture of a player who treats the sanctioning/reward mechanism as noise to ignore rather than as a strategic instrument. They appear to rely on direct contribution choices and group momentum rather than trying to shape others’ behavior financially. The absence of any small, symbolic rewards (even 1-unit tokens for stable cooperators or rescuers from punishment) suggests either that they are indifferent to expressive signaling through the incentive mechanism, or that they judge any such spending as strictly wasteful. They might assume that the group will maintain cooperation without explicit reinforcement, or that their own benefit from such reinforcement is too uncertain to justify costs. Thus, they are a “non-rewarding free-rider on other people’s incentive efforts”: they benefit from others’ use of rewards and punishments to preserve cooperation but do not contribute to that enforcement infrastructure.

The player uses rewards sparingly but, when they do, they do so in an extremely inclusive, egalitarian way. On a single occasion they spend a large amount on rewards and distribute small, equal rewards to essentially all active peers, regardless of any minor differences in recent behavior. The trigger is a moment of perfect group cooperation: they celebrate a “full house” of contributors by generously rewarding everyone, using the mechanism as a group-wide “thank you” rather than as fine-grained selective reinforcement. Outside of that one “celebratory” burst, they do not use rewards again, even though they continue to receive rewards from others during multiple rounds. They verbally acknowledge a specific player’s reward with gratitude once, but they do not reciprocate via the mechanism itself; their gratitude is expressed socially via chat, not materially. This pattern suggests they view rewards as a way to mark and celebrate high collective performance rather than as an ongoing strategic tool. They are willing to incur substantial cost once to reinforce a strong norm and to express appreciation to the whole group, but they are not a persistent or targeted rewarder.

They are highly willing to use reward altruistically, even at large personal cost, especially when the group is fully cooperative. They repeatedly grant positive transfers to almost all or all other players after unanimous max contributions, including very large bulk rewards that dramatically reduce their own payoff. Reward is used broadly and inclusively rather than selectively; they do not preferentially reward friends or chatty players, but essentially the entire contributing group. They also single out specific individuals for extra reward (for example one player receives concentrated positive transfers at two different times), which appears to be a mixture of gratitude and perhaps a corrective gesture after previously punishing them. Reward is not used strategically to shift marginal incentives in a precise way; instead it functions as a social and moral signal: “we are doing this right; I value your cooperation.” Once the time pressure becomes salient and the game is nearly over, they stop rewarding and focus only on enforcing the norm via punishment in obvious defection cases. This suggests some awareness of cost–benefit tradeoffs, but overall they are much more generous than a payoff-maximizer would be.

They also never reward, even in periods where many others are actively rewarding generous cooperators. This suggests they prioritize retaining earnings over spending on reinforcement, or they may simply believe that cooperation is now a stable group norm and does not need additional incentivization. Their gratitude and approval are expressed via chat (agreeing, joking, and socially affiliating) rather than via costly rewards. This pattern is consistent with a “low‑intervention” behavioral style: they contribute fully themselves but do not invest extra resources in managing others’ behavior through rewards. If they value reciprocity, it is expressed verbally and through continued cooperation rather than monetary signals.

They also never spend any coins on rewards, even in periods of near-perfect cooperation and clear group-level gains. They benefit from others’ occasional mass reward waves but never reciprocate or reinforce them. This suggests they do not use positive incentives as a strategic tool, nor do they express gratitude via the formal mechanism. Combined with their refusal to punish, this places them as a “pure contributor-only” type: all their pro-sociality is expressed through contributions, not through second-order interventions. Their stance toward reward systems—whether skeptical of cost-effectiveness, indifferent, or confused—is unknown; what is clear is the behavioral rule: do not spend on reward, even when many others are doing so and even when they are a direct beneficiary.

They never spend on rewards despite frequently receiving rewards from others. This consistent non-use suggests either that they do not perceive reward-giving as necessary for sustaining the cooperative norm, or that they are focused on preserving their own earnings while letting others bear the cost of positive reinforcement. Yet they also do not complain about or discourage reward use; they accept it passively. Their chat behavior implies appreciation for the cooperative environment and for others’ presence, but this appreciation does not translate into costly signaling via the reward mechanism. There is no direct evidence that they consciously choose a “no reward” strategy; the simplest interpretation is that they treat full contribution itself as sufficient cooperative behavior and view additional transfers (reward/punish) as optional noise rather than core tools. Overall, they are a pure contributor rather than a meta-enforcer or meta-rewarder.

They also never use rewards, even though many others start spending heavily to reward and punish. This is notable because they themselves receive substantial positive transfers from others at various points, yet they never reciprocate via the reward mechanism. This suggests that while they appreciate cooperation and encourage it verbally, they do not see the explicit reward tool as necessary or worthwhile, perhaps viewing it as an inefficient use of funds or simply not integrating it into their strategy. Their reinforcement of norms is carried by communication and by their own consistent contributions, rather than by costly positive incentives. They rely on verbal encouragement and example-setting rather than material rewards. There is no evidence of using reward to thank specific players, to build alliances, or to shape others’ behavior; their prosociality is expressed through contribution and morale-boosting chat instead.

They also make extensive use of rewards, though less consistently than punishment and with more rounds of complete non‑use. Their rewarding is often broad and inclusive: in some periods they distribute small rewards to many or nearly all other players, including those who are already contributing fully. In other cases they concentrate rewards on a subset of cooperators. There is no clear pattern that they reward only those who have treated them well or only those with particularly cooperative histories; rather, the pattern looks like periodic positive reinforcement experiments or gestures that increase others’ payoffs without clear strategic targeting. They sometimes combine reward and punishment in the same round, distributing positive and negative transfers across different people while everyone contributes fully, further underscoring that their use of incentives is not simply conditional on contribution. Overall, they are willing to spend nontrivial amounts on reward, but do so episodically and non‑systematically, without clear gratitude signaling or sustained strategic reinforcement of specific partners. Reward, for this type, is another lever to manipulate the payoff landscape rather than a consistent tool for building particular alliances or norms.

They never spend any resources on reward, even when many others are actively rewarding high contributors and even when they personally benefit from those rewards. They accept incoming rewards passively without comment. This consistent non-use of rewarding suggests either: - A preference for conserving coins rather than investing in positive reinforcement, or - A belief that high contributions are simply expected and do not merit extra compensation. Given their strong willingness to spend on punishment, the asymmetry indicates they primarily rely on negative incentives to shape others’ behavior, not on positive reciprocity or gratitude signaling. They do not appear to use rewards strategically to coordinate or reinforce the “max out” equilibrium, even though others clearly do, which aligns with a “strict norm enforcer, not a cheerleader” profile.

They are very willing to use rewards, especially in the first half of the game. Initially, they frequently give small, evenly distributed positive transfers across almost all or all active players, including some who have not deviated and some who have.
