They never escalate reward usage even when others temporarily lower contributions, suggesting they do not see rewarding as their main tool for steering group behavior. Overall, they behave as a “pure contributor” more than a “conditional rewarder,” and they conserve their own payoff by mostly avoiding reward costs.

The player never spends anything on rewards despite repeated opportunities, even when others are clearly cooperative and when they personally benefit from high group contributions. This indicates a strong reluctance to incur personal cost for second-order cooperation (i.e., rewarding cooperators), even though doing so is cost-neutral in this mechanism. They do use the reward mechanism but only in a limited, selective way. Relative to their strong cooperation, their reward spending is modest and sporadic rather than systematic. This player never uses the reward mechanism, despite it being available in every round and despite being rewarded multiple times. Evidence is insufficient to distinguish among these, but their pattern is consistent with a “non-rewarding cooperator/defector” who leaves the provision of incentives entirely to others.

Their reward behavior is extremely polarized: they begin with one very large, non-optimizing reward outlay and then essentially abstain from rewarding, except for a smaller late-game burst. This indicates that they quickly recalibrate, either learning that the cost-benefit tradeoff of this reward technology is poor or deciding that others are already rewarding enough so their own rewards are unnecessary. There is no clear pattern of rewarding particular identities across the game, so repeated-target alliance-building via reward is not evident. Their later small reward outlay occurs when the group is highly cooperative, which is compatible with a one-off “thank you” or expressive gesture rather than a strategic attempt to change behavior. Across the game, they do not seem motivated to fine-tune group incentives via reward, and they do not respond to defectors by rewarding cooperators more selectively. Reward, for them, functions more as a sporadic, non-strategic expression of prosocial intent than as a consistent instrument of social control.

This player is a frequent and relatively heavy user of positive reward, spending substantial resources on rewarding others across many rounds. This suggests they see reward as a strategic tool rather than as a rare, last-resort signal. Their reward pattern is broadly pro-social but not purely altruistic. At the same time, they also reward in rounds where they themselves have defected on contributions, which implies that they may be “buying” social capital or goodwill from others: attempting to maintain a cooperative environment and positive relationships while personally exploiting the public good. Still, their consistent willingness to pay for rewards indicates a stable orientation toward shaping others’ behavior and/or sustaining a cooperative norm through carrots rather than through pure self-interest. They do not appear to use reward in a purely retaliatory way (e.g., withholding rewards from low contributors as a “soft punishment”); instead, they mainly seem to reward positive behavior or perceived allies, with little overt evidence of selective exclusion. Overall, their reward behavior is best described as “generous but instrumentally motivated reinforcement.”

They never spend on reward, despite having many opportunities and despite often earning high payoffs that would give them slack to invest. This player never spends any coins on rewarding others, despite encountering repeated and clearly observable high contributors and generous rewarders. They never spend on rewards in any round, despite repeatedly observing others incur substantial costs to reward contributors and, at times, noncontributors. This player is a consistently active user of the reward mechanism. They almost never spend on rewarding others and resist using the reward mechanism as a routine tool. They almost never spend on rewards despite having many opportunities. When they do use the reward option, it is rare, low in magnitude, and not part of a sustained pattern. This player essentially does not use the reward mechanism. They appear to target high contributors and reliable cooperators rather than low contributors. Evidence is indirect because we do not see their target list, but: when many others contribute high amounts and the group outcome is strong, reward volumes in the group are high and the player’s reward spending is high; they do not spend in low-contribution rounds even when that would be an opportunity to “nudge” defectors. This supports the interpretation that they prefer rewarding those already cooperating, not trying to convert non-cooperators. They occasionally receive nontrivial reward inflows in the same rounds when they themselves devote large amounts to rewarding others, which implies that at least some other players recognize them as a cooperator and rewarder. However, the player’s own reward behavior does not seem directly contingent on being personally rewarded: they keep giving rewards in many cooperative rounds even when their own incoming rewards are low or zero, and they stop using reward entirely later without a visible collapse in incoming rewards. Thus, reward seems driven more by their attempt to sustain a cooperative climate than by simple tit-for-tat gratitude. Once they decide that the cost of reward is not justified—likely when cooperation levels in the group are already relatively high and/or the remaining horizon is short—they abruptly stop rewarding and never resume. This “switch off” pattern indicates a strategic assessment rather than a purely emotional or norm-driven commitment.

This player also abstains entirely from using reward, even though they are frequently surrounded by high contributors and even when they themselves benefit from rewards granted by others. They receive rewards multiple times, yet never reciprocate with any kind of positive reinforcement. This indicates that they do not use reward strategically to encourage or stabilize cooperation, and they do not visibly express gratitude or reciprocity through the available reward channel. Given that reward is individually inefficient here, their behavior is consistent with a payoff-maximizing attitude that sees reward as a poor investment. It might also reflect low engagement with the social signaling dimension of the game: they do not attempt to shape others’ expectations about them as “nice” or “supportive,” nor do they try to selectively encourage particularly generous group members. Taken together with their non-use of punishment, they behave as a player who adapts only via their own contribution level, never via second-order interventions (social incentives). They free-ride on whatever stability others try to create with reward and punishment, but they do not participate in that enforcement system themselves.

Throughout almost all rounds, this player never uses the reward option, despite frequently being in groups with high contributors and seeing other players actively reward. This suggests that either they judge the reward technology as inefficient or they do not see private positive transfers as necessary to sustain cooperation. Their own pattern of steady high contributions without rewarding implies they rely on contribution behavior itself as their main prosocial signal. At the very end, they do issue a reward to a single player while simultaneously punishing others. The chosen recipient is a player who, by that stage, has alternated between low and high contributions and has been both punished and rewarded by others. Rewarding this specific player at the same time as punishing others looks like an attempt to differentiate “approved” from “disapproved” behavior: they signal support to a partner they may perceive as recently moving into a more cooperative or less disruptive role, or simply as less culpable for the sanction chaos. It is unlikely that this is a purely instrumental attempt to influence future behavior, since horizon length is unknown and the game ends; it aligns more with expressive or normative signaling: showing who they see as more deserving. Given how rarely they use reward despite many cooperative partners, they appear to value cost efficiency and see reward as optional rather than essential. When they finally reward, they do so in a highly selective, one-off manner tied to a broader moral classification of others, rather than as a continuous reinforcement of high contributors. This positions them as a “sparing rewarder”: willing to use positive incentives symbolically when making a strong endline statement, but not inclined to spend steadily to encourage cooperation.

This player uses reward frequently, even though the reward technology is inefficient for increasing others’ payoffs. They reward players who contribute high amounts, and they do so in a way that appears consistent over time: persistent high contributors receive repeated rewards, and moderate-but-cooperating contributors sometimes receive recognition as well. They almost never reward low contributors. Their pattern suggests that they view reward as a tool to reinforce cooperative norms and to show approval rather than as a way to maximize group efficiency in a narrow payoff sense. They are willing to sacrifice quite a lot of their own payoff to reward others, even in rounds where they themselves earn relatively little. This implies that they value establishing or maintaining a cooperative culture and perhaps a reputation (even under anonymity of individual actions) more than maximizing personal earnings. Their use of multiple reward units to the same player in a single round shows they sometimes emphasize strong positive reinforcement when someone’s contribution strongly matches their norm of “full cooperation.” They do not appear to use reward strategically to curry favor or avoid punishment, since they continue to reward even after suffering punishments and without obvious payoff benefit. The behavior is consistent with a prosocial, norm-reinforcing type.

This player uses rewards frequently and in a structured way, but is clearly more willing to pay for punishment than for reward, despite punishment being far more cost-effective and reward relatively weak. They reward players who contribute high amounts relative to others and with some consistency: full or near-full contributors are typical recipients of their rewards. This looks like an attempt to reinforce cooperative behavior and to differentially support partners they see as “team players.” They sometimes combine punishment and reward in the same decision phase, punishing low contributors while simultaneously rewarding high contributors. This dual pattern points to a normative signaling strategy: they are not simply venting against free-riders; they are also explicitly marking who is “doing the right thing.” They are willing to reward even when it is materially inefficient, suggesting that communicating approval and shaping future behavior matters to them. Over time, their reward allocation becomes somewhat more selective and shifts in tandem with perceived effort levels: when a partner’s contributions improve or remain high, that partner is more likely to be rewarded; when a previously high contributor drops markedly, they are less likely to receive rewards and may eventually be punished. The player does not systematically reward every high contributor in every opportunity, so their rewarding is contingent and somewhat strategic rather than automatic. They also occasionally reward partners who are not the absolute top contributor but are clearly above the worst performer; this implies that they recognize gradations of effort and offer positive reinforcement not only to perfect cooperators but also to solid contributors. Overall, their reward behavior is consistent with “selective positive reinforcement”: they spend on reward to mark and encourage high or improving contribution, using it as a softer counterpart to their harsher punishment regime.

This player is an active and persistent rewarder, regularly spending part of their payoff to reward others. They do not treat reward as a rare or emergency tool; instead, they integrate it as a standing part of their play, repeatedly incurring personal cost to do so. They appear motivated primarily by two linked aims: reinforcing cooperation and acknowledging cooperative partners. When the group is uniformly cooperative, they still spend to reward, signaling approval and attempting to stabilize the high-cooperation pattern. When one partner deviates by not contributing, they do not retaliate in the reward stage; instead, they continue a reward pattern that favors cooperators. Their response to deviation is selective encouragement of the cooperative peer, not punishment or withdrawal of cooperation. They are willing to spend on reward even when the immediate economic return is poor or when their own payoff is already relatively low due to others’ free riding. This suggests they value sustaining norms of mutual support and “doing the right thing” over short-run material self-interest. At times, they increase their reward spending beyond their usual level, indicating a readiness to escalate positive reinforcement when cooperation is strong or when they may be trying to consolidate a cooperative equilibrium. They do not appear to use reward purely as a tit-for-tat reciprocity device. They continue to reward even in periods when they themselves receive little or no reward, which implies that their willingness to reward is not strictly contingent on being rewarded. However, there is some responsiveness: when they are rewarded, they maintain or intensify their own rewarding, consistent with a reciprocal, gratitude-like tendency layered on top of their baseline prosocial norm. Overall, their reward “type” is: consistently prosocial positive-reinforcer, using rewards to support and stabilize cooperation, tolerant of exploitation, and only weakly conditional on personal benefit or reciprocal treatment.

This player is an active, selectively generous rewarder who uses rewards as a normative and relational tool rather than a pure payoff-maximizing instrument. They repeatedly incur personal cost to reward others, even when doing so cannot rationally increase their own material payoff given the one-to-one reward cost and their already maximal contributions. This reflects a willingness to sacrifice private earnings to acknowledge and encourage prosocial behavior. They do not reward mechanically every round; instead, their reward spending fluctuates. The pattern suggests they are responsive to perceived cooperation levels and to the broader cooperative climate. When facing a clear defector alongside a consistent cooperator, they continue to cooperate and still spend on rewards, which strongly implies that their rewards target cooperators rather than defectors. While the interface hides identities of rewarders and who receives what, the player persists in rewarding, indicating they may view rewards as generalized support for “good group members” rather than as explicit bilateral trades. Their use of reward appears to serve several roles: - **Positive reinforcement of norms:** They behave as if rewarding is a way to uphold a “full contribution” standard and to sustain the cooperative equilibrium, even though the direct financial return is weak or negative. - **Gratitude and approval signaling:** They spend on rewards when others also contribute fully, suggesting they want to express approval and possibly gratitude for shared cooperation, independent of strict instrumental benefit. - **Resilience to exploitation:** Even after observing a peer free-ride, they do not turn off rewards or contributions; instead, they continue to participate in the reward system. This points to a forgiving or resilient personality that does not quickly retaliate or withdraw positive engagement. Strategically, they appear to treat rewards as a long-run investment in group cohesion rather than as a calculated, short-term optimization tool. They accept lower own payoffs to maintain a cooperative, supportive atmosphere, aligning with a “prosocial, norm-enforcing cooperator” type who endorses both high contribution and positive reinforcement, and who is reluctant to punish or disengage even when others underperform.

The player never spends any coins on rewarding others, despite consistently observing that both partners contribute fully and despite repeatedly receiving reward from them. This is a robust, stable pattern: their willingness to reward is effectively zero. The absence of any rewarding, even when they themselves are contributing fully, suggests that they do not treat the reward mechanism as a tool for strategic reinforcement or gratitude signaling. Several interpretations are consistent with this behavior: - They behave as a “pure contribution-based cooperator”: they support the public good directly but do not engage in second-order cooperation (rewarding others for cooperating). Their notion of fairness or responsibility seems to stop at contributing their own share, not at actively subsidizing or recognizing others’ contributions. - They may reason that reward is individually inefficient: every reward coin costs them exactly one coin and gives the recipient exactly one coin, so there is no social multiplier. From a payoff perspective, rewarding is neutral at the group level and strictly costly to the rewarder; a payoff-sensitive player might therefore choose to never use it, even while contributing to the public good itself. - They do not reciprocate received rewards in kind. They continue to accept rewards but never mirror them. This indicates low concern for “direct reciprocity” via explicit reward channels, even though they might reciprocate indirectly by continuing to contribute fully to the public pool. The triggers for rewarding appear absent: neither high partner cooperation nor the experience of being rewarded is sufficient to induce them to give rewards. There is no evidence that they experiment with reward early, learn about it, and stop using it; rather, they simply never engage with it. Strategically, the player seems to rely entirely on contribution choices, not on reward, as their means of sustaining cooperation. They implicitly “free-ride” on others’ second-order prosociality: they benefit from others rewarding them for cooperation but do not pay into that reward system themselves. This positions them as a first-order cooperator but a second-order free-rider in environments where rewarding is available but not mandatory.

The player shows a generally low propensity to use the reward mechanism. They rarely spend their own coins to reward others, and when they do, the spending is modest and not sustained. This suggests that, although they are willing to cooperate through contributions, they are reluctant to incur additional personal costs to actively reinforce others’ good behavior via explicit rewards. Because reward and cost are one-to-one in this setting, the immediate material incentive to reward is neutral, so any use of reward reveals social preferences or strategic thinking. Their sparse usage implies either a weak taste for “positive reciprocity” or uncertainty about the effectiveness of reward in this environment where rewarders are anonymous. There is no clear pattern of systematically rewarding the highest contributors, consistently rewarding specific cooperative peers, or rewarding in response to having received rewards themselves; any such patterns, if present, are weak and not clearly inferable (low confidence). When they do choose to reward, it likely reflects one of two motives (which cannot be disentangled from the data): (a) a situational attempt to encourage already-high contributions when the group is cooperating well, or (b) occasional gratitude or norm signaling toward cooperators. However, the absence of consistent reward use even in highly cooperative phases indicates that they do not rely on the reward option as a central strategic tool. Instead, they primarily support cooperation through their own high contributions and seem to treat rewarding as optional and secondary. They do not appear to use rewards in a sophisticated strategic way to shape others’ expectations over time (e.g., no clear “I will reward you if you keep contributing” regime). Their overall profile is best described as a high contributor who depends on their own cooperative example and on group norms, rather than on active, costly reinforcement through the reward mechanism.

This player is effectively a non-rewarder. Across the interaction, they never spend their own coins to reward anyone, even when surrounded by consistently high contributors and even after receiving rewards themselves. Their non-use of the reward mechanism is not easily explained by a belief that reward is financially inefficient, since here the cost and benefit are matched one-for-one. A purely monetary maximizer would be indifferent, and someone interested in shaping others’ behavior might try using reward at least once. Their consistent abstention indicates either that they discount the impact of reward on future behavior, that they view rewarding as outside their role or responsibility, or that they prefer to free-ride on whatever motivational effects others’ rewards provide. Receiving rewards does not change their stance: after being rewarded, they do not reciprocate by rewarding back or by rewarding others more broadly. This suggests that gratitude signaling via the explicit reward channel is not part of their behavioral repertoire. If they “thank” others at all, they do so only implicitly through continued contributions, not by spending to transfer coins. They also do not attempt strategic reinforcement of specific partners. Even when there are clearly high and low contributors, they never target the high contributors with reward to stabilize cooperation or to encourage particular individuals. This implies either that they see the group as a homogeneous mass (not worth differentiating), or that they prefer to let the contribution and redistribution mechanism stand on its own without adding another layer of incentives. Taken together, their type on the reward dimension is “passive beneficiary of others’ incentive efforts.” They accept rewards when given but never initiate them, they do not use the mechanism to shape norms or build alliances, and they appear to rely solely on their contribution choices (not costly rewards) to express their cooperative stance or respond to group behavior.

The player is an unusually heavy and active user of the reward mechanism, with a consistent willingness to spend on rewarding others even when they themselves contribute very little. This indicates a strong preference for using rewards as the main channel of “doing their part” and as a lever to shape group behavior. They repeatedly sacrifice a substantial fraction of their potential payoff to grant rewards. This willingness persists in both situations where they contribute nothing and in those rare cases where they contribute more, suggesting that their reward behavior is not tightly coupled to their own giving level. Instead, rewards appear driven by observed contributions of others: they are more inclined to reward when others are clearly cooperative and maintain relatively high contributions. The pattern is consistent with a “reward-focused free rider”: someone who wants to see and support cooperation in others without personally bearing much of the contribution cost. Their rewards are likely targeted at high or reliably cooperative contributors rather than low ones, although the exact targeting is unknown. The timing of their reward bursts tends to align with environments where many others are giving near the maximum, which supports the interpretation that they are reinforcing high-contribution norms rather than trying to lift up defectors. There is no evidence that they strategically withhold rewards to punish low contributors; instead, they seem to emphasize positive reinforcement over negative sanctioning (and punishment is not available in this game). They also sometimes reward in periods where their own immediate monetary interest would be better served by not spending. This implies that their utility is not purely monetary; they likely derive satisfaction from encouraging and “thanking” cooperators, or they believe that maintaining a cooperative climate via rewards will pay off indirectly in future rounds. Because they cannot see remaining rounds, these future benefits are uncertain, suggesting either a partially social-motivated preference (valuing others’ outcomes or norms) or a heuristic belief that “rewarding cooperators keeps the system working” even without precise horizon calculations. Their response to being rewarded themselves appears muted: occasional rewards directed at them do not noticeably change their long-run tendency to free-ride in contributions, nor do they obviously increase or decrease their own use of the reward tool. This suggests that, for them, receiving rewards is not a key driver of their own cooperative or rewarding behavior; they are more of a “one-way supporter” than a conditional reciprocity type. In new PGG environments, this player type is best described as a reward-oriented, norm-supporting free rider: they stay cheap on contributions, but spend actively to reward perceived cooperators, using the reward mechanism as their primary way to influence group behavior and to express approval, rather than aligning their own contributions with the cooperative standard they promote.

This player uses rewards selectively and sparingly, but clearly as a social and strategic tool rather than randomly. They do not attempt to maximize their own income through rewards (which are payoff-neutral here) and also do not use them mechanically every round. Instead, they deploy rewards in specific contexts that suggest three overlapping motives: (1) reinforcing high cooperation during or after strong group performance, (2) reciprocating when they themselves are rewarded, and (3) supporting the cooperative majority rather than punishing or targeting low contributors. They never use rewards in a negative or retaliatory sense; all use is implicitly prosocial. Their pattern fits a “targeted positive reciprocity” style: when cooperation across the group is high or when they experience being rewarded, they are more likely to spend coins to reward others in subsequent or same-phase situations. They show no evidence of trying to bribe under-contributors into better behavior; instead, they appear to focus on rewarding those who already cooperate at high levels, consistent with a preference for recognizing and stabilizing cooperative norms rather than converting defectors. Their willingness to spend on rewards is moderate. They sometimes invest a nontrivial portion of their payoff into rewarding others, and at other times they refrain entirely, even when others have cooperated well. This suggests that rewards are not governed by a rigid rule (e.g., “always reward high contributors”) but are contingent on their current perception of the group dynamic. When cooperation is already robust and they may feel especially supported (e.g., after receiving multiple rewards), they become more generous with rewards, potentially as a form of gratitude signaling or mutual recognition. When cooperation levels dip or the environment feels more fragile, they appear more conservative with rewards, possibly conserving resources or awaiting clearer evidence of stable cooperation. They do not show systematic tit-for-tat patterns at the individual level, because reward sources are anonymous. However, they clearly respond at the aggregate level: after phases in which they receive more reward, they become more willing to pay to reward others, indicating a generalized reciprocity pattern (“someone was nice to me; I will be nice to the group”) rather than direct reciprocity. Overall, this player is a high cooper­ator who uses rewards as a reinforcing and expressive device—encouraging and acknowledging cooperation—rather than as a precise strategic lever or a way to manage defectors.

This player is extremely reluctant to spend on rewarding others. Across the game, they almost never incur the cost to reward, despite often being a high contributor in groups that also contain other high contributors. Their default stance is to keep all remaining coins rather than use them to reinforce cooperation. When they do spend on rewards, it happens very rarely and at a low level relative to the total number of opportunities, suggesting a strong bias toward self-payoff preservation once the contribution stage is complete. Their sparse reward use does not clearly map onto consistent observable triggers such as a sharp increase in others’ cooperation or a clear “all cooperate” group configuration. Because rewards are anonymous and symmetric in cost and benefit, reward usage here is not strategically efficient in a narrow sense; the fact that the player almost always avoids it suggests either a calculation that rewards add little marginal benefit to group behavior, or a preference structure where contributing to the public good is morally salient but engaging in costly interpersonal reward is not. There is weak evidence that they sometimes experiment with or respond to the reward institution when they themselves receive substantial rewards from others: on a small number of occasions they spend a modest amount on rewarding after having been rewarded relatively generously. However, this relation is low-confidence: the timing is sparse, and the data do not show a robust pattern of tit-for-tat gratitude or systematic reciprocity. Overall, they do not appear to use reward as a strategic lever to shape others’ future contributions or to send strong social signals. Their social reinforcement style is thus “cooperate-by-default, reward-rarely.” Incentive-wise, they seem to separate the two stages: in contribution, they accept personal sacrifice to increase the public fund; in reward, they behave closer to a self-interested type, keeping their payoff instead of further investing in others. This asymmetry suggests that their core cooperative identity is expressed through direct contributions to the shared project, not through targeted, costly interpersonal incentives. Their use of rewards is best characterized as minimal, occasional, and non-systematic, with no clear conditional rule detectable from the transcript.

This player is a selective, norm-reinforcing rewarder who uses the reward option sparsely but meaningfully. For many rounds they either do not reward at all or spend only a token amount, despite consistently high contributions from others and receiving some rewards themselves. This indicates that they do not treat rewarding as an automatic reciprocity rule and do not use it as a constant “gratitude tax” in response to every instance of high cooperation. When they do invest heavily in rewards, it coincides with environments where most peers contribute close to their own high standard and at least one player persistently gives very little. In those situations, they spend a substantial share of their payoff to reward, despite the reward technology being individually cost-neutral. This pattern suggests they treat reward as a way to strengthen a cooperative norm among high contributors (and possibly to counterbalance the presence of a visible low contributor), rather than as a self-interested investment. They appear motivated to recognize and reinforce “solidarity” contributors rather than to micromanage every deviation. They also occasionally make small, isolated reward expenditures in otherwise normal rounds. Those seem consistent with targeted recognition of specific cooperative peers or testing how the mechanism works, not with a systematic strategy of buying influence. They do not escalate reward use when others lower their contributions, nor do they appear to punish low contributors via non-reward (since non-reward is their default). Overall, they use reward as a discretionary tool to encourage and signal approval of high cooperation when the group is mostly aligned with their strong-cooperation norm, rather than as a constant or tit-for-tat strategy. Their willingness to spend notably on rewards while already contributing the maximum suggests a prosocial, norm-focused personality that values supporting high contributors even at a substantial personal cost.

This player is a “selective, low-intensity rewarder” who uses rewards sparingly as a social reinforcement tool rather than as a main instrument of influence. For substantial stretches, they refrain from rewarding at all, even when they themselves are highly cooperative. This indicates that they do not see rewarding as necessary for routine reciprocity or as a strict obligation for every instance of high contribution. When they do spend on rewards, it tends to coincide with environments where group cooperation is already strong or improving, not when it is collapsing. This suggests they use rewards to acknowledge and gently support an existing cooperative norm rather than to build one from scratch. Their reward behavior is more confirmatory than corrective: they appear to “nod” to good behavior with occasional spending but do not try to actively rescue low-contribution environments by investing heavily in rewards. They never escalate to frequent or large reward expenditures, showing a cautious attitude toward the cost of rewarding. They seem attentive to their own payoff: they are willing to sacrifice some payoff to encourage or recognize others, but they keep that sacrifice bounded and intermittent. This points to a balanced orientation: pro-social but not self-sacrificing to an extreme when it comes specifically to rewarding. The triggers for rewarding appear to be: (a) seeing many others contribute at high levels, and/or (b) experiencing a generally cooperative, stable group context. They do not appear to reward in direct proportion to others’ exact contributions; instead, they respond to the overall climate of cooperation. They also do not systematically mirror rewards they receive: being rewarded does not reliably convert into them rewarding more in the immediately surrounding periods. This weak reciprocity in the reward channel suggests they treat incoming rewards more as a pleasant by-product than as part of a tit-for-tat contract. Strategically, they seem to treat rewards as a supplementary, low-frequency signal layered on top of their main strategy of consistently high contributions. They rely much more on their own cooperative example than on the reward technology to shape others’ behavior. Their gratitude signaling is present but muted: they sometimes spend to acknowledge a cooperative environment, but they are not a strong “reward evangelist,” and they do not appear to aim at identifying or cultivating specific partners, especially given that identities of rewarders are not observable. Overall, this player is best characterized as a robust cooperator who uses rewards sparingly and selectively to support cooperation, but who fundamentally relies on their own high contributions and tolerance for occasional free-riding rather than on intensive incentive engineering via the reward mechanism.

They almost never spend on reward and clearly treat the reward mechanism as economically inefficient. For most of the game they spend nothing, even when others are handing out substantial rewards. This indicates that they prioritize maximizing their own payoff within a cooperative framework and do not see costly rewards as necessary to sustain cooperation in a high-performing group. On a small number of occasions they do spend minimally on reward, but these instances are rare and modest; there is no clear pattern of targeted strategic reinforcement (for example, no clear reactionary rewarding of former defectors or heavy contributors). Because rewarders are anonymous, their sparse spending suggests they are not trying to cultivate an individual reputation but may occasionally reward out of generalized goodwill or a sense of fairness when everyone is cooperating. They accept rewards from others without comment and never reciprocate in a visible, systematic way. Overall, they are a “cheap” cooperator on the reward dimension: strongly cooperative in contributions but highly reluctant to incur personal costs to further reward others when cooperation is already high.

They are willing but selective about using costly rewards. They do not simply avoid the mechanism; instead, they use it intermittently, with small to moderate spending bursts at a few points. The pattern suggests they use rewards primarily as a way to reinforce high cooperation and maintain group morale rather than as a systematic strategic tool. There is no evidence they target low contributors with negative signaling via non‑reward; instead, they continue to reward in an environment where almost everyone is already fully contributing. They accept personal payoff losses from rewarding, indicating a pro‑social orientation and a willingness to sacrifice to acknowledge good behavior. At the same time, they are not as extreme as the most generous rewarders in the group: they do not continually empty their own pocket into the reward mechanism. This indicates a balance between altruism and self‑interest; they value the group outcome and social recognition but still care about preserving a reasonable payoff. They do not explicitly discuss how they choose recipients, and the anonymity of rewarders prevents precise inference. Given their overall style, it is plausible (but low confidence) that they reward either broadly (small amounts to many) or toward especially cooperative/norm‑supportive others, as a way of “joining in” when others start gifting. They do not appear to use rewards tactically to shape specific individuals’ future behavior; rather, they use them as occasional tokens of appreciation inside an already cooperative equilibrium.

They never spend coins on rewards at any point, despite being in a highly cooperative, norm-following group where many others do frequently reward. Their own reasoning is not explicitly verbalized, but earlier in the game they are present during others’ discussions about reward efficiency (noting the 2:1 cost structure), and they later persist in not rewarding even when rewards become common and directed broadly among cooperators. This pattern suggests that they treat the reward mechanism as economically inefficient or strategically unnecessary in a group that already cooperates at maximum. They do not appear to use rewards as a tool for gratitude, reciprocity, or reputation-building; instead, they maximize their own payoffs while relying on the general cooperative norm to sustain contributions. They also do not attempt to use rewards to discipline or rehabilitate the brief free-riders; their approach is strictly non-interventionist with respect to others’ payoffs.

They are highly conservative with rewards. For most of the game they give no rewards, even when others begin gifting coins relatively freely. They explicitly express skepticism about spending coins at an unfavorable exchange rate, and they generally appear to treat the reward mechanism as economically inefficient rather than as a strategic tool to sustain cooperation, at least early on. Later, they do eventually spend a small amount on rewards once, suggesting they are not ideologically opposed to rewarding but require a strong justification—likely a combination of established high cooperation and a desire to reciprocate general generosity. Receiving rewards does not lead them into mirroring behavior; they continue mostly not to reward even when repeatedly gifted small amounts. Their rare act of rewarding suggests a threshold-based pattern: they reward only when the cooperative equilibrium feels very stable and rewarding feels more like a gesture than an instrument of control. Overall, they behave like a cost-focused, low-frequency rewarder who uses rewards sparingly and not as a main instrument for shaping others’ behavior.

They never spend any of their own coins on rewards at any point. This is robust despite multiple opportunities, visible examples of others rewarding generously, and a cooperative environment where everyone contributes fully most of the time. Their communication suggests a cost–benefit calculation: they explicitly note that rewards are relatively inefficient and imply that it is probably not “worth it” in general, except perhaps in rare cases where someone’s behavior is exceptionally praiseworthy. However, they never encounter or at least never act on such a threshold case in this game. This pattern indicates a type who separates contribution norms from reward norms: they are willing to incur the cost of contributing for the public good (because the multiplication factor makes it efficient and norm-consistent), but they are not willing to incur an individually net-costly expenditure for second-order rewarding, even when others do. Their stance is mildly appreciative of reward use in principle but effectively “reward-frugal” and payoff-protective in practice.

For much of the game, they do not spend on rewards at all and explicitly frame the reward technology as inefficient: they understand that each unit of reward costs them more than it gives the recipient. This indicates they attend to payoff structure and initially lean toward not using costly incentives when they seem individually suboptimal. However, later they spend substantial amounts on rewards in a few rounds, despite no obvious change in others’ contribution behavior. Because individual reward targets are not observable, their exact pattern is unknown, but the timing suggests they may be using rewards as a form of end‑game generosity or gratitude toward the group rather than as a strategic lever to change behavior. Their own reward receipt is inconsistent across rounds, yet they still eventually give sizeable rewards, which implies they are not strictly reciprocity‑driven. Overall, they are cautious and payoff‑sensitive about reward use early, but willing to incur significant costs later, likely to express appreciation or reinforce a positive group experience rather than to optimize earnings.

They never spend any coins on rewards, even though others occasionally reward them. Their payoff line consistently shows zero spent on rewarding others, while they sometimes receive small rewards from the rest of the group. This pattern suggests they recognize and accept others’ prosocial rewarding but do not reciprocate via the costly reward mechanism. Given their consistent full cooperation, this is unlikely to be due to selfish noncooperativeness; instead, they likely view contributions themselves as sufficient prosocial behavior and judge the reward technology as either inefficient or unnecessary. There is no evidence of targeted, strategic reinforcement or gratitude signaling via rewards. Their cooperative strategy is expressed solely through contributions rather than secondary incentive tools.

They never spend any of their own coins on rewards in any round, despite the presence of a functioning reward mechanism and substantial reward activity by several peers. They nevertheless receive rewards from others multiple times, indicating that they benefit from the system but choose not to reciprocate materially. This pattern suggests that, in this environment, they view the reward mechanism as inefficient or not worth the cost relative to its impact on payoffs, and they are not motivated to use it for reciprocity or gratitude signaling. Their earlier statements focus solely on maximizing bonuses through contributions, never mentioning rewards as a tool for coordination or enforcement. They also do not adjust contribution behavior in response to gaining or not gaining rewards, further indicating that rewards are irrelevant to their strategy. Their “type” therefore is a non‑rewarding, efficiency‑driven cooperator who prefers to conserve private payoff rather than invest in costly positive sanctions, even when others are generous.

They never spend anything on rewards, despite repeated opportunities and despite others sometimes using rewards generously, including “gift‑splurges” by certain players. Their own behavior is 100% cooperative but 0% rewarding. This indicates a clear reluctance to incur personal costs solely to transfer payoff to others, even when those others are fully cooperative and when norm‑reinforcing rewards are common in the group. The behavior is consistent with a payoff‑conserving or efficiency‑minded stance: since the reward mechanism has a net negative efficiency effect (it costs more to give than the recipient gains), they appear to treat it as generally not worth using. They also do not use rewards symbolically (e.g., to thank high contributors), even though they sometimes receive small rewards from others. Overall, they rely on contribution behavior itself, not costly prosocial side‑payments, as their main cooperative signal. Their use of the reward mechanism appears purely instrumental and they choose not to deploy it at all.

They never spend any coins on rewards, despite repeated opportunities and visible examples of others using rewards to thank or encourage cooperators. This suggests a strong reluctance to incur personal costs for second‑order cooperation in this environment. They appear to treat reward as individually irrational given the unfavorable exchange rate, and they do not engage in symbolic or “cheap” rewarding even in final rounds, despite the anonymity of rewarders and a highly cooperative group. Receiving rewards from others in several rounds does not induce any reciprocation or reward activity from them. Their pattern fits a “first‑order cooperator, second‑order free‑rider”: they contribute fully to the public good but free‑ride on others’ willingness to reward and sustain norms. If they have a strategy about rewards, it is to ignore the mechanism as an inefficient use of resources.

They never spend coins to reward anyone, despite many opportunities and although they themselves receive small amounts of reward from others. Their behavior is thus strongly “reward-frugal” or non-rewarding, even in a highly cooperative context. This suggests that, in their implicit cost–benefit calculation, the 2-for-1 structure makes rewarding not worth it. They appear to rely instead on cheap-talk communication and a shared cooperative norm to sustain high contributions, rather than using material reward as a strategic tool. There is no evidence that being rewarded changes their own contribution or reward behavior: they receive occasional tokens of appreciation yet do not reciprocate materially. This points to a clear separation in their mind between cooperation (which they embrace) and costly rewarding (which they avoid). Their stance looks more efficiency-driven than gratitude-driven: they value group benefit via contributions but see costly transfers as wasteful under these parameters. There is no sign of targeted “teaching” via rewards, nor of rewarding defectors or high contributors selectively; the option is simply unused.

They rarely use the reward mechanism and, when they do, they spend only small amounts relative to what is possible. Given that they always see near‑universal cooperation and that the reward technology is inefficient, they appear reluctant to sacrifice their own payoff to redistribute coins. Their occasional rewards occur in already highly cooperative contexts, suggesting their motivation is weak, nonstrategic prosociality rather than a calculated attempt to shape behavior. They show no clear pattern of systematically reinforcing former defectors or targeting specific high contributors; in a group where almost everyone cooperates, their sparse rewarding is best interpreted as periodic, low‑cost appreciation rather than a structured incentive scheme. They do not respond in kind to receiving rewards; when they are rewarded several times, their own reward behavior does not visibly escalate, indicating that gratitude signaling via reward is not central to their strategy. They are thus a “cooperative but reward‑frugal” type: they value group cooperation but treat the costly reward mechanism as largely unnecessary.

They are a relatively generous, sometimes heavy, reward-giver in a context where rewards are individually costly and socially inefficient. They repeatedly spend substantial amounts on rewards in some periods, even when no one has defected and contributions are unanimous. This suggests they use rewards primarily as social reinforcement and reciprocity rather than narrow profit maximization. They appear willing to incur significant personal cost to “thank” or encourage others, sometimes concentrating rewards enough to noticeably lower their own payoff. There is no evidence that they target rewards only toward those who previously rewarded them; they receive some rewards from others but also give out rewards in rounds where their own incoming rewards are zero, implying a more general prosocial or group-affirming motive. They do not obviously use rewards as punishment substitutes (for example, by excluding defectors while rewarding everyone else); when the group is cooperative, their rewards are inclusive. Over time, they oscillate between heavy giving and no giving, which may indicate experimentation with how much rewarding makes sense, but they never develop a purely self-interested “no reward” stance.

They are initially skeptical about the value of rewarding when it is materially inefficient, explicitly questioning whether it is “worth” spending coins. Their reward behavior is low to moderate overall and non-punitive: they never use rewards in a retaliatory or exclusionary way. When they do spend on rewards, they do so in rounds where everyone is fully cooperating and there have been no recent defections, suggesting that their main trigger is positive reciprocity or appreciation rather than discipline. They sometimes engage in broad, near-universal rewarding bursts, even at substantial personal cost, which appears less strategic and more expressive (e.g., signaling gratitude or solidarity). Their pattern is not tightly calibrated to marginal incentives; they sometimes abstain entirely despite universal cooperation, and at other times they give more generously, which indicates that mood, group harmony, or a desire to “share the surplus” occasionally override strict payoff-maximization. They do not systematically reward specific high or low earners; their targeting pattern is unknown, but nothing suggests selective favoritism or sanctions.

They never spend any of their own coins on rewards, even though they sometimes receive rewards from others. This indicates that they are not willing to incur personal cost to reinforce good behavior under these parameters. Given that the reward technology is inefficient (cost to giver exceeds gain to receiver), their choice is consistent with a payoff-maximizing view that such rewards are not worthwhile. There is no evidence that they attempt to use rewards to shape others’ behavior, express gratitude, or build alliances. Their cooperative behavior is entirely driven through contributions and group norms, not through costly interpersonal incentives.

They do not spend any resources on rewarding others at any point, despite having multiple salient high contributors available to reward and despite themselves having experienced being rewarded. This indicates a low intrinsic willingness to use costly reward as a social tool. They do not appear to use reward strategically to encourage future cooperation, nor to express gratitude, nor to align with high cooperators. The absence of reward behavior, combined with high sensitivity to personal payoff, suggests that they perceive reward as a pure cost with no sufficiently compelling future benefits, at least in the time horizon they consider. They do not engage in selective or symbolic low-cost rewarding either, implying that even small, expressive reciprocity is not a priority. The player therefore behaves as a non-rewarder type: they accept rewards from others but do not reciprocate or propagate reward, even when doing so might support a cooperative norm.

They never spend any resources to reward others, despite the availability and use of this mechanism by multiple peers. Their choice remains unchanged across distinct environments of contribution and reward behavior by others. This indicates a strict separation between their willingness to contribute to the public good and their willingness to engage in targeted positive reciprocity. They do not use rewards to encourage high contributors, to reciprocate reward, or to build cooperative reputations. This could reflect one or more of: a preference to keep payoff resources once contributed decisions are made, a belief that the reward mechanism is inefficient or irrelevant, low engagement with strategic signaling, or simply indifference to the social leverage that rewards could provide. Overall, they are a pure contributor and a non-rewarder.

This player never uses the reward mechanism, despite it being cheap and visible. They do not reward even very high contributors, and they do not appear to use reward strategically to steer behavior or build alliances. This indicates a strong reluctance to spend on others once contributions are decided. Either they undervalue social reinforcement as a tool, or they treat reward as purely costly without sufficient expected return. There is no evidence of gratitude signaling or positive reciprocity via rewards. They also do not appear to use reward to differentiate between high and low contributors; they simply abstain. Their default stance is to keep all residual payoff rather than invest in social incentives.

This player is willing to spend on rewards but does so selectively and at a moderate level rather than indiscriminately. They clearly recognize and use the reward mechanism, including directing some reward to themselves, which indicates attention to both social signaling and personal payoff. Their own self-reward shows they are not purely altruistic; they are willing to use available tools to boost their own outcome when it does not require social approval. At the same time, they also allocate reward to others, indicating some desire to acknowledge or reinforce behavior they view as positive. The pattern of reward is not extensive relative to their high level of contribution, suggesting that reward is a secondary tool rather than their primary cooperative strategy. They do not engage in reward-based retaliation (since punishment is unavailable), and there is no sign that they spend reward resources to counter free riders indirectly (e.g., by over-rewarding cooperators to offset defectors). Their reward behavior looks more like targeted appreciation and mild strategic self-gain than a systematic attempt to reshape group incentives.

The player never uses the reward mechanism, even when others around them spend resources to reward high contributors. This indicates a strong reluctance to incur any personal cost to shape others’ behavior or to express approval. Rewarding is not part of their strategic toolkit; they either do not value the potential future benefits of reinforcing cooperation or do not believe their individual rewards would matter enough to justify the cost. They also do not appear to use reward as a signal of gratitude or alliance formation. High contributors and active rewarders receive no reinforcement from this player. This suggests that social signaling, reputation-building, and norm-enforcement via positive incentives are low priorities. Their approach to reward is strictly self-regarding: if it costs them, they abstain. There is no observable pattern of selective or strategic reward (for instance, rewarding particularly high contributors or those who might reciprocate). As such, their “trigger” for rewarding appears to be effectively absent; they act as a pure non-rewarder.

They never use the reward mechanism, even when surrounded by many high contributors and visible reward activity by others. This indicates a strong reluctance to incur any personal cost for social reinforcement, even for clearly cooperative peers. They do not appear to use reward strategically to influence future behavior, build goodwill, or reciprocate group norms. Their default stance is to preserve their own payoff rather than trade off even a small amount to shape the social environment. This points to a narrow, short-term payoff focus and a low propensity for altruistic or strategic rewarding. Whether this reflects disbelief in the effectiveness of rewards, indifference to others’ welfare, or simple inattention is unknown, but the behavioral pattern is strictly non-rewarding.

This player does not spend any resources on rewarding others, despite having the opportunity and seeing others make both high and low contributions, and despite themselves being a high contributor. They receive rewards from others but do not reciprocate or use reward to reinforce either cooperative partners or norm violators (via differential rewarding). This suggests a general reluctance to engage with the reward mechanism, rather than a targeted or strategic use of it. Their non-use of reward could stem from several possible motives, which cannot be distinguished with high confidence: a preference to avoid additional strategic complexity; a belief that reward is inefficient or not worth the cost; or a focus on contributing directly to the public good rather than shaping others’ behavior. They appear not to use reward to signal gratitude, build alliances, or encourage high contributors. Overall, they are a non-rewarder / abstainer from positive sanctions, even while being highly cooperative in contributions.

This player never uses the reward mechanism, even when others contribute substantially more or less than they do. They thus reveal a strong reluctance to spend personal resources to influence others’ behavior or to express approval. There is no sign of targeted reinforcement (e.g., no extra incentive given to consistently high cooperators) and no evidence of using reward strategically to build alliances or to shape future contributions. Their non-use of reward persists across different group contribution patterns, suggesting that their default stance is to preserve their own payoff rather than invest in social incentives. This might reflect a belief that the reward tool is inefficient, unnecessary in such a short or uncertain-horizon game, or unlikely to change behavior enough to justify the cost. It may also indicate low inclination toward expressive prosociality: they do not pay to “say thanks” or to signal moral approval. Overall, they behave as a self-protective cooperator: willing to contribute moderately, but not willing to pay extra to manage the social environment.

This player is generally cautious with reward but becomes selectively willing to spend when peers’ behaviors diverge more clearly. They do not reward by default and do not reward in proportion to others’ exact contribution levels; instead, they reward only in situations where they likely infer especially noteworthy cooperative behavior or possibly to encourage specific players. Their reward pattern is sparse and targeted, indicating that they view rewarding as a tool to shape behavior rather than as a routine expression of generosity. The fact that they are willing to incur a nontrivial personal cost to reward at all implies some weight on social preferences or norm reinforcement, but the low overall intensity suggests they continue to prioritize preserving their own payoff. They do not reward themselves (not possible here) and receive no reward from others, so their decision to reward is not a straightforward reciprocity response. They instead appear to use reward strategically to support others’ cooperation, albeit in a minimalist way, rather than as a broad signal of friendliness or gratitude.

The player is willing to spend a small, stable amount on rewards each time. Their reward expenditures remain low relative to their total resources, suggesting they view rewarding as secondary to contributing and are cautious about its cost. They do not escalate or disengage from rewarding despite changes in others’ behaviors and payoffs, which implies a weak but persistent inclination to reinforce cooperation. Because they reward even when they themselves are not being materially favored, their rewards likely track cooperative behavior (high contributors) or role-model players rather than pure reciprocity. They do not appear to use rewards aggressively as a strategic lever (e.g., large, targeted incentives); instead, they use minimal, symbolic or diffuse rewards. This pattern fits an “encouraging but frugal rewarder” who wants to signal approval without sacrificing much payoff.

This player does not use the reward mechanism at all, despite multiple others employing it and despite the group being generally cooperative. This points to a clear reluctance to incur personal costs purely to enforce or encourage norms. Their stance is best characterized as “non-rewarding cooperator”: they contribute to the public good but refrain from engaging in costly social sanctioning, positive or negative. They do not selectively reward high contributors who reward others, nor do they attempt to strategically reinforce cooperators to shape future behavior, even though the mechanism is salient and visibly used by several peers. This suggests that either they do not view rewarding as necessary or effective, or they prioritize conserving their own payoff over active norm enforcement. Their lack of reward also implies they are not using rewards to signal gratitude or to form alliances; any cooperative intention they have is expressed solely through contribution levels, not through second-order cooperation.

This player is selectively generous with rewards and uses them instrumentally to reinforce cooperative behavior. They never use reward to “buy” money for themselves; all reward spending is directed to others. When they do reward, they focus on individuals whose contributions are relatively high within the group, including at least one who is not at the very top, implying they care about recognizing meaningful cooperation rather than only extreme acts. They do not reward clear low contributors or defectors, even when those players earn high payoffs, showing that they treat reward as a conditional, merit-based tool rather than as an equalizing transfer. The timing and pattern of their reward use indicate they are trying to encourage and stabilize cooperative norms, not simply expressing personal gratitude or reciprocity. Overall, they are willing to sacrifice some private payoff to support cooperators but do so in a targeted manner rather than broadly.

The player uses rewards and is clearly willing to incur personal cost to reinforce others’ behavior, but only after they themselves have chosen to cooperate. They do not immediately reward high contributors when they are acting selfishly; instead, the use of reward emerges together with their own cooperative move. This suggests that their rewarding is part of an alignment with a cooperative mode rather than a standing principle to praise high contributors regardless of their own stance. They are selective rather than indiscriminately generous. They incur a nontrivial total cost to reward multiple others, indicating that they care enough about reinforcing certain partners or behaviors to sacrifice part of their own payoff. The selection of multiple recipients with high contributions suggests that their main trigger is observed cooperative effort rather than neediness or low payoffs. Strategically, this looks like an attempt to stabilize or encourage a cooperative norm: they reward contributors when they themselves have invested heavily, which can be read as “I’m in with you; I want you to keep doing this.” The use of reward in a low-MPCR environment (where contributions are not individually profitable) further suggests that they view reward as a social or normative tool rather than a strictly payoff-maximizing investment. They do not reward when they are free-riding, implying that they do not see reward as a cheap way to appear prosocial without real sacrifice; instead, they pair reward with genuine contribution. This pairing is consistent with a self-image (or strategy) of conditional cooperation: once they decide the setting merits cooperation, they both contribute and reward.

This player never uses the reward option, even when many others do and when there are clear high contributors present. They show a zero-reward pattern across opportunities, which indicates a strong reluctance to spend resources on rewarding, regardless of others’ contributions or how they themselves are treated. Their behavior suggests they do not use reward as a strategic tool to reinforce cooperation, even when that might credibly encourage pro-social partners. They also do not use reward as an expression of gratitude or as a social signal; they remain completely inactive in this mechanism. Possible motives (all low-confidence, as internal reasoning is unobserved): - They may prioritize conserving coins over influencing others. - They may view rewarding as inefficient or unnecessary given the short horizon. - They may be socially reserved or disinterested in forming explicit alliances via reward. What is clear from behavior alone is that they separate contribution from reward: they are willing to sacrifice to help the group via contributions, but not willing to spend any extra to differentially benefit individuals through the reward channel.

This player is selectively willing to incur costs to reward others, but only after observing an additional round of behavior. Initially they refrain from rewarding anyone despite clear high contributions by several others. Later, they begin to use rewards in a targeted way. Their rewards go to players who exhibit strong cooperative behavior, not to themselves or to clear free-riders, indicating that the trigger for rewarding is substantial contribution to the public good rather than reciprocity of being rewarded or simple equality. They use rewards sparsely and at low cost, which suggests they value the idea of reinforcing cooperation but keep that secondary to their own payoff. The pattern is consistent with strategic reinforcement rather than impulsive generosity: they appear to use rewards to acknowledge particularly cooperative behavior and possibly to signal approval and encourage such behavior, while avoiding broad or unconditional rewarding. This is a weak, selective prosocial-rewarder profile.

This player never uses the reward mechanism, even though several peers contribute highly and some peers reward others. They do not spend any coins to reinforce cooperative behavior, nor do they use reward as a strategic tool to shape future contributions. This suggests either that they see cooperation as a personal duty rather than something to be incentivized in others, or that they are strongly payoff-conserving once they have committed to full contribution. Given that they are themselves rewarded without reciprocating through the available mechanism, their use of material reward as a social signal is minimal or absent. Their strategy appears to be “cooperate fully, but do not engage in costly social engineering via rewards.”

They never spend any coins on rewards. This is true even though they frequently contribute at the maximum, sometimes while others do not, and even though the reward technology is individually beneficial in expectation (giving up 4 to give 5). This suggests one or more of the following motivations, with uncertain weighting: a preference for keeping their own payoffs; skepticism about the marginal effect of rewards in this context; inattention or low interest in that mechanism; or a normative view that cooperation itself is expected and does not merit extra payment. There is no sign that they use rewards strategically to encourage laggards or to strengthen bonds with other cooperators, despite having clear candidates to reward. The absence of any reward use, combined with their steady cooperation, indicates that they prioritize simple, norm-based contribution over more complex incentive engineering.

Despite being a non-contributor, this player spends on rewards regularly. They consistently pay the cost to give reward units to others, across many rounds. The pattern of recipients suggests they are focused on rewarding particular individuals rather than distributing rewards evenly to all cooperators. They do not use reward to universally reinforce contribution norms; instead, they appear to selectively reinforce a subset of players (e.g., specific cooperative or perhaps sympathetic others). Their willingness to spend on rewards while not contributing themselves points to a more nuanced payoff calculus: they may see reward as a cheaper or more controlled way to influence or acknowledge others while still exploiting the main public good. Since the marginal efficiency of reward is modestly above 1, they can slightly increase social welfare for chosen recipients at a cost to themselves, while still overall benefiting massively from others’ contributions to the PGG itself. They do not use reward strategically to redirect behavior toward themselves (e.g., they do not reward people only when they favor them in some way beyond contribution); instead, the reward seems to be loosely pro-social but targeted. There is no evidence they ever reward low contributors or use rewards to “bribe” others into tolerating their free riding. Overall, they are surprisingly willing to incur modest personal costs to give positive transfers, but they keep their primary strategic advantage—non-contribution—intact.

They never spend any of their own coins on rewards. This is consistent and appears deliberate rather than due to inattention: they explicitly state they are not interested in adjusting rewards, and they maintain that stance even when others use rewards actively. Their behavior suggests a belief that direct contributions and communication are sufficient, and that the reward mechanism is either unnecessary, inefficient, or not worth the cognitive/monetary cost. They do not seem to adopt a strategic reward policy (e.g., rewarding high contributors to reinforce behavior); instead, they abstain entirely. This abstention persists even when they themselves are rewarded, indicating they do not treat rewards as a reciprocal gift-exchange system. Their cooperative effort is expressed through maximal contribution and social coordination, not through the incentive mechanism.

The player never spends any coins on rewards across the game, despite many others using the reward mechanism. This holds both when cooperation levels are low and when nearly everyone is contributing fully, and persists even after they themselves receive rewards. Their behavior indicates a strong reluctance to incur personal cost for positive enforcement, even when it might benefit group cooperation or reciprocate kindness. They do not experiment with small or symbolic rewards, suggesting either that they do not see reward spending as worthwhile, or they prefer to keep all personal earnings. There is no evidence that they adjust reward behavior in response to others’ contributions, to group appeals about efficiency, or to being rewarded themselves. As a “type” in future games, they behave as a non-rewarder: they rely on others’ reward efforts but do not join them, effectively free-riding on the presence of a reward mechanism and on others’ willingness to use it.

They never spend any coins on rewards. This holds throughout, even when they benefit heavily from others’ cooperation and when reward is known to be slightly socially efficient (cost–benefit ratio above 1). For them, the reward mechanism is effectively ignored as a tool for norm enforcement or gratitude. The absence of rewarding persists even when they themselves receive rewards on multiple occasions. They neither reciprocate to benefactors nor use reward to reinforce high contributors. This rules out a strategy of selective or “cheap” reciprocity via reward. Instead, their behavior suggests a personally payoff-maximizing stance toward the reward mechanism: any cost, however slightly efficient socially, is avoided. This implies they do not actively invest in sustaining cooperation via institutional tools; they are willing to ride on others’ use of rewards but will not spend to maintain norm compliance. From a strategic standpoint, they act as a “reward free-rider.”

This player never spends any coins on rewards. Even when other players are clearly contributing fully and when some reward flows are present in the group, they refrain from incurring personal costs to reinforce cooperation. Their refusal to use the reward mechanism persists even after they themselves receive rewards, which rules out simple ignorance of the tool. This suggests they either view rewarding as not worth the personal cost, or they prioritize maintaining their own payoff over actively shaping others’ behavior. They do not appear to use rewards instrumentally (for example, to encourage waverers or to thank consistent cooperators). Their approach fits a “non-enforcer cooperator”: they contribute to the public good but leave the burden of costly norm enforcement to others.

They never use the reward mechanism, despite consistently high contributions and multiple opportunities to reinforce other cooperators. This is not due to lack of candidates: most players repeatedly contribute fully. Silence in the reward phase and zero spending suggest one or more of the following, none of which can be distinguished with certainty: they prioritize maximizing their own payoff over spending on others; they see the marginal benefit of reward as not worth the cost; they are uncertain or indifferent about how to use the mechanism; or they prefer to express approval socially rather than via costly incentives. What is clear is that they do not engage in strategic reward to shape others’ behavior, nor do they appear to “reciprocate” rewards materially even after being rewarded once. Their cooperative ethic does not translate into willingness to incur extra personal cost for peer reinforcement beyond contribution.

They never spend any coins on rewards in the entire game. This is consistent and stable despite clear opportunities and a technology that is mildly efficient. Their revealed preference is to keep all their own payoff rather than incur costs to positively reinforce cooperators or to strategically “buy” more future cooperation. This suggests either skepticism about the effectiveness of the reward mechanism, a strong concern for not losing additional coins on top of what they already sacrifice by cooperating, or low intrinsic motivation to reward others even when they appreciate their contributions verbally. They do not appear to use rewards instrumentally (e.g., to shape gorilla’s behavior) or symbolically (e.g., to thank high contributors), even after being rewarded themselves; they compartmentalize material and verbal responses, relying on chat for social signaling rather than the costly mechanism. Their strategy is thus “cooperate and talk, but do not pay to reward.”

They never spend any coins on rewards, despite being eligible every round and seeing others use the reward mechanism. This indicates a clear reluctance to incur personal cost for positive reinforcement, even when others are clearly cooperating and sometimes rewarding them. This is not due to ignorance of the mechanism (they verbally demonstrate understanding of contributions and total payoffs, and they can see reward flows in the public summaries). Instead, they seem to rely on communication and social norms to sustain cooperation, rather than costly reward. Their strategy is “contribute and encourage verbally” rather than “contribute and selectively subsidize.” This suggests either a payoff‑conscious limit on altruism (they will sacrifice via contributions but not via extra costs), skepticism that rewarding materially changes behavior, or a preference for non‑monetary social reinforcement (thanks, encouragement) over explicit reward spending. They never use reward instrumentally as a carrot for defectors or as a gratitude payment for allies.

They use rewards sparingly and in a targeted, pro‑social way. Most of the time they do not spend on rewards, suggesting they are cost‑conscious and do not view reward as their primary tool. When they do pay for reward, it is aligned with reinforcing cooperation or positive group behavior: they reward another high contributor and also once pay for reward in a round where they themselves do not contribute, likely as an attempt to offset their own selfish action or to support others’ contributions despite their own defection (interpretation is low‑confidence but consistent with the pattern). Their reward choices are not obviously retaliatory or manipulative; they do not use rewards to curry favor with the main defector or to build a narrow alliance. Instead, their sparse rewards look like occasional, principled acknowledgments of cooperative play. Overall, they see rewards as secondary to direct contribution and communication, and they deploy them only intermittently.

They almost never use the reward mechanism, despite being a high contributor surrounded by mostly high contributors. Their default is to spend nothing on rewards; only one round shows them spending on reward, and even then they do not establish any systematic pattern of rewarding cooperative peers. They receive rewards a couple of times from others, but do not reciprocate or build visible tit-for-tat reward exchanges. This strongly suggests that, relative to contribution, they see rewards as low-priority or inefficient, even though the tech is favorable. Their strategy appears to be to promote norms verbally and through their own contributions rather than by paying to reinforce others. They do not use reward as a tool to target the stubborn free-rider either, even when that player is clearly identified by the group; they simply ignore reward as a lever, indicating either that they view it as not worth the cost, or that they mentally compartmentalize “playing correctly” (contribute) from “meta-tools” (reward).

The player does use the reward mechanism, but infrequently and selectively. Most rounds they choose not to spend on rewards despite many others rewarding quite actively. When they do reward, they spend nontrivial amounts in concentrated bursts rather than distributing small amounts broadly, indicating that rewards are deliberate interventions, not routine “tips.” Because the log does not show exactly whom they reward, only that they sometimes devote resources to rewarding, the most we can say is: they are willing to incur personal cost to influence behavior, but they do so sparingly.
