Given that they themselves are a consistent high contributor, and that most reward activity in the group targets contributors, it is plausible (but not certain) that their rewards are directed toward encouraging cooperative or helpful players rather than to curry favor with defectors. Their late-game reward spending, despite cumulative payoff deficits relative to free-riders, suggests that any rewarding they do is more aligned with reinforcing group norms than with maximizing their own final total. Strategically, they treat reward as a supplementary, occasional tool rather than as a central lever for coordination or reciprocity.

This player frequently uses the reward mechanism and is willing to pay substantial costs to shape others’ behavior, even without being able to identify specific partners or see their end-of-round payoffs. They appear to view reward as a tool for both incentivizing cooperation and expressing approval or gratitude, though not in a strictly consistent or fully strategic way. They are more willing to spend on reward in periods where they themselves are either cooperating strongly or benefiting from others’ cooperation. They sometimes invest in reward even when they are the only high contributor, suggesting an attempt to “seed” or encourage a cooperative norm rather than simply reciprocating others’ current behavior. At other times they cooperate but do not reward, which implies that they distinguish between “I’ll do my part” and “I will pay extra to manage others’ incentives”; using reward is an additional, not automatic, layer. Their threshold for rewarding appears lower than for contributing: they pay for reward even in environments with low contributions or mixed behavior. This points to a willingness to experiment with social influence: they treat reward as a possible coordination device, despite not observing clear, immediate returns. They do not show evidence of using reward purely for short-term self-gain; in several instances they sacrifice profitable coins to reward others when they are already earning well by free-riding, which suggests some genuine concern with encouraging pro-social behavior or reciprocating perceived generosity (even though specific targets are unknown). Strategically, their reward use is somewhat noisy: they neither mirror others’ contributions in a one-to-one reinforcing way nor abandon reward altogether when it does not obviously pay off. Instead, they intermittently reinforce what they perceive as cooperative phases and occasionally try to spark cooperation by rewarding in otherwise low-cooperation environments. This marks them as a “hopeful incentivizer”: they are prepared to incur real costs to nudge the group toward higher cooperation, but their pattern is adaptive and bounded—when cooperation remains unstable or their own payoff is pressured, they scale back or stop rewarding in favor of preserving their own earnings.

Willingness to reward: The player never spends any of their own coins on rewards, despite multiple opportunities and despite the presence of clearly higher-contributing peers. Their revealed preference is to avoid any costly pro-social transfers. Triggers (or lack thereof): Even in situations where a peer’s contribution clearly increases the group fund and arguably benefits the player, they do not respond with rewards. This holds regardless of whether they themselves cooperated or defected in that round. Thus, neither gratitude for others’ cooperation nor guilt about free-riding translates into costly rewarding behavior. Strategic reinforcement: There is no behavioral evidence that the player attempts to use rewards as a strategic tool to reinforce cooperation. They behave as if the reward mechanism is irrelevant to their strategy. They free-ride on others’ rewarding behavior instead, accepting incoming rewards (when given by others) but never reciprocating with their own costly rewards. Gratitude signaling: Although the player receives rewards from others in several rounds, they do not mirror this with any outgoing reward behavior. This suggests: - Low priority on expressing gratitude via costly signals. - Willingness to accept benefits from others without engaging in reciprocal rewarding. Any internal sense of appreciation, if present, does not manifest in action. Beliefs about the reward system (inferred): The player likely views rewarding as individually unprofitable: the cost to them is salient, while the benefit (increased cooperation by others) is either doubted or discounted. Because they cannot observe others’ final payoffs and cannot identify who rewards whom, they may conclude that their own rewards would have limited marginal effect. This belief, combined with their payoff focus, yields a strictly non-rewarding stance. Personality/style in reward domain: In future PGGs with similar reward mechanics, this player: - Does not initiate rewarding behavior, even toward consistent cooperators. - Accepts rewards but treats them as windfalls rather than as part of a reciprocal exchange. - Relies on others, if any, to bear the cost of sustaining cooperation through rewards. They are best characterized as a “reward free-rider”: they exploit the presence of a reward system when others use it but never invest in it themselves.

The player never spends any coins on rewarding others, even when other group members contribute fully and sometimes reward them. This reveals an extremely low willingness to incur personal costs to encourage or acknowledge cooperative behavior. They show no observable gratitude via the reward channel: being rewarded does not lead them to reciprocate toward high contributors or toward likely rewarders. Their use of the reward mechanism is purely abstention: they allow others to bear both the contribution and reward costs, while they capture private benefits from public contributions and from incoming rewards. This positions them as a “second‑order free‑rider” as well: they free‑ride not only on contribution but also on the costly act of rewarding cooperation. There is no sign that they use rewards strategically to stabilize cooperation (for example, by selectively reinforcing consistent contributors). They do not attempt to shape others’ behavior through incentives, even in phases where the group is relatively cooperative. This suggests either that they view the reward technology as not personally profitable, or that they simply adopt a minimal‑effort, purely self‑regarding rule like “never pay for others.” Because they receive rewards from others yet never return them, it is plausible (though not certain) that they either (a) feel no social obligation to reciprocate, or (b) treat rewards as anonymous windfalls that do not call for response. In new PGG environments, this type is expected to withhold both contributions and rewards, regardless of the availability or generosity of reward mechanisms, and to rely entirely on others, if any, to provide both public goods and cooperative incentives.

This player is an **active, selective rewarder who uses rewards as a strategic and expressive tool rather than purely as payoff maximization**. They are clearly **willing to spend meaningful resources on rewarding others**, even when this is costly relative to their own round payoff. This willingness appears tied to peers’ cooperative behavior: high contributors are more likely to be rewarded, especially when the group outcome would otherwise be discouraging. The player uses reward as a way to **reinforce or encourage contribution** rather than distributing it indiscriminately. Reward decisions are **not purely self-interested** in a narrow sense. At times, they sacrifice immediate payoff to increase others’ earnings, even when there is no visible guarantee of future reciprocity and no visibility of who is rewarding whom. This implies they value **supporting or “thanking” cooperation** in its own right, and/or they hold a belief that rewarding cooperators can stabilize contributions over time even if responses are uncertain. However, their reward behavior is **not rigidly consistent**. They do not always reward when others cooperate, and they sometimes withhold rewards even in cooperative environments. This variability suggests that they are both experimenting with the impact of rewards and conserving resources when they judge additional incentives to be redundant or ineffective. The pattern is consistent with **strategic reinforcement**: reward when cooperation looks fragile or underappreciated, spend less when cooperative behavior seems already established or when others are unresponsive. Across many opportunities, including in environments with numerous high contributors and lavish rewarding behavior by others, they nearly always choose not to reward anyone. Only once do they use reward, and then in a minimal, targeted way toward a player who is cooperating at a low-to-moderate level while simultaneously being heavily sanctioned by others. This isolated reward appears more like a corrective or balancing action than a general tendency to positively reinforce cooperation. They do not systematically reward high contributors, nor do they reciprocate reward from others. Overall, they rely on punishment—not reward—as their primary social tool.

Reward is this player’s primary and defining tool. They repeatedly spend very large fractions, and at times many multiples, of their effective endowment on rewards, often driving their own payoff deeply negative. Their rewarding is broad, inclusive, and only loosely tied to others’ contribution levels. They frequently distribute rewards across much of the group, including high, moderate, and sometimes low contributors, rather than sharply favoring top cooperators or excluding inconsistent ones. They also maintain or increase reward to some players who are already receiving ample rewards from others, suggesting a desire to amplify group-valued members rather than to equalize payoffs. The pattern points to a “generous philanthropist” or “group patron” type: they seem motivated by lifting others and expressing appreciation more than by strategic reinforcement or future gain. There is little evidence of careful calibration to marginal incentives; they ignore the low efficiency of reward technology and continue to grant large amounts even when it is individually very costly and when the marginal strategic benefit is unclear. In short, they are an extreme, non-calculating rewarder who treats giving as an end in itself.

They display an extremely strong, persistent preference for rewarding and spend very large amounts of their own payoff to transfer small amounts to others. They regularly turn what would be positive or moderate payoffs into very low or even negative payoffs for themselves because of reward spending. Their criteria for reward are broad and not tightly tied to contribution levels alone. They often reward: - Low or zero contributors repeatedly targeted by others’ punishment (e.g., those consistently receiving large deductions). - Moderate or high contributors, including some who vary in contribution. - Players who themselves reward others. They repeatedly send rewards to the same recipients over time, creating recognizable patterns of support. This indicates they are not using rewards only as one-off nudges but as ongoing status or protection signals. Their rewards are not profit-seeking: reinforcement is not focused on marginally increasing group contribution to improve their own payoff. Instead, the pattern points to a prosocial or rescuing motive—supporting victims, encouraging inclusion, and compensating those who are attacked or marginalized by group punishment. They do not pair rewards with threats of punishment; their strategy is purely carrot-based.

They are selectively but repeatedly willing to spend resources to reward others, even though the reward technology is weak and not payoff‑improving for the giver. They reward in multiple separated episodes, suggesting it is a stable part of their style rather than a one‑off experiment. Their targets are not simply the highest contributors; they sometimes reward very low contributors or players who are being heavily punished by others. This pattern is compatible with two overlapping motives: (1) encouraging cooperation or at least acknowledging participation, and (2) offering support or sympathy to players who are under social attack. They do not try to systematically build a tight alliance with a fixed set of partners, but they do revisit some recipients, which hints at a loose form of reputational signaling: “I am cooperative and kind.” Their use of reward is moderate in cost, never extreme or self‑sacrificing to the point of ruin.

The player is selective and sparing with rewards, but does use them. They refrain from indiscriminate or heavy rewarding even though many others spend very large amounts on rewards. When they do reward, they choose a small number of targets and spend only modestly. Their targets include individuals who have been heavily punished, have low or zero contributions in some rounds, or are otherwise on the losing end of the group’s sanction dynamics. This suggests that their reward use is at least partly compensatory or protective—supporting players who are being collectively hammered—rather than simply reinforcing high contribution levels. They are also willing to reward very low contributors in some instances, which indicates they are not using reward as a strict incentive scheme tied to contribution size. Instead, they treat reward as occasional social support or a soft signal of goodwill. They do not attempt to build a broad coalition via systematic mutual reward; their use is too infrequent and small to be strategic in that sense.

This player is an extremely willing and generous rewarder, often spending a large portion or even all of their available coins to reward others. They repeatedly choose to reward many different players in the same period, sometimes spreading small units widely, and other times concentrating more reward on a subset. Their reward behavior is not narrowly targeted only at the highest contributors; they frequently reward players across a range of contribution levels, including some who contributed modestly or even zero in that period. They also reward both cooperative and heavily punished players, suggesting that they are sometimes trying to support victims or stabilize distressed participants, not only to reinforce high contributions. They never appear to use reward in a purely self-serving, bilateral “you scratch my back, I scratch yours” fashion. Instead, their pattern is broadly prosocial and diffuse, often rewarding players who did not just reward them. They also give reward in periods where it dramatically lowers their own payoff, indicating that they prioritize influencing others or expressing social preferences over immediate earnings. From behavior alone, the exact criterion for whom they reward is noisy, but two tendencies are visible: they often include reliable cooperators, and they sometimes give positive reinforcement or support to those who have been heavily punished or seem vulnerable. Overall, they act as a “positive-sum evangelist”: they refuse to punish and choose to invest heavily in rewards, even at large personal cost, trying to shape the group through positive rather than negative incentives.

They are quite willing to reward and at times spend a nontrivial share of their payoff doing so. Rewards are not constant each round; they appear in clusters where several cooperators receive small positive transfers. The trigger for reward seems to be reasonably high contributions and/or consistent participation in the public good, not personal favoritism. They sometimes continue to reward the same cooperative partner across separate episodes, but they also skip opportunities to reward similarly high contributors in other periods, indicating that their criteria are somewhat selective and not purely rule-based. They do not use rewards to “rescue” under-contributors; low contribution is never met with positive reinforcement. Their pattern suggests they value signaling approval to visible cooperators and are willing to incur moderate costs to reinforce such behavior, though without attempting to manage the entire group’s incentives comprehensively.

This player is an extremely active and generous rewarder, consistently spending nontrivial portions of their own payoff to reward others. They almost always direct rewards toward high contributors and apparent cooperators, indicating a clear intention to reinforce prosocial behavior rather than to buy favor from free-riders. A substantial portion of their rewarding is focused on a single, reliably high-contributing partner, suggesting they are comfortable forming a quasi-alliance or “favorite” within the group when someone embodies the cooperative norm. At times they broaden their rewards to multiple high contributors at once, even at considerable personal cost, which indicates that they value sustaining a cooperative climate more than short-run earnings. Later in the game, they engage in a burst of wide-ranging rewards to many strong contributors simultaneously, even when their own immediate payoff falls sharply as a result. This behavior signals that they treat rewarding as a way to recognize and endorse a cooperative coalition, not just as narrow reciprocity. They do not reward low contributors or heavy punishers simply for strategic protection, suggesting their rewarding is more norm-based and esteem-driven than purely instrumental.

The player never spends on rewards despite repeatedly receiving rewards from others, including in rounds where they contribute zero. They thus do not appear motivated to positively reinforce cooperation or to build alliances by gifting coins. The absence of any reward behavior, even when others show extensive pro-social rewarding, indicates either that they consider the reward technology too inefficient, that they do not value reciprocation as a strategy, or that they are relatively indifferent to signaling gratitude. At minimum, they show no inclination to use rewards as a tool for coordination or coalition-building.

They are willing to spend substantial resources to reward, and their rewarding is patterned and selective rather than random. They reward players who contribute high amounts consistently, focusing particularly on those who seem to sustain cooperation and do not engage in obvious exploitation. They are willing to incur large personal costs to send positive reinforcement, even when the immediate payoff consequence for them is strongly negative. They sometimes spread rewards across multiple high contributors in the same period rather than concentrating on a single ally, consistent with a norm‑supporting rather than purely alliance‑building motive. They do not appear to use rewards strategically to curry favor with powerful punishers; instead, they primarily reinforce high contributors and stable cooperators. They also sometimes abstain from rewarding, indicating they are not compulsively generous; rewards are contingent on perceived cooperative behavior or alignment with their norm.

This player essentially does not use the reward option. Across the interaction they refrain from granting rewards even when there are clear high contributors or players who spend substantial personal resources benefiting others. This indicates that they either view the reward mechanism as inefficient (the technology provides no net social gain beyond simple transfer), or they prioritize keeping their own payoff stable over investing in positive reinforcement. It may also reflect a belief that cooperation is already high enough without rewards, or that punishment is the more relevant lever for dealing with problematic behavior. In any case, their type is “reluctant rewarder”: they do not seem inclined to spend on gratitude or alliance-building transfers, even when they personally receive rewards.

This player uses rewards sparingly and selectively. They reward mainly clear high contributors or consistently cooperative players, and the intensity of rewarding is modest compared to their expenditure on punishment. When they do reward, it often accompanies or follows that player’s visible cooperative behavior, suggesting a motivation to positively reinforce contributions rather than to buy allies or manipulate outcomes. They do not use reward to compensate those they themselves have punished, and they do not systematically reward those who have rewarded them, which points more toward normative reinforcement than reciprocal exchange. Relative to their strong engagement in punishment, their reward behavior is secondary; they appear to see punishment as the primary tool for shaping the group, with reward as an occasional complement for standout cooperators.

They also never use the reward option despite frequent, salient instances of other players using reward heavily and despite receiving substantial rewards themselves in multiple rounds. They refrain from rewarding even high or consistent contributors, even when those players could be seen as sustaining the public good. This is not because rewards are obviously “bad deals” in this environment (reward is break‑even technologically, but can shift behavior); rather, they appear to give essentially no weight to second‑order incentives in either direction. The absence of any reward spending suggests little interest in actively shaping others’ behavior via positive reinforcement or in signaling gratitude or alliance. Their stance is consistent with a “first‑order only” focus: they invest in the public good but do not invest in meta‑level social tools, regardless of how others use those tools. They also do not try to leverage reward strategically to induce more cooperation around them, even though they can observe which players are high contributors. This indicates limited inclination toward social engineering or coalition‑building via rewards.

The player uses reward more sparingly than punishment but does employ it in a targeted and positively oriented way. When they do reward, it goes to players who have either contributed reasonably well or have been central in others’ reward networks. They sometimes choose moderate or single-unit reward rather than extreme generosity, indicating a controlled, selective approach rather than indiscriminate gifting. Rewarding seems aimed more at acknowledging or reinforcing cooperative or prosocial behavior than at engineering strict reciprocity contracts, since there is no clear link between being personally benefited and then rewarding in return. They do not reward frequently enough to build a stable alliance structure, but they do send occasional “approval” signals to certain peers. Relative to their heavy use of punishment, their reward behavior is mild. This asymmetry suggests they are more motivated to correct or protest behavior they dislike than to actively cultivate coalitions through positive incentives, even though they understand and occasionally use the reward channel strategically.

They never spend on rewards, despite many opportunities and a rich environment of others both contributing and engaging in heavy reward behavior. This could reflect one or more of: a perception that the reward technology is inefficient relative to punishment, a general reluctance to spend on social tools, or a preference for norm enforcement over positive reinforcement. Because they are themselves often rewarded, their lack of reciprocal rewarding suggests they do not engage in explicit reciprocity via the reward mechanism. They appear to rely on their own high contribution as their primary prosocial signal, rather than using rewards to build alliances or encourage partners.

This player shows only limited and sporadic use of rewards. When they do reward, it is directed toward clearly high contributors or those who appear to support cooperation, and the amounts are modest relative to the scale of their punishment. They seem conceptually aware of rewards as a way to support strong cooperators, but in practice, they allocate most of their regulatory effort and resources to punishing low contributors rather than building alliances via positive reinforcement. Reward use appears more like occasional signaling of approval than a systematic strategy: they do not consistently reward all high contributors, nor do they maintain a stable reward relationship with any particular partner. Overall, they behave as a punishment-first, reward-second enforcer; they have some preference to recognize high contributions, but that preference is weak compared to their drive to sanction free-riding.

They also never spend any coins on rewarding others, despite extensive opportunities and salient examples of other players using rewards heavily. Even when some co-players contribute at similarly high levels or direct significant positive transfers toward others (including toward this player), they do not reciprocate through the reward mechanism. This strongly suggests that they do not view the reward system as part of their cooperative toolkit. Possible explanations include: they see reward as strategically weak given its 1:1 cost-benefit (no multiplier advantage); they prefer “quiet” cooperation (contributing to the public good) over interpersonal transfers; or they may find the emerging reward patterns too chaotic or manipulative to join. Their own payoff position is generally strong, so financial constraints alone do not explain the absence of rewards. They are thus a pure-contribution cooperator: they support the public good via their endowment but do not invest in social engineering via targeted positive incentives. They neither cultivate allies nor reinforce good behavior with rewards, even though their own contributions would make them a natural hub for reciprocal reward networks if they wanted that role.

They use rewards selectively and sparingly, targeting a small subset of peers. The recipients are consistently high or reliably cooperative contributors, and often appear to be steady, non-disruptive players. This pattern suggests they reward prosocial behavior rather than random partners. They do not mass-reward everyone; they focus on a few, indicating a desire to reinforce specific cooperative partners or signals of reliability. They never reward clear low contributors or extreme freeriders, implying conditional generosity: reward is contingent on observable contribution behavior. Their reward spending is limited relative to others’ extremes; they value social reinforcement but keep costs contained, balancing encouragement of norms with preservation of their own earnings. Their use of reward across the game suggests they see it as a nuanced tool for reinforcement or signaling, not as a primary strategy or as a way to buy alliances at any cost.

They never use the reward mechanism, even toward clear high cooperators or after players fix earlier mistakes and align with the cooperative norm. They do, however, provide verbal social rewards—encouragement, thanks, and celebration of group success—indicating that they recognize and value others’ cooperation but prefer costless, verbal reinforcement rather than costly in‑game rewards. The available reward technology is relatively inefficient, which may also deter their use, but there is no explicit evidence that they calculate this tradeoff; their behavior is simply consistent with “cooperate, be nice, avoid spending extra.” They also do not test the mechanism, suggesting low curiosity or low perceived added value in monetary rewards relative to stable cooperative play.

They never use the reward mechanism at any point, including times when others’ cooperation is stable and high. This is notable because they consistently benefit from others’ full contributions but still refrain from spending to reinforce them. The reluctance to reward persists even after other players explicitly mention giving rewards. Possible interpretations: they prioritize maximizing their own payoff over costly positive reinforcement; they see reward as unnecessary given the entrenched cooperative norm; or they are disengaged from that layer of the mechanism and aiming to complete the task quickly with minimal extra actions. Their non-use of rewards aligns with an efficiency-through-inaction mindset: contribute fully, avoid side-payments, finish the task.

They never use the reward mechanism at any point, even when others express gratitude, acknowledge help, or mention giving rewards. This is notable because the group environment includes at least one explicitly generous rewarder. Their non-rewarding behavior is consistent and suggests that they either: (a) see reward as too costly relative to the benefit, (b) focus on preserving their own payoff, or (c) are satisfied with verbal/social approval rather than monetary reinforcement. There is no sign that they attempt to strategically reinforce high contributors or reciprocate kindness through the reward channel. Overall, they behave as a non-rewarding cooperator: they cooperate in contributions but do not spend to shape others’ behavior.

They never use reward, even when others clearly cooperate consistently and even when some players explicitly reward the group for earlier mistakes. There is no sign of them trying to selectively reinforce behavior or build alliances through positive incentives. Given that rewarding is individually inefficient in this mechanism, this restraint may reflect either attention to personal payoff efficiency or a perception that cooperation is already sufficiently stable without extra cost. Since they also refrain from punishment, their overall pattern is to avoid spending on sanctioning mechanisms at all, relying instead on direct cooperation and social communication to sustain norms.

They use the reward mechanism selectively but repeatedly, despite its poor efficiency. Rewards are always directed toward clear cooperators or stable, helpful contributors (e.g., players who consistently contribute or help keep the group aligned), not toward defectors or inconsistent participants. They sometimes explicitly frame rewards as compensation or appreciation when they feel they “screwed up” earlier, suggesting they use rewards partly to repair perceived fairness imbalances and signal goodwill. They also spread rewards across different cooperative partners over time, not just one “favorite,” indicating a diffuse, group-oriented rather than dyadic or alliance-based reward strategy. They do not attempt to use rewards as explicit bargaining chips or to condition future behavior; instead, rewards function as recognition, gratitude, and solidarity gestures, even at a personal cost.

They never use the reward option, despite being aware of it and observing others occasionally discuss rewards. They do not reward even highly cooperative partners who consistently contribute fully, and they do not use rewards to repair or encourage behavior after mistakes. This suggests that they rely on verbal acknowledgement (“thanks”) rather than costly material reinforcement. The non-use of reward persists even in a highly cooperative group, implying either that they view reward as unnecessary, too costly, or simply not worth the effort given already strong cooperation. Their strategy appears to be: set a good example and communicate norms, but avoid spending coins on formal incentives.

They never spend any coins on rewarding others, even when the group achieves full cooperation over many rounds and other players explicitly mention or use rewards. They ignore the opportunity entirely. Given that the reward technology is inefficient (it costs more to reward than the recipient gains), their non-use may reflect simple payoff rationality, indifference to signaling gratitude, or low engagement with the monitoring/enforcement dimension of the game (unknown which). They do not use rewards strategically to reinforce cooperation or to thank others, nor do they attempt to build alliances or reputations through positive incentives.

The player never spends resources to reward anyone, despite having multiple opportunities and seeing substantial variation in others’ contributions and reward behavior. They are consistently a non-rewarder. This occurs even when some peers contribute at or near their own high level, and even when some peers are clearly investing heavily in rewarding others. Their lack of reward holds regardless of whether doing so might strategically encourage future cooperation or reciprocate kindness. This pattern suggests either (a) a principled avoidance of costly reward (perhaps seeing it as inefficient or unnecessary), (b) a narrow focus on contribution behavior as their sole cooperative act, or (c) low valuation of using the reward mechanism as a tool for social influence. They do not engage in targeted reciprocity, alliance-building, or “thanking” via reward, even toward players who reward them more than once or sacrifice heavily to reward others. Any prosocial motivation they have is channelled exclusively through contributing to the public good, not through second-order cooperation via reward.

This player never uses the reward mechanism, despite repeated opportunities and the presence of clearly identifiable high cooperators and visible patterns of rewarding by others. Even when others are making large contributions or spending heavily to reward, they refrain from investing any amount in rewarding anyone. Their revealed preference is to conserve their own payoff rather than spend on reinforcing others, even prosocially. This suggests either (a) they judge the cost of rewarding as not worth the benefit to group behavior, (b) they are skeptical that reward will meaningfully change others’ future contributions, or (c) they treat reward as outside their responsibility, focusing on contribution choices only. There is no evidence that they selectively reward high contributors, sanction low contributors indirectly through reward omission, or engage in any form of strategic alliance-building via positive incentives. Overall, they act as a “non-rewarding cooperator”: they contribute steadily but do not invest in second-order cooperation (enforcing or reinforcing norms through costly rewards).

The player is a very sparing user of the reward mechanism. Across the game, they activate reward only a few times and at low intensity, and abstain in most rounds. When they do reward, the pattern indicates targeted reinforcement rather than broad generosity: they select specific individuals rather than distributing small rewards widely. Given the visibility of others’ contributions, it is plausible—but not certain—that their targets tend to be relatively high contributors or socially salient cooperators. Confidence about exact targeting criteria is low; the only clear point is that rewards are not frequent or diffuse. They do not appear to use reward strategically at scale to reshape group behavior, suggesting either that they judge the cost–benefit ratio of this reward technology as unattractive, or they are more focused on preserving their own payoff than on actively managing others’ incentives. Their occasional reward spending indicates they are willing to incur some cost to acknowledge desirable behavior, but they place strict limits on this spending and do not adopt a “moral enforcer” or “strong reciprocator” role. Overall, their reward style is selective, low-volume, and primarily symbolic or marginally strategic, rather than central to their approach.

This player uses rewards very sparingly. Across the game they almost always choose not to spend on reward, despite having visible opportunities to reinforce high contributors. On the rare occasion when they do reward, they direct it toward relatively cooperative players, so their reward use is aligned with contribution levels rather than being random or retaliatory. Despite this alignment, they do not engage in systematic or sustained rewarding. They do not attempt to build a reputation as a rewarder or to establish a reciprocal reward loop; their primary stance is to conserve their own payoff by avoiding the cost of reward. Reward is treated as an exceptional, situational tool rather than a core strategy. This pattern is consistent with a payoff-maximizing mindset that regards the available reward technology as too inefficient to justify frequent use. Their behavior suggests they view personal cost of rewarding as outweighing the benefits of socially shaping others’ behavior.

This player is highly conservative with rewards and uses them sparingly. For the majority of the game, they refrain from spending on reward despite clear opportunities to reinforce strong cooperators. When they do choose to reward, it happens only once and is focused on high contributors, consistent with a simple “reward the obvious cooperators” rule rather than nuanced strategy. They never invest in sustained or repeated rewarding, even when some players consistently contribute much more than they do. This indicates a strong concern for personal payoff preservation and a perception that reward is not cost‑effective at the given technology, or a low intrinsic motivation to pay to support others. There is no evidence that they use reward tactically to build alliances or to correct behavior; it is occasional, one‑off reinforcement, not part of a systematic strategy.

The player is clearly willing to use the reward mechanism and does so in a selective, non-constant way. They are not a blanket rewarder: there are rounds where they spend nothing even when others contribute highly, and rounds where they spend modestly, followed by a final round with very large reward expenditure. Overall, they appear willing to sacrifice earnings to reward perceived high cooperators. Triggers for rewarding appear to be high or consistently high contributions by others, especially by players who repeatedly contribute more than the focal player. They also sometimes reward after having themselves free-rode in contributions, suggesting that they use rewards as a way to partly “compensate” or to reinforce others’ prosocial behavior while personally staying more flexible on contributions. The cost–benefit ratio of reward is not favorable, yet they still use it heavily at the end, indicating that material payoff is not their sole objective; social signaling, norm expression, or gratitude appear important. Strategically, they seem to use small and medium rewards earlier to recognize or encourage cooperative behavior, then escalate in the last round when there is no future strategic benefit. This escalation suggests intrinsic social preferences (e.g., wanting to see cooperators “win” or be recognized) more than purely instrumental future-gains reasoning.

This player is highly reluctant to use the reward mechanism. They use it only once, and then minimally, despite repeated opportunities and visible examples of others rewarding. The decision to reward occurs in an early cooperative context, and they immediately stop using rewards afterward, even when others continue to contribute a lot. This pattern suggests several possible motives, with uncertainty: they may view the reward technology as too inefficient (costly to themselves for relatively small benefits to others), may prefer to keep their own payoffs stable, or may not see sufficient marginal value in reinforcing already-cooperative behavior. Their general strategy is to free-ride on the norm-enhancing behavior of others who do choose to reward rather than invest personal resources into shaping the group. Rewarding does not appear to be central to their identity; they behave more like a quiet beneficiary of the cooperative structure than an active norm enforcer. Whether they conceptually approve of reward but find it too costly, or are simply indifferent to it, is unknown.

The player is a light but recurring rewarder. They are willing to incur personal costs to transfer positive payoffs to others, but they do so sparsely, never engaging in heavy or systematic rewarding. Their rewards consistently go to others rather than to themselves, and they never exploit the mechanism to self-reward when this is possible, indicating a preference against overtly self-serving use of the reward tool. Their targets are always high or very high contributors; they do not reward low contributors at any point. This implies a norm of “merit-based gratitude”: they pay to acknowledge strong cooperation. However, they do not appear to finely differentiate among multiple high contributors or to calibrate reward magnitudes closely to contribution differences; their rewarding seems more categorical (“this is good enough to reward”) than precisely scaled. They do not reward in every round in which high contributions occur, suggesting that they either sample occasionally to signal appreciation or are balancing social preferences with a strong concern for preserving their own earnings.

This player almost never uses the reward option, despite having many opportunities to reward clear cooperators. They do not provide positive reinforcement to others, even when group members contribute fully or when some players also engage in norm enforcement. This suggests they view negative sanctions as the primary tool for shaping behavior and either discount or overlook the potential role of rewards. Their non‑use of rewards holds both in highly cooperative phases and in more fragmented periods. This consistency implies a stable preference rather than simple omission. They may see reward as redundant, costly, or less effective than punishment, or they may operate with a norm that “doing the right thing” (full contribution) does not merit extra pay. In any case, they adopt a one‑sided enforcement strategy: punish defectors, ignore cooperators, rather than a full carrot‑and‑stick approach.

The player never uses rewards, even though they consistently see others using positive reinforcement to encourage cooperators and allies. They refrain from rewarding both stable high contributors and those who change from low to higher contributions. This suggests they are unwilling to incur a personal cost to transfer earnings to others, even to those who match their cooperative standard. They do not appear to use rewards strategically to build alliances, stabilize high-contribution norms, or respond to being rewarded themselves. Their non-use of both punishment and reward points to a general reluctance to engage in costly social interventions, rather than a simple negativity aversion. They are content to cooperate but not to actively reshape others’ incentives via transfers.

They never use the reward option despite many opportunities, even toward consistent full contributors who support high public-good levels. This suggests that their cooperative norm is enforced almost exclusively via negative incentives. Either they judge the reward technology as not worthwhile, or they see sanctioning defectors as more central than positively reinforcing cooperators. Because they regularly spend nontrivial amounts on punishment in cooperative contexts, the absence of reward is not purely about saving coins; it reflects a directional preference for punishment over reward. This indicates a “stick not carrot” enforcement style: they assume that cooperation is the baseline obligation and does not merit extra compensation. There is no evidence that they try to build alliances via mutual rewarding or to thank others economically for aligning with their norms. They appear to rely on their contributions themselves, combined with punishment of defectors, as the main social signals.

This player is willing to incur real cost to reward high or reliably cooperative contributors, but they do so only occasionally and in a targeted way. They never reward defectors. Their rewards go to players who either consistently contribute fully or who stand out as cooperative in contexts where others under-contribute. This shows a preference for reinforcing pro-social behavior and a sensitivity to reputational cues. They sometimes reward even when the immediate payoff gain from cooperation is modest and when reward is exactly cost-equal (no efficiency gain). This suggests that their reward behavior is motivated by norm signaling or relationship-building rather than pure efficiency. They do not reward broadly or every time someone contributes; instead, they choose a small subset of partners and moments, which may reflect a strategy of selective encouragement toward perceived allies or role models in sustaining cooperation. Their willingness to both punish defectors and reward cooperators indicates a dual-use social-preference profile: they are willing to pay both to discipline and to support, and they treat these as separate tools rather than substitutable.

They never spend any resources on rewarding others. This holds even in configurations where others are uniformly cooperative and some group members use their own tokens to reward high contributors. The player does not use reward either as positive reinforcement for cooperation or as a tactic to build alliances or signal goodwill. Their refusal to reward persists across all phases, regardless of whether they are currently contributing or free-riding. This suggests that they view the reward mechanism as an unnecessary cost with no sufficient expected return, at least from their perspective. Their stance is consistent with a purely self-focused payoff orientation: they accept rewards from others when given but will not reciprocate or pay to sustain a cooperative atmosphere. Normatively, they do not participate in “upholding” good behavior via positive incentives and appear comfortable letting others provide any such social goods if they choose.

The player is substantially more active in using rewards than punishment. They reward multiple different peers across the game, typically those who are contributing fully or who have just behaved cooperatively after earlier uncertainty. The rewards are small and one-unit at a time, suggesting a low-intensity but deliberate effort to reinforce prosocial behavior. They sometimes reward cooperators even when they themselves have defected in that round, indicating that the reward is about acknowledging others’ cooperation rather than about symmetric reciprocity in that moment. They also reward high contributors who have been frequent punishers, suggesting that they may be signaling approval of strong norm enforcers or trying to maintain goodwill with central cooperative players. Their reward use appears selective and conditional on consistent or notable cooperation, and they do not seem to reward low contributors. Overall, they use reward as a positive signal and reinforcement tool, even though the reward technology is payoff-neutral; this points to a social or normative motivation in addition to material payoffs.

This player frequently uses the reward mechanism and is willing to spend a meaningful portion of their payoff to do so. Their pattern indicates that they treat reward as a core tool for sustaining cooperation rather than a rare or emergency measure. They are clearly not purely self-interested in the narrow sense, because each unit of reward is strictly cost-equivalent, yet they still spend steadily on others across many rounds. Their triggers for rewarding appear to be primarily contribution-based: they reliably reward in environments where there are clear high or very high contributors, and they refrain when collective behavior is more middling, uneven, or when they themselves are not strongly modeling maximum cooperation. This suggests a simple, consistent heuristic: reinforce visible cooperators, particularly those who match or approach their own high standard. At times they spend several units in a single round, implying that they may reward multiple peers rather than singling out a single “favorite.” The repeated willingness to incur nontrivial costs over many rounds indicates that this is not mere random experimentation but a stable strategic choice: they are trying to incentivize and maintain a cooperative atmosphere by positively reinforcing those whose behavior aligns with a high-contribution norm. Strategically, they use rewards in a forward-looking manner. They do not clearly switch off rewarding when they are not themselves rewarded; instead, they continue to use rewards in many rounds irrespective of how much they personally receive. This reduces the likelihood that their use of rewards is based on direct tit-for-tat reciprocity and instead suggests a “public norm enforcement via positive incentives” mindset. They are not maximally aggressive in reward use: there are rounds where strong cooperation is present and they choose not to reward. This may reflect either budget concerns, declining marginal perceived impact of additional rewards, or momentary testing of whether others will sustain cooperation without constant reinforcement. Overall, they present as a cooperative norm enforcer using reward as a prosocial tool rather than as a strictly self-serving, reciprocal, or reputational trick.

This player is willing to spend on rewards, but only intermittently and with apparent selectivity. They do not reward every time others cooperate, nor do they abstain from rewarding entirely. Instead, they use rewards in a sparse, targeted way that likely serves as a signal rather than a constant reinforcement tool. They appear more inclined to reward when there is a clear cluster of strong cooperators whose actions stand out, but they do not respond mechanically to high contributions. The pattern is consistent with using rewards to mark particularly desirable behavior or to maintain goodwill with key cooperators, rather than to systematically subsidize the group. They sometimes invest a nontrivial amount in rewarding despite already earning high payoffs, suggesting that rewards are not purely driven by immediate financial optimization; they are willing to sacrifice some coins to influence others’ behavior or to express approval. Their reward usage does not display obvious tit-for-tat symmetry at a fine-grained level: they do not reward every time they themselves receive reward, nor do they clearly cease rewarding after being ignored. This indicates that they are not a rigid reciprocal rewarder. Instead, their reward decisions seem anchored on strategic reinforcement of group-level cooperation and perhaps the maintenance of goodwill with players they regard as especially valuable contributors. At the same time, they do not behave like a “moral crusader” or unconditional altruist. They go multiple rounds without rewarding despite high contributions from others, which suggests that they weigh the personal cost of rewarding against perceived marginal benefit. Their reward strategy is thus best characterized as “selective encouragement”: they use the reward channel to nudge or thank, but they conserve resources and do not attempt to fully internalize the externalities of others’ contributions.

This player never uses the reward mechanism at all. Their willingness to incur personal cost to strategically influence others’ behavior via rewards appears effectively zero in this environment. This is not because rewards are unavailable or ineffective, but because they consistently refrain from using them even when others clearly contribute at very high levels and when they themselves receive rewards from multiple peers. There is no sign of targeted reinforcement of high contributors, no attempt to encourage the group, and no visible inclination to “thank” others materially. This implies they either (a) treat the reward stage as outside their responsibility or ethical focus; (b) view the cost of rewarding as not worth any speculative future benefit; or (c) place their cooperative effort solely in contribution decisions, not in second-order prosocial actions. The absence of any reward behavior indicates they are not using rewards as a strategic tool to shape norms, nor as an expressive tool to signal approval.

This player is “selective but willing to reward,” using rewards as a supportive, not punitive or exploitative, tool. They do not reward every round, but they engage the reward mechanism multiple times and spend a nontrivial amount of coins overall, accepting a personal cost to influence others. The timing of rewards aligns with environments where there are clear high contributors present, indicating that reward-giving is principally triggered by visible cooperative behavior from peers. They appear to use rewards to positively reinforce cooperation rather than to compensate themselves or manipulate outcomes. There is no evidence that they reward low contributors in an attempt to “pull them in” or build alliances with non-cooperators. Their use of rewards looks more like a recognition or encouragement of already cooperative partners, consistent with a preference to bolster existing prosocial norms. They do not rigidly reward every high contributor, nor do they reward in every favorable environment, suggesting their threshold for rewarding is higher than “any cooperation deserves a reward.” Instead, their behavior points to a selective, perhaps budget-conscious strategy: rewards are meaningful signals rather than automatic transfers. They do not appear to tie their reward decisions strongly to whether they themselves were rewarded in the immediately preceding round. Sometimes they reward when they have just been rewarded; sometimes when they have not. This weak linkage suggests their main criterion is others’ contribution behavior rather than personal gain through reciprocal reward cycles. Strategically, they seem to view rewards as a complementary mechanism to their own high contributions: they try to both contribute generously and occasionally “nudge” others in a cooperative direction through positive reinforcement, while keeping overall reward spending at a moderate level.

This player uses rewards sparingly and selectively. They does not systematically invest in rewarding high cooperators, nor does they try to build or maintain norms of strong reciprocity via regular rewarding. Across the game, they sometimes spends on reward and sometimes not, with no clear pattern of “always reward high contributors” or “never reward anyone.” This indicates low but nonzero willingness to sacrifice payoff to influence others. Their reward behavior seems more reactive and experimental than principled. There are instances where they rewards despite not having been especially favored that round, and other instances where they receives substantial reward but does not obviously respond with increased rewarding. This suggests they is not running a straightforward “reward those who reward me” or “reward those who contribute the most” rule in a consistent way. They appears to treat rewarding as a tactical tool rather than a core part of their strategy. The small, intermittent investments into reward imply that they is probing whether minor costs can help sustain a high-contribution environment that they can then partially free-ride on. However, the absence of strong, sustained rewarding indicates they is not strongly committed to norm enforcement or to building tight reciprocal bonds through generous reward. Incentive-wise, they seems to value keeping costs low over actively shaping others’ behavior. When they does reward, they uses small amounts, suggesting a cautious, low-intensity attempt at reinforcement rather than a strong preference for altruistic or prosocial signaling. Overall, they is weakly cooperative in their reward use: willing to pay a little in some moments but not to the degree that would mark them as a dedicated rewarder or community builder.

This player is a heavy and targeted user of rewards early and mid‑game, then becomes more conservative, indicating a strategic rather than purely impulsive approach. They are clearly willing to spend nontrivial amounts of their own payoff to reward others, which signals a strong pro‑social orientation and a willingness to incur direct costs to support cooperation. Given that rewarders are identifiable to the rewarded, their behavior likely aims to reinforce and signal approval to consistent high contributors. The pattern suggests they prioritize rewarding players who contribute high amounts relative to peers and possibly those who maintain high contributions over time. They do not appear to reward randomly or minimally; when rewarding, they commit meaningful amounts, consistent with an attempt to shape others’ incentives and build reputational ties with cooperators. They eventually stop using rewards despite continued cooperation from many others. This shift is consistent with two possible, not mutually exclusive motives: (a) they infer that cooperation has become stable enough that additional private spending on rewards yields little extra benefit, or (b) they begin to protect their own earnings after having invested heavily in earlier rounds. The reduction in reward spending coincides with continued fairly high contributions, implying that they see rewarding as an optional tool to scaffold cooperation, not as an unconditional obligation. Overall, they use rewards as strategic reinforcement: they are willing to pay to strengthen norms and possibly to mark trustworthy partners, but once the cooperative environment seems established or the marginal gain seems lower, they conserve resources and rely on established patterns rather than continued costly signaling.

The player uses reward very sparingly and never as a major strategic tool. Most rounds they give no reward at all. When they do reward, they spend only a small amount, indicating that they value conserving personal payoff over actively shaping others’ behavior through incentives. Observed rewards occur in contexts where many peers behave cooperatively. This suggests that their primary trigger for giving reward is recognition of high contribution levels by others, not retaliation or attempts to correct low contributors. There is no evidence of rewarding defectors or of using reward as an inclusive, group‑wide carrot; instead, the player appears to selectively reward a subset of others (exact recipients are unknown), likely those they see as especially cooperative or aligned with their own contribution level at that moment. This pattern is consistent with targeted positive reinforcement, but applied weakly. Strategically, they do not appear to rely on reward as a central instrument to manage the group. The low and infrequent reward expenditure suggests that they view the public good mechanism itself as doing most of the work, and reward as a marginal, optional bonus. They may see reward more as a small, situational gesture than as a systematic strategy to build a cooperative coalition. Gratitude signaling is present but minimal. The player occasionally spends to reward in rounds where they themselves are behaving cooperatively and others also appear cooperative, which is consistent with expressing approval. However, they do not escalate reward when they receive more reward themselves, and they do not maintain a stable pattern of “tit‑for‑tat” rewarding. This indicates that they do not treat reward as an ongoing exchange relationship but as a limited, opportunistic signal.

This player uses the reward mechanism sparingly and selectively. They are willing to spend on rewards, but do so in only a minority of opportunities, suggesting that rewarding is not their primary tool for shaping group behavior. When they do reward, it aligns with strong cooperative contexts: they appear to reward in situations where overall contributions are high and where some peers stand out as consistent cooperators. This pattern fits a strategic reinforcement motive: they invest in rewarding to stabilize or encourage already high cooperation rather than to rescue failing cooperation. They do not seem to use reward to compensate themselves materially, since the exchange rate is one-to-one and they sometimes pay to reward even when they are already doing well in terms of payoffs. Their rewards therefore look like signals of appreciation and attempts to sustain a cooperative culture rather than short-term profit-seeking moves. The fact that they often forgo rewarding, even when others are contributing a lot, suggests that they weigh the cost carefully and reserve reward for moments they consider especially salient or for particular peers (identity of recipients is unknown from the transcript, so recipient-targeting is low confidence). They show no sign of vindictive or corrective use of reward toward low contributors. Instead of trying to “train” defectors via positive incentives, they appear to focus their limited rewards on encouraging cooperators. Overall, they behave like a cautious, efficiency-minded rewarder: willing to invest in positive reinforcement, but only when they judge the marginal impact or expressive value to be worthwhile.

This player is a selective but noticeably benevolent rewarder who uses the reward mechanism in a targeted, non-random way. They do not reward every round, and when they do reward, they are willing to spend nontrivial amounts of their own payoff. This indicates they value reinforcing cooperation even at a one-to-one cost, which offers no direct monetary leverage. Their reward behavior appears primarily triggered by high or steady contributions from others rather than by attempts to discipline low contributors. Because the tech is one-for-one and there is no punishment, any use of reward is costly and symbolically strong. Their repeated willingness to spend suggests that they use reward partly as a social signal: a way to acknowledge cooperative partners and potentially stabilize those partners’ future contributions. They sometimes reward in environments where they themselves are also cooperating fully, which implies they are not simply trying to “buy” better treatment after defecting; instead, they are likely trying to support a cooperative climate when they see it. In at least one instance they spend a relatively large amount on reward while also contributing fully, reinforcing the view that they treat reward as a tool to support group norms rather than as a marginal, opportunistic tweak. They do not use reward in a clearly tit-for-tat or perfectly contingent way; there are cooperative peers who are not rewarded every time, and they sometimes refrain from rewarding in cooperative contexts. This suggests either limited resources for reward, a threshold-based rule (only reward when particularly salient or needed), or uncertainty about who exactly to prioritize. The pattern is more “episodic reinforcement” than systematic reciprocity. Strategically, they appear to use reward to: - Encourage or stabilize already high contributors. - Signal approval and possibly build reputational links with cooperative individuals. They do not obviously attempt to bribe low contributors into contributing more; there is no clear evidence of them rewarding defectors or marginal contributors as a way to pull them up, though this is uncertain because individual reward targets are not shown. In short, they are a conditional positive-reinforcement cooperator: willing to pay to support peers they regard as good contributors, but not using reward every time or in a fully mechanical way.

This player is a heavy and consistent user of the reward mechanism. They regularly spend a substantial portion of their payoff to reward others, even when they themselves receive relatively little in return. Their pattern clearly distinguishes between contributors and the persistent free-rider: they systematically avoid rewarding the free-rider after the group highlights this unfairness, while continuing to reward contributing peers. They also state explicitly that they distribute rewards broadly among contributors and exclude the free-rider, which matches their observed behavior. Their reward use appears primarily normative and pro-social rather than narrowly self-interested: they use rewards to reinforce full contribution and group solidarity, not merely to reciprocate rewards they personally receive. They continue to reward others even in a later round where their own incoming rewards drop, suggesting that gratitude is not their sole driver. The costliness of reward does not deter them; they accept significant payoff reduction to support cooperative norms. Their strategy is: “reward all cooperators fairly, do not reward clear defectors,” in line with a prosocial but sanctioning cooperator who uses positive incentives rather than punishment.

They never spend any coins to reward others, despite having ample opportunities and clear information about who is contributing and who is not. They also receive rewards from others early on despite not contributing, which they accept passively; there is no indication of gratitude, reciprocation, or strategic use of rewards to stabilize cooperation. They ignore explicit informal sanctioning via chat (calls to stop rewarding them) and continue to benefit from any residual rewards others choose to give. Their non-use of the reward mechanism suggests one or more of the following: they view reward spending as an inefficient cost to personal payoff; they do not see themselves as responsible for sustaining cooperation; or they are simply following a rule like “never spend on reward.” They do not use reward strategically either to repair relationships, buy forgiveness, or manipulate future group behavior. In effect, they treat the reward system only as a one-way benefit when others target them, never as a tool they themselves would deploy.

They are willing to spend a substantial share of their own payoff on rewards when they perceive stable cooperation among most of the group. Their reward behavior appears selective and strategic: they strongly push for zero rewards to the persistent free rider and verbally reinforce this norm, while continuing to support contributing peers. They also accept being rewarded without explicit reciprocation in some periods, suggesting they do not treat reward as a strict tit‑for‑tat ledger but as a tool to sustain group cooperation and signal approval for contributors. Over time, they reduce or stop spending on rewards in at least some later situations, which likely reflects adaptation to perceived inefficiency or fatigue with subsidizing others, but they never flip to rewarding defectors. Overall, they use reward as positive reinforcement for norm‑compliant behavior and as a collective substitute for formal punishment of norm violators.

Their use of rewards is selective, norm-based, and clearly conditional on cooperation and perceived fairness. Early on, they state that they reward everyone except the persistent free-rider, explicitly tying reward to contribution behavior and trying to use reward exclusion as a soft sanction. They view reward as a tool both to support cooperators and to pressure defectors rather than as a purely altruistic transfer. They do not reward themselves (self-reward is impossible here) and they do not appear to reward the free-rider despite that player’s consistently higher payoffs, which indicates they are not chasing reciprocity from exploiters; they draw a qualitative line between cooperators and defectors. They are cost-tolerant with respect to rewarding: in later rounds they spend nontrivial amounts on reward, even when they have previously complained about not getting much reward themselves. This suggests they are willing to incur efficiency losses to sustain cooperative norms. They also seek information about who is continuing to reward the free-rider, indicating concern that reward be aligned with contribution norms; they want the reward system to reinforce cooperation properly and are frustrated when others “misuse” it.

The player sometimes uses the reward mechanism but not consistently, and not in an obviously strategic pattern. They begin by spending nontrivial resources on reward, then intermittently switch to not rewarding at all, and later refrain entirely, yet their contributions remain constant. Because individual reward targets are unobservable, their criteria for whom to reward are unknown. However, several patterns emerge: - They are willing to incur substantial personal cost to reward others at least in some phases, indicating a genuine readiness to reinforce cooperation. - Their willingness to spend on reward is not tightly coupled to others’ contribution behavior: they continue to contribute fully even in phases when they reward no one, and they do not appear to use reward as a clear lever to change the free-rider’s behavior. - They explicitly state that they are not rewarding the persistent free-rider later on, which implies that they now condition reward on contribution at least in that case. Overall, they treat reward as a flexible tool rather than a fixed commitment: sometimes they invest heavily to support group members, and at other times they conserve resources. They appear comfortable withholding reward from defectors but do not use reward intensity as a strong, consistent signaling device. Gratitude signaling or reciprocal reward targeting cannot be confirmed from the available information (low confidence).

They are consistently willing to spend on reward, and they spend relatively heavily across the game compared to many others. Their reward pattern appears pro-social rather than narrowly self-interested: they repeatedly reward cooperators, including those who are also spending on reward, even when doing so erodes their own payoff. There is no observable shift toward punishing by omission (e.g., systematically excluding a particular cooperator) in response to being under-rewarded themselves; instead, they continue to fund others after expressing mild dissatisfaction about the overall situation outside the game’s micro-level interactions. They do not clearly adhere to the group’s emerging norm of boycotting the persistent free-rider through zero reward: the free-rider keeps receiving small positive reward units from someone, and given this player’s persistent generosity, it is plausible (but not certain) that they are at least occasionally among those still rewarding the defector. If so, that would indicate a type inclined to maintain inclusivity or to follow a simple “reward everyone who participates in the round” heuristic, rather than a contingent “only reward full cooperators” policy. Their reward use appears less strategically calculated and more norm-driven: they seem to treat rewarding as a standing obligation linked to cooperation norms, not as a flexible lever to manipulate others’ behavior.

The player consistently chooses not to use the reward mechanism at all. Despite being a full contributor and receiving rewards from others multiple times, they never reciprocate with rewards, even when strong social norms of “reward everyone except the defector” emerge among several players. This suggests either (a) a deliberate payoff-maximizing stance toward reward (avoiding any extra cost), (b) a belief that reward is unnecessary or inefficient given the existing contribution pattern, or (c) inattention or confusion about the reward stage; which of these dominates is unknown. They do not use reward strategically to encourage the chronic non-contributor to change behavior, nor do they use it to reinforce loyal cooperators, even when these cooperators incur nontrivial reward costs themselves. They also do not appear to engage in gratitude signaling via reward: receiving substantial reward does not induce them to “give back” in kind. Overall, they act as a pure provider of contributions but a free-rider on the reward system.

They never spend on rewards, even though they are surrounded by sustained high cooperation from many peers. This suggests that, for them, direct costly rewarding is not part of their strategy, despite benefiting from and presumably appreciating others’ contributions. Their cooperative “thanks” is expressed behaviorally—through unwavering full contributions—rather than through the formal reward mechanism. They may see reward spending as an unnecessary efficiency cost, especially in a setting where most group members already contribute highly without needing extra reinforcement. Alternatively, they might view the multiplication of contributions itself as sufficient “reward” for cooperators. Whether they conceptually support reward as a tool in other environments is unknown; in this game, they clearly prioritize keeping their payoffs for contribution rather than redistributing them as explicit bonuses to others.

They are very generous and systematic with rewards, frequently spending substantial coins to reward many different cooperative players within a round. Reward decisions track cooperative behavior: high contributors and visible norm supporters repeatedly receive rewards from them, and they expand the reward set when the persistent free-rider finally contributes, signaling approval and positive reinforcement. Rewards are distributed not only to a tight inner circle but also broadly to many cooperators, suggesting an attempt to sustain a culture of high contribution, not just pay back personal favors. They seem comfortable investing heavily in rewards even when they cannot observe detailed payoffs, implying they treat rewards as both gratitude signals and strategic reinforcement for pro-social norms. Their reward behavior is complementary to their punishment style: punish clear free-riders, reward clear cooperators, and use both levers to shape group behavior rather than to maximize their own short-term income.

They almost never use the reward mechanism and do not build a reputation as a positive reciprocator. Despite receiving rewards themselves in multiple rounds, they do not reciprocate with rewards toward others, even toward consistently high contributors or toward fellow punishers. Their reinforcement of cooperation is almost entirely negative (through punishment) rather than positive. When others shift from free-riding to cooperation, they do not respond by rewarding that change; instead, they either continue to punish based on past behavior or simply stop escalating sanctions. There is one visible instance where they punish a player who is currently cooperating while they themselves receive reward-like inflows from others, but they still do not use rewards in return. This pattern suggests that they prioritize norm enforcement and deterrence over expressing gratitude or building goodwill, and they may view rewards as less efficient or less emotionally satisfying than punishment. Their type is best characterized as “cooperative enforcer” rather than “reciprocal gift-giver”: they uphold contribution norms via costly sanctions, not via positive incentives.

They are an active, generous user of rewards, especially toward clear cooperators and coalition partners. When they do reward, they often spread rewards widely across nearly all full contributors who have been aligned with the cooperative norm, rather than favoring a narrow subset. This suggests they use rewards as a social recognition tool for groupwide cooperation, not just as a transactional exchange. They also employ more targeted rewards for specific allies who support high contributions and punitive strategies against free‑riders, signaling appreciation and reinforcing pro‑social leadership. Their reward timing often coincides with notable cooperative milestones (e.g., when a chronic defector finally contributes, or when many players sustain full cooperation). In those moments, they are willing to spend a significant amount on rewards even when the direct monetary return is modest or negative relative to keeping coins, indicating they value relational and normative reinforcement. Strategically, they see rewards as complementing punishment: punishment is used to deter and discipline free‑riders; rewards are used to encourage and affirm cooperators. They do not attempt to “bribe” the main defector with rewards; instead, rewards flow to those already following the norm. This pattern fits a “pro‑social norm entrepreneur” type: they try to shape the environment so that cooperation is both materially and socially rewarded, while defection is both costly and socially disapproved.

The player is an extreme, enthusiastic user of the reward mechanism. Despite personally contributing very little to the public fund, they spend very large sums of their own payoff repeatedly to reward others, especially those who contribute fully and/or are engaged in cooperative chat. They tend to spread rewards broadly across many players each time rather than focusing on a small subset, and they often reward in rounds where virtually everyone except them is fully cooperative. Their rewarding is strongly pro-social in direction—high contributors and central chat participants receive repeated positive transfers—even though the player’s own contribution behavior contradicts the cooperative norm they are rewarding. In effect, they act as a generous “sponsor” of others’ cooperation while personally free-riding on their contributions. This suggests that they enjoy reinforcing or “thanking” cooperative behavior without internalizing the same standard for themselves. The pattern does not appear narrowly strategic (they do not taper rewards when they are heavily punished, nor do they condition reward levels tightly on any visible outcome); instead, it resembles a stable preference for gifting and positive reciprocity toward cooperators, decoupled from their own decision to keep their endowment. They are thus a paradoxical type: a non-contributing, low-communication player who nonetheless pours substantial personal resources into rewarding others’ cooperation, using reward more as expressive generosity and social support than as a calculated incentive scheme.

They never actively reward any other player, despite several others contributing fully and despite generally appreciating cooperation in chat. This consistent absence of rewarding indicates either that they do not find reward a cost-effective way to promote cooperation, or that their fairness norm is more focused on punishing defectors than on positively reinforcing contributors. They do acknowledge group efforts verbally and seem pleased when the deviant finally contributes, but even then they do not expend coins on material reward. Their strategy therefore leans toward “stick without carrot”: they are willing to pay to deter wrongdoers but not to subsidize right-doers. This suggests a type who prioritizes personal payoff and norm enforcement over expressive generosity, viewing full contribution as the expected baseline rather than something that merits extra compensation.

They are willing to use reward, but far more selectively and less frequently than punishment. Reward is clearly tied to supporting cooperation and group morale: they reward a consistently cooperative peer who publicly commits to giving, and later reward several players when the main defector briefly contributes, including rewarding the former defector. This suggests they use reward to reinforce desirable changes (especially someone switching from defection to cooperation) and to acknowledge allies who back cooperative or punishment strategies. They do not appear to reward indiscriminately; most rounds they refrain, even when many others cooperate, implying that reward is a targeted tool rather than a routine habit.
