They also never experiment with extremely high or zero reward levels for extended stretches, implying a moderate, balanced orientation rather than extreme generosity or extreme self-interest. Overall, they are cooperative in both contribution and reward domains, but their rewarding is more context-sensitive and reciprocity-driven than their near-constant willingness to contribute.

This player never spends resources on rewarding others, despite having many opportunities and often sitting in cooperative groups where reward could further reinforce prosocial behavior. At the same time, they repeatedly receive reward units from others, sometimes in substantial amounts. They accept these benefits but do not reciprocate through the mechanism provided. From this pattern, it appears they separate cooperation from social reinforcement: they are willing to incur the cost of contributing to the public good itself but are unwilling to incur the additional cost of meta-cooperation via reward. They might see direct contribution as the “proper” channel for helping the group and view reward as unnecessary, inefficient, or strategically opaque—especially given that rewarders and recipients are anonymous and they cannot target specific individuals based on behavior. Their lack of rewarding does not change even when they are the target of generous rewards, suggesting that gratitude, if present, does not translate into costly reciprocal reward-giving. They also do not use the reward mechanism as a tool to shape others’ contributions; there is no observed attempt at strategic reinforcement. Overall, they are a pure contributor rather than a “social enforcer”: they invest in the public good itself but abstain from second-order cooperation via rewards.

The player never uses the reward mechanism, despite facing many opportunities where rewarding cooperators could, in principle, reinforce behavior or reciprocate benefits. This indicates a strong reluctance to spend private resources on rewarding others, even when the technology is favorable and despite personally receiving rewards on multiple occasions. Their non-use of rewards holds across contexts: when others cooperate a lot, when they themselves contribute, and when they free-ride. They also do not use rewards to build reciprocity or to signal gratitude for being rewarded. This pattern suggests that they either (a) discount the effectiveness of rewards as a coordination or incentive tool, (b) treat reward spending as an unattractive cost regardless of social benefits, or (c) prioritize personal payoff over prosocial signaling in this dimension. Because they sometimes cooperate but never spend on rewards, their social preference profile appears asymmetric: they are willing to bear private cost via contributions that benefit everyone (including themselves), but they are unwilling to bear private cost targeted at others’ payoffs. They thus behave as a non-rewarding, payoff-conscious cooperator: they may appreciate cooperation and group efficiency but do not invest in positive sanctions to shape others’ behavior. Their gratitude, if present, is not expressed through costly rewards.

This player is an active, sometimes heavy, rewarder who uses reward as a cooperative reinforcement tool rather than as a precise, efficiency-focused investment. They repeatedly spend substantial portions of their available payoff on rewarding others, including in situations where group cooperation is already high and where the personal cost of rewarding is significant. Their willingness to pay for others to benefit is strong and persists even when the direct financial return from doing so is uncertain or low. They do not appear to use reward in a tightly calibrated, strategic way aimed at maximizing their own future earnings; instead, their pattern fits a prosocial or norm-enforcing style, aiming to support or encourage contributors. They do not systematically withhold reward when others free-ride, but the available data do not reveal how they target specific players, so the exact trigger rules and recipient selection are unknown. Late in the game, they are willing to increase reward spending rather than conserve resources, implying that they treat reward as an expression of cooperative commitment or appreciation rather than a long-run investment tool. Overall, they behave like a principled cooperator who is prepared to incur personal costs to sustain or recognize contributions by others.

This player never uses the reward mechanism at any point, despite facing many situations where others contribute substantially and even where they themselves receive substantial rewards from the group. They consistently leave the reward option unused, which points to a stable, deliberate stance rather than oversight or confusion. Several interpretations are consistent with this pattern. First, they may have a strongly payoff-maximizing view of the reward tool, judging the cost of rewarding as not worth the benefit to others, even when reward is slightly socially efficient. Second, they may view contributions as sufficient expression of cooperation, seeing extra reward as unnecessary or strategically ineffective when rewarders and punishers are anonymous and outcomes are not fully visible. Third, they may abstain because they perceive no reliable way to direct reward toward “deserving” individuals given anonymity and noisy contribution patterns. What is clear from behavior is that gratitude or reciprocity, if present, is not expressed through costly reward. Even when they are themselves the target of generous rewarding by others, they do not reciprocate through the same channel. This suggests that they separate their cooperative norm (“I will usually contribute to the public good”) from any obligation to engage in second-order cooperation via reward. They rely on first-order contributions as their main cooperative act and treat the reward mechanism as dominated or irrelevant for their purposes. In a new environment, this type is expected to contribute to the public good with moderate reliability but to systematically ignore voluntary reward options, unless those options directly affect their own payoff in a clear and individually advantageous way.

This player is extremely reluctant to spend on rewards. In almost all rounds they choose not to allocate any reward units, despite frequently receiving rewards from others, sometimes in substantial amounts. This asymmetry—benefiting from rewards while rarely giving any—points to a strongly self-oriented payoff motive in the use of the reward mechanism. When they do reward, it is infrequent and low in magnitude. The few instances of spending on rewards occur in contexts where they themselves contribute. This pattern is most consistent with a conditional, targeted reinforcement strategy: they are willing to invest a small amount to support a cooperative configuration that they are currently part of. It is less consistent with unconditional gratitude or a norm-based sense of “thanking” contributors, because they do not reward in most of the many rounds where others contribute and they free-ride. They do not use rewards to systematically reshape others’ behavior: there is no consistent pattern of counteracting defectors (which is impossible directly via reward, but they also do not focus rewards in a way that clearly encourages the highest contributors on a regular basis). Instead, reward use is sporadic and seems opportunistic—activated when they themselves are cooperating and the environment is already cooperative. They appear to treat the reward tool chiefly as a minor, situational adjustment rather than a central strategic lever. The overall low frequency and low intensity of reward giving, combined with a willingness to accept large reward inflows without reciprocation, suggests that norms of reciprocity and gratitude are weak for this player, at least under anonymous, non-identifiable reward conditions. Their default is to conserve their own payoff rather than invest in sustaining cooperation through costly positive sanctions.

This player uses the reward mechanism actively and repeatedly. They are willing to pay the cost to transfer positive payoff to others, even in the absence of guaranteed identification or direct reciprocity. This indicates a genuine propensity for positive reciprocity and possibly a desire to support or stabilize cooperative behavior in the group. Reward usage is not constant across all rounds, but it recurs throughout the game, including both earlier and later phases. This suggests that rewarding is part of their core strategy, not a one-time experiment. Because they sometimes choose not to reward, their behavior appears selective rather than purely generous; they seem to deploy rewards in particular states of the game rather than indiscriminately. The likely trigger for rewarding is others’ cooperation. The player consistently sees a substantial number of high contributors, and they are often a full contributor themselves in rounds when they decide to incur reward costs. This pattern is consistent with a “reward high contributors / support the cooperative majority” heuristic. They appear willing to sacrifice some of their own payoff to encourage or reinforce group-level cooperation, even though rewarders and targets are anonymous and there is no guaranteed direct return. They also appear comfortable with asymmetric flows of reward: they sometimes receive sizable reward from others and sometimes very little, yet they continue to use rewards themselves. This suggests they are not strictly conditionally altruistic in a narrow “only reward if I am rewarded” sense. Instead, they behave more like a pro-cooperative supporter, willing to give positive reinforcement without requiring one-to-one reciprocity. Strategic sophistication is moderate. They recognize that rewards can shape incentives, but their pattern does not show tight, round-by-round tit-for-tat responsiveness. They do not obviously shut down reward giving in response to later free-riding by some players, nor do they ramp it up mechanically when they personally gain more from others’ rewards. Rather, they appear to follow a relatively stable internal rule: occasionally invest in positive incentives to sustain a cooperative environment, while not making reward-giving an every-round obligation. In new PGG environments, this type tends to (a) contribute substantially, (b) selectively reward perceived cooperators, and (c) tolerate some imbalance between what they give and what they get, as long as the overall cooperative climate seems viable.

The player is an active and relatively generous rewarder, frequently spending a nontrivial portion of their payoff to reward others. They consistently use the reward mechanism across the game, which signals a strong inclination to reinforce cooperative behavior even at personal cost. This goes beyond minimal or token use; they repeatedly allocate meaningful amounts to reward. Their rewarding appears targeted toward perceived cooperators rather than non-contributors. They continue to reward in environments where many others are contributing, and their own contributions often coincide with substantial reward spending, suggesting an attempt to support and stabilize a cooperative coalition. There is no evidence that they use rewards to try to convert clear free-riders, nor that they reward in a way that would systematically favor low contributors; this pattern is more consistent with selective prosocial reinforcement than with indiscriminate generosity. The player also appears responsive to being rewarded: they sometimes receive substantial rewards from others and do not respond by reducing contributions or withdrawing; if anything, they maintain or increase cooperative and rewarding behavior after sizable incoming rewards. This is consistent with a gratitude or reciprocity motive—though this inference is of low confidence since individual reward flows are not attributable to specific peers. Their use of rewards is at least partly strategic. They invest in rewards throughout the repeated interaction, when influencing others’ future behavior is possible, and they cease spending on reward only in the final round, when there is no future to shape. This indicates that they likely view rewarding as a tool to sustain cooperation over time rather than as purely intrinsic giving. In summary, this player is a prosocial rewarder who uses the mechanism to reinforce cooperation among others, is willing to incur considerable costs to do so, and adjusts reward spending to the remaining horizon. Their reward behavior complements their contribution pattern: they act as a cooperative supporter who occasionally free-rides but generally invests both in the public good and in socially reinforcing others’ cooperative choices.

The player begins by not using the reward option despite being a high contributor who receives substantial rewards from others. Later, they start to spend a moderate to high amount on rewards in several rounds. Because reward decisions are not fully observable in the transcript, their exact targeting pattern is unknown. However, their timing aligns roughly with group norms that encourage mutual rewarding among contributors, suggesting they adopt “reward cooperators” once that norm seems established. They never use reward to retaliate or exclude, and there is no sign they withhold reward from free‑riders as a strategic signal; whether they deliberately avoid rewarding low contributors is unknown. The costliness of reward does not stop them from using it substantially in later phases; they appear willing to sacrifice individual payoff to reinforce positive behavior or reciprocate. Their pattern suggests a supportive, reciprocity‑oriented rewarder rather than a purely profit‑maximizing one, but the precise rule they follow (e.g., rewarding only full contributors vs. broadly rewarding many players) is uncertain.

They never spend anything on rewarding others, even in phases where nearly everyone else is heavily using the reward mechanism to support contributors. This is stable across the entire game. This reveals: - Strong reluctance to incur any personal cost for others’ benefit, even when cheap rewards are a common norm. - No apparent inclination toward strategic reinforcement of cooperation: they rely on others to carry the cost of norm‑supporting rewards and simply respond with their own contribution decisions. - No use of reward as gratitude signaling; they do not reciprocate or “thank” rewarders through the mechanism. They act as a pure consumer of the reward system: they accept incoming rewards but never participate in providing them.

They are a consistently high and repeated reward-giver, spending substantial amounts to reward others across many rounds. Their reward behavior appears structured by a self-imposed norm they articulate: reward everyone who “helped” once per round. In practice, they continue to reward heavily even when others reduce contributions or when reward patterns become noisy and sometimes asymmetric. Their willingness to spend is not narrowly payoff-maximizing, because the reward technology is individually inefficient and they use it extensively anyway. This suggests they use reward as a social/strategic tool: to reinforce contribution, to sustain a cooperative atmosphere, and to uphold a mutual-help norm. They do not visibly use reward to punish low contributors by exclusion in any systematic or verbalized way; their orientation is positive reinforcement rather than selective sanctioning.

They are willing to spend heavily on rewards in some phases despite personally never contributing, indicating that their free-riding on contributions is not simply “never pay any cost.” Early and mid-game, they spend substantial amounts to reward others, and at one point explicitly state that they reward everyone, consistent with a broad, non-discriminatory rewarding pattern. This suggests a “meta” strategy of being a non-contributor but occasionally a generous rewarder, possibly to maintain goodwill or to encourage continued group contributions that they themselves do not join. However, their reward activity is inconsistent: they alternate between heavy rewarding and no rewarding. When another player later points out that rewarding is individually unprofitable compared to not giving, they explicitly agree and then cease rewarding for a period, implying that they understand and can pivot based on cost-benefit reasoning. They never explicitly state their rule for whom to reward, but observed behavior is consistent with rewarding widely rather than selectively based on who contributed or who rewarded them. Their rewarding appears more like occasional prosocial spending or conformity to a perceived group “rewarding norm” than like a tightly optimized incentive strategy.

The player is willing to spend on rewarding others, but their pattern is adaptive and somewhat cost-sensitive. They initially participate in rewarding cooperators, including following group suggestions to spread reward broadly among contributors. They explicitly notice that rewarding can reduce their own net payoff relative to not rewarding, and this realization leads them to sometimes cut back or skip rewarding, especially after seeing that high reward expenditure can be individually costly. They still resume rewarding in later stages, especially when coordination around contribution is strong or when giving help to specific others (e.g., after helping someone understand the interface), suggesting that they use reward both as strategic reinforcement of cooperation and as a social gesture. Their reward behavior reflects a tension between cooperative reinforcement and self-interest: they do not blindly reward every round, but they remain generally supportive of rewarding contributors when it seems collectively beneficial or socially appropriate.

The player is willing to spend very large amounts on rewards in some rounds and nothing in others, indicating both capacity for strong positive reciprocity and episodes of complete non-reward. They sometimes invest heavily in rewarding others even when it is personally very costly relative to the payoff they end up with, which suggests that supporting or reinforcing cooperation can matter to them more than immediate profit in those moments. At other times, they choose not to reward at all, even when many group members contribute fully and others are actively rewarding. This pattern implies that their reward usage is volatile and not governed by a simple, stable rule. Their reward allocations are likely aimed at cooperators (given the group norm and their own contributions in those same phases), but their exact targeting strategy is unknown. They do not use reward to sanction defectors indirectly (by excluding them from rewards) in any clearly consistent, easily inferable way; whether they deliberately withhold reward from low contributors is unknown. They do not discuss reward strategy in chat, so their motives—whether gratitude, norm enforcement, or experimental play—are uncertain. Overall, they act as a “bursty positive rewarder”: occasionally extremely generous in reinforcing cooperation, but not reliably so.

They are willing but highly selective rewarders. Across the game, they reward only once, doing so in a relatively substantial, one-off burst. This reward event occurs in a context where most players are contributing heavily and several others are also engaging in broad, generous rewarding. Their behavior here looks like participation in a positive reciprocity episode: they join the wave of rewarding when cooperation is high and others are actively rewarding contributors. Outside that episode, they consistently choose not to spend on reward, even when many others continue to reward cooperators and when reward is clearly being used by others to sustain cooperation. This indicates that they do not treat reward as an ongoing, norm-enforcing obligation. Instead, they appear to view rewarding as optional and situational: they might reward to align with a temporarily strong cooperative climate, but once that moment passes or once they have personally benefited enough, they revert to self-focused payoff maximization. They never use reward to steer defectors or to “buy back” trust after having free-ridden; they also do not use reward to systematically reciprocate those who have rewarded them (beyond that one burst). Their triggers for rewarding are therefore best described as: “high cooperation plus signaling alignment with the rewarding majority,” rather than stable, strategic enforcement of contribution norms. Strategically, their reward use is low-frequency, non-punitive, and weakly tied to long-term group welfare; it serves more as a temporary cooperative gesture than as a consistent instrument of social control or reciprocity.

This player is a heavy and persistent rewarder. In most rounds, they spend a substantial portion of their post‑contribution endowment on rewarding others, and they do so even when the cost‑to‑benefit ratio of the reward mechanism is unfavorable. They continue this pattern across the game, only pausing reward use in a very low‑reward round and in the final round. Their spending level indicates they prioritize reinforcing cooperation and expressing prosocial intent over maximizing their own payoffs. They seem willing to incur repeated personal cost to sustain a cooperative atmosphere and to support high contributors. Their reward behavior is consistent with a “strong positive reciprocity” or “peer‑supporter” type rather than a narrow payoff‑maximizer.

This player is willing to spend nontrivial amounts on rewards, including in rounds where they themselves are not contributing. Their use of rewards shows two main motives: (1) a desire to reciprocate or “make up for” earlier confusion or non‑rewarding, and (2) an attempt to support and encourage cooperators, even at a personal cost. They sometimes spend heavily on rewards despite the relatively inefficient reward technology (more cost than benefit per unit), implying that maintaining cooperative norms and signaling goodwill matters to them alongside payoff maximization. They are not purely strategic in a narrow payoff sense; they show a readiness to incur costs to support others once they understand the mechanism. Their reward behavior appears somewhat unstructured rather than finely targeted or contingent: they do not explicitly announce detailed tit‑for‑tat schemes or selective rewarding tied to individual past contributions. Instead, they lean on broad “reward those who helped” norms, following others’ suggestions and trying to participate in reciprocal exchange more generally.

The player never uses the reward option, even when the partner shifts from defection to consistent cooperation. This indicates a reluctance to spend resources on positive reinforcement, perhaps because they view cooperation as a baseline expectation that should not be subsidized, or because they weight material payoffs over prosocial signaling via rewards. They also do not appear to use rewards strategically to build alliances or secure future cooperation; instead, they rely on punishment and their own contribution pattern to manage behavior. Overall, they function as a “punishment-only” norm enforcer rather than a combined punisher–rewarder.

The player never uses the reward option despite multiple opportunities and despite long stretches where the partner contributes maximally and does not punish. This indicates a very low propensity for positive reciprocity via material rewards. Even when the partner behaves cooperatively and refrains from sanctions, the player does not invest to reinforce or acknowledge this behavior. Their social control toolkit is one-sided: they are willing to impose costly punishment but unwilling to spend even minimal resources to reward. This pattern is consistent with a type that views the game primarily through a payoffs-and-grievances lens, not through gratitude or alliance-building. It also suggests that if they wish to signal approval or cooperation, they prefer to do so via their own contribution choices rather than through explicit monetary rewards.

This player never uses the reward mechanism, even though others frequently contribute fully and sometimes reward them. They forgo chances to positively reinforce cooperative partners, despite the relatively symmetric cost/benefit of reward. This suggests they either see reward as strategically useless, too costly, or simply not worth the effort. Their non-rewarding holds both when others are clearly more cooperative than they are and when they themselves are benefiting substantially from others’ generosity. They show no attempt to reciprocate rewards they receive, no effort to encourage high contributors, and no use of reward as a signaling or alliance-building tool. Their approach to the reward stage is essentially passive and self-focused: keep all own coins rather than invest in social capital or future behavior shaping.

The player is an unusually active user of costly rewards, even though the reward technology is payoff-neutral (they pay as much as the recipient gains). They frequently reward high contributors and very rarely reward non-contributors. This pattern strongly indicates that they use rewards as positive reinforcement for cooperation rather than as random gifts. They also sometimes reward the chronic non-contributor, but this only happens in specific contexts where that player has just contributed or where the player seems to be experimenting with “carrot” rather than “stick.” These moments suggest a dual strategy: they are willing to try both punishment and reward with the same person, perhaps to see which instrument is more effective in shifting behavior, or to signal that improvement will be acknowledged. Their rewards to the consistently cooperative peer are especially frequent and sustained. This is likely both gratitude signaling and forward-looking reinforcement: they want to solidify that person’s cooperative role, maintaining an implicit alliance around public-good provision. Because they reward even when it clearly decreases their own payoff and when cooperation by that peer is already stable, the motive does not appear purely strategic; there seems to be an intrinsic preference for reciprocating “good behavior” and recognizing fairness. Importantly, the player is comfortable combining reward and punishment in the same period across different targets. This shows they compartmentalize reactions: they positively reinforce one individual while simultaneously sanctioning another. They are not globally “nice” or globally “harsh,” but context-sensitive, with distinct evaluative tracks for each peer.

The player is willing to reward, but does so rarely and only toward a consistently cooperative partner. They never reward the persistent free-rider, even when that player contributes in some rounds, which implies that they reserve positive reinforcement for partners who demonstrate sustained or reliable cooperation over time. The size of their rewards is minimal; they use single units rather than stacking multiple units, consistent with a symbolic “thank you” or recognition rather than a strong attempt to shift incentives. Their rewards are not tightly coupled to particular payoff gains but appear to affirm aligned behavior: they reward a peer who matches their full-contribution standard, including after that peer has previously cooperated reliably or returned to cooperation. They do not use rewards tactically to induce a free-rider to improve, so their reward usage is more like selective appreciation than a deliberate incentive scheme.

They never use rewards, despite repeated opportunities and despite the presence of clear high contributors whose behavior could be positively reinforced. This suggests either that they have a strong preference for punishment-based enforcement over positive reciprocity, or that they view rewarding as unnecessary, inefficient, or less salient than punishing defectors. They prioritize spending coins on negative sanctions rather than on bonuses for cooperators, even when cooperation is widespread. There is no indication of strategic use of rewards to build alliances or encourage conditional cooperation; their enforcement style is purely punitive. Whether they conceptually approve of rewards but consider them dominated in this specific environment (e.g., due to cost symmetry with punishment) is unknown; all that is observed is a consistent absence of rewarding behavior.

The player never uses the reward option, even when there are consistent high contributors and even when they themselves benefit from others’ cooperation. This suggests a strong reluctance to spend resources on positive reinforcement. They do not appear to seek to build alliances or signal gratitude via costly rewards. Their cooperative moves are not accompanied by attempts to shape others’ behavior through incentives; instead, they treat contribution and redistribution as the main levers of interaction. This may reflect a cost-minimizing mindset, indifference to direct reciprocity tools, or uncertainty about the strategic value of rewarding; which of these is primary is unknown.

This player never spends to reward others despite repeatedly observing high contributions by several peers and personally receiving rewards themselves. Their pattern is thus “cooperate but do not positively sanction,” distinguishing their prosocial contributions from prosocial incentives. They appear unwilling to incur additional personal costs beyond contribution, even to support or reinforce cooperative partners. This could reflect a belief that contribution is already sufficient as their cooperative duty, a preference to conserve resources for themselves once the public fund is determined, or skepticism about the efficacy or necessity of rewards. Their sole use of the sanctioning system is punitive, and even that is minimal, indicating they treat the reward/punish mechanism cautiously and only employ it when they feel a strong reason; that reason is never positive reinforcement in this game.

The player never uses the reward option, despite receiving positive rewards themselves. This indicates a reluctance to spend resources to benefit others, even when others have behaved cooperatively or have rewarded them. They do not engage in strategic positive reinforcement of high contributors, and they do not reciprocate rewards in a direct, targeted way. This non-use is stable across cooperative and non-cooperative personal strategies: they withhold rewards regardless of whether they are contributing fully or free riding. That pattern suggests that their cooperative behavior is driven more by contribution choices than by active use of the incentive mechanism. They rely on adjusting their own contributions rather than shaping others’ behavior with rewards. Because they never reward, it is unknown whether they conceptually value rewarding fairness or generosity but consider it too costly, or whether they simply prioritize personal payoff and see reward as dominated by keeping coins. From observed behavior alone, they are best considered a non-rewarder who does not invest in alliance-building or norm encouragement via positive incentives.

They never use the reward mechanism despite being surrounded by increasingly high contributors. They do not reward the players who consistently contribute fully, even though they clearly observe who is cooperative. This indicates that they are not inclined to spend personal resources to encourage or reciprocate prosocial behavior. They do not practice strategic rewarding to stabilize cooperation or to build alliances. Overall, their use of the optional mechanisms is purely self-interested: they employ neither reward nor punishment, conserving all coins for themselves.

The player never uses reward, even though they interact repeatedly with high contributors and themselves receive multiple rewards later. This indicates a clear preference for negative over positive incentives. They seem to regard contribution as an expected duty, not something that merits paying personal cost to praise or subsidize. Their willingness to spend on punishment but not on reward implies they view social control primarily through the lens of sanctioning defectors rather than reinforcing cooperators. Strategic use of reward to build alliances or secure future cooperation is absent; they prioritize conserving resources for self and for punishing norm violators rather than spending to encourage already-good behavior.

The player is willing to spend on rewards and uses them selectively to reinforce cooperative behavior. They reward high contributors while still free‑riding themselves, which indicates a strategic or norm‑signaling use of rewards rather than purely reciprocal gratitude. At times they reward multiple cooperative peers simultaneously, suggesting they care about broadly supporting the cooperative cluster rather than forming a narrow dyadic alliance. The magnitude of rewards remains small relative to potential payoff, consistent with a cautious or budget‑conscious approach: they acknowledge and support cooperation but do not invest heavily in it. They never combine reward with simultaneous punishment, preferring exclusively positive sanctioning. Overall, they act as a low‑intensity positive reciprocator and norm supporter: using reward to encourage cooperation, including cooperation they are benefitting from without yet fully matching.

This player is highly willing to reward and uses reward as a central tool of social regulation. They systematically direct rewards toward those who contribute fully or substantially, and they withhold rewards from clear free-riders. Their use of reward is both generous and costly: they accept large personal payoff reductions to provide positive incentives, suggesting that reinforcing cooperation and expressing approval of prosocial behavior is a key part of their strategy. Reward is not random generosity; it is patterned, proportional, and linked to observed contribution behavior. When a previously low contributor improves, this player is willing to grant them substantial rewards, indicating a strong focus on encouraging change and acknowledging better behavior rather than locking in reputations. The reward behavior appears both strategic (aimed at sustaining or increasing group cooperation) and expressive (signaling gratitude and support to cooperators), even when the practical impact on future rounds is limited by the short remaining horizon.

They never use the reward mechanism, despite the presence of other clear high contributors and despite receiving rewards themselves. This indicates that they separate their own contribution norm from active pro‑social reinforcement. They appear unwilling to incur extra cost even to benefit cooperators, suggesting that while they internalize a norm of contributing, they do not extend that norm to second‑order cooperation (rewarding others). It is unknown whether this reflects a belief that contributions are their own responsibility, a desire to conserve resources, or low attention to the incentive mechanism. What is clear is that they do not use rewards strategically to build alliances or condition future group behavior.

They are willing to use rewards in a broad, egalitarian way but are inconsistent over time. On some occasions they reward all cooperating peers equally, clearly treating reward as a symmetric group-level bonus rather than as targeted reciprocity. On many other occasions they give no rewards at all, even when others discuss how mutual rewards could increase everyone’s earnings. Their rewarding appears driven by episodic generosity or experiments rather than by a tight, ongoing strategy. They do not appear to condition rewards on specific individuals’ deviations, since peers’ contribution levels are almost always identical and high and they reward everyone symmetrically when they do reward. They also do not respond directly to exhortations that “everyone has to” give rewards to make the scheme profitable; they sometimes comply with that logic and sometimes ignore it, suggesting that they prioritize simplicity and avoiding cognitive or coordination burden over fully exploiting the reward technology. Their final statement that they will join a mutual reward plan, without actually rewarding in that last decision, indicates either mis-timing, misunderstanding, or very low salience of reward decisions relative to contributions. Overall, they are a cooperative, non-punitive player who treats rewards as optional, occasional goodwill rather than as a central strategic tool.

They are selectively but genuinely willing to reward, and they understand the positive efficiency of mutual rewarding. Early on, they experiment with broad, near-uniform rewards across the cooperative group, accepting a short-term personal cost to reinforce a fully cooperative environment. Over time, they reduce reward usage, possibly because others do not consistently reciprocate or because the group already maintains full contributions without it. When someone expresses feeling disadvantaged by others’ lack of rewarding, they respond both verbally and behaviorally by directing additional rewards to that person, which functions as targeted support and acknowledgment. Near the end, they explicitly advocate for a coordinated, symmetric reward scheme that would increase everyone’s payoff if universally adopted, showing that they view rewards as a tool for mutual gain rather than as gifts or favoritism. They do not use rewards strategically to manipulate defectors; instead, they reinforce cooperators and try to organize group-level reward norms.

They are an active but selective user of rewards, with a clear preference for reinforcing cooperation and reciprocating kindness. They reward only others, never themselves (self-reward is not possible), and they never pair rewards with punishment. Early in the interaction, they explicitly commit to rewarding after another player notes having given rewards without receiving any, and they follow through by distributing rewards broadly across cooperative group members. Over time, they alternate between periods of extensive, almost egalitarian rewarding of many or all contributors and periods of no rewarding, even while everyone continues to cooperate. They frequently include players who have been communicative or who express concern about being left out of rewards, and they occasionally target subsets rather than the entire group, suggesting a mix of reciprocity, gratitude, and perhaps an attempt at payoff balancing for specific individuals. They show some understanding of the underlying payoff logic, recognizing in chat that mutual rewarding can increase earnings if everyone participates, and they restate that this only works under full mutual compliance. However, they do not consistently enforce or follow that mutual-reward scheme once it becomes clear that not everyone always rewards; instead, their rewarding becomes more sporadic and selectively generous rather than strictly payoff-maximizing. Overall, they function as a prosocial rewarder: willing to spend their own payoff to thank, include, or support others, more motivated by reinforcing cooperation and fairness perceptions than by precise strategic optimization.

They are an active, often generous rewarder, using rewards as a primary strategic and social tool. Early on, they reward broadly across cooperative partners, sometimes covering nearly everyone, and they do this even when it makes them temporarily worse off in net coins. They openly express disappointment when others do not reward them back and explicitly state that this asymmetry caused them to lose relative to others, indicating that they track payoff consequences and care about reciprocity. Over time, their reward pattern becomes more selective and episodic: they alternate between rewarding many partners, rewarding only a subset, and occasionally not rewarding at all. These adjustments correlate with their expressed concern about “losing” when others do not reciprocate, implying that they condition their generosity on perceived group reciprocity and coordination. They clearly understand the efficiency logic of symmetric rewarding and even articulate a simple “if everyone gives, we all earn more” calculation. In practice, they sometimes withhold rewards when they believe not everyone is participating or when they feel under‑rewarded, then resume rewarding after mutual assurances from others. Their use of rewards thus mixes altruistic reinforcement of cooperation, desire for fairness and reciprocity, and explicit payoff optimization. They never use rewards to single out defectors for exclusion; instead, they mostly direct rewards toward consistently contributing partners, occasionally omitting or including different subsets without obvious hostility. Overall, they are a “pro‑social reciprocator with positive reinforcement bias”: willing to spend to reward cooperation, but gradually sensitive to whether the reciprocal reward norm is actually being followed.

They also never use rewards, despite repeated periods where others are fully cooperative and despite group discussion about using mutual rewards to raise earnings. This shows an unwillingness to spend personal resources to positively reinforce others, even when the group frames symmetrical mutual rewarding as profitable. They receive nontrivial amounts of reward from others and sometimes acknowledge group plans verbally in a supportive way, but they do not reciprocate via the mechanism itself. This combination—accepting rewards, continuing to cooperate, but not giving rewards—suggests a type who values cooperation enough to contribute fully, but either does not internalize the additional norm of active reciprocity through costly reward, or evaluates the reward mechanism as not worth its cost. Their non-use persists even when others explicitly revisit and endorse mutual rewarding near the end, which indicates that it is a stable preference rather than inattention. Overall, they are a high contributor who free-rides on others’ reward efforts: they benefit from others’ generosity without engaging in strategic reinforcement themselves, and they rely on basic contribution norms rather than incentive engineering to sustain cooperation.

They are a generous and sometimes heavy user of rewards, with a clear pro-social orientation. They repeatedly spend substantial amounts to reward multiple or all other players, especially when group contributions remain maximally cooperative. They favor broad, symmetric reward distributions: when they do reward, they typically reward everyone who is cooperating, not selectively favoring particular partners. This implies a strong preference for fairness and group-wide reinforcement of the high-contribution norm, rather than forming cliques or reciprocating only towards those who rewarded them. They do occasionally vary their reward intensity, sometimes giving nothing, sometimes rewarding a subset (notably a player who had previously complained about not receiving rewards), which suggests some responsiveness to others’ expressed experiences of fairness and loss. They also engage in reward even when it is personally costly and not clearly profit-maximizing unless others also reward; nonetheless, they do not insist or coordinate aggressively around mutual rewarding, and at times decline to reward even when others argue it would raise group payoffs. Overall, they use rewards as a cooperative signal and as positive reinforcement for norm adherence, but they do not rigidly reward every round and appear to balance cost considerations with their desire to be fair and supportive.

They are a high, sometimes extreme, rewarder with a strong inclination toward inclusive generosity. They frequently spend significant resources to give positive incentives to others, often distributing rewards broadly and symmetrically across all cooperating peers. This behavior is consistent with a strategy of reinforcing the fully cooperative equilibrium and signaling goodwill rather than maximizing immediate personal payoff. At several points they experiment with giving no rewards or selectively rewarding just one or a few partners, suggesting some sensitivity to their own net earnings and to whether others are also rewarding. However, they repeatedly return to wide, almost egalitarian reward distributions, and explicitly describe themselves as giving to everyone. They do not appear to condition rewards on fine-grained differences in others’ behavior; as long as others are contributing fully and broadly aligned with the group, they tend to treat them similarly. The single defector receives no reward and instead is punished, showing that rewards are reserved for cooperators. Overall, they use reward as a primary tool to sustain cooperation and express solidarity, accepting short-run personal costs in exchange for maintaining a generous, high-trust environment.

They are a heavy, sometimes extreme, user of rewards. Often they spend a substantial portion of their net payoff on rewarding others, even when it leaves them with relatively low earnings. They repeatedly choose to incur costs to reinforce others’ behavior, indicating a strong willingness to use positive incentives as a central tool. Although exact recipient patterns are unknown from the summary, the timing and persistence of their spending suggest they primarily aim to support cooperators and possibly high contributors, rather than behaving randomly. They do not appear to target or discriminate via non-reward against specific low contributors; instead, their main lever is actively giving out rewards rather than withholding them. Their reward usage is not purely altruistic in payoff terms—they also receive substantial rewards in many rounds—but they continue to reward in rounds where reciprocation is uncertain or where some players clearly destroy their own payoffs, indicating that they are not just maximizing a narrow return on reward expenditures. Overall, they treat the reward mechanism as a normative and relational instrument: something one “should” use to sustain cooperation, even at personal cost.

They are a frequent and heavy user of reward, often spending a large portion of their payoff to grant others relatively modest net benefits. They are willing to incur substantial personal cost to reward others, even when it leaves them with very low payoffs, so they are clearly not narrowly payoff-maximizing. The primary apparent trigger for rewarding is contribution level: they systematically direct more reward toward players who are consistently contributing high or full amounts, and they tend to withhold or minimize reward for persistently low contributors. They sometimes continue rewarding even in environments where many others are cutting back or where they themselves are not being generously rewarded, suggesting that they treat reward partly as a normative signal (“this is good behavior”) rather than a purely strategic incentive. However, their reward intensity fluctuates: there are periods where they reward many targets heavily and periods where they reward no one, which may reflect fatigue, reassessment of effectiveness, or temporary attempts to conserve payoff, though the exact motive is unknown.

This player is a persistent, relatively heavy user of the reward mechanism. They spend on reward in most rounds, sometimes at substantial personal cost that materially reduces their short-run payoff. This indicates a willingness to pay to shape others’ behavior or to express approval, not just to maximize immediate income. The exact recipient pattern is not directly observable, but timing suggests they tend to reward players who contribute highly and/or who also reward others, especially in earlier and mid-game phases when cooperation is strong. The fact that they keep rewarding even when others’ reward patterns become noisy or when they themselves receive little or no reward in a given round suggests intrinsic preference for norm reinforcement rather than purely reciprocal tit-for-tat. They sometimes spend quite aggressively on reward in situations where average contributions or others’ payoffs are uneven, which may reflect attempts to stabilize cooperation or support perceived “good citizens” in the group. At other times, they scale back reward or even skip it, which indicates some sensitivity to cost and outcomes; they do not blindly reward every round. Overall, they use reward both as a social signal and an instrument, prioritizing encouragement of cooperation over short-run payoff maximization.

This player uses the reward mechanism sparingly and in small amounts. They almost never invest large portions of their payoff into rewarding others, generally spending either nothing or a minimal amount. They do, however, use reward consistently across many rounds, so the choice to reward is deliberate rather than accidental or one-off. Their reward is not obviously proportional to contribution level or perfectly targeted at the highest contributors. Instead, they appear to treat reward as a low-intensity, occasional nudge rather than a strong incentive system. They do not attempt to become a central reward hub or to enforce a norm by heavy spending; they preserve their own payoff and only “tip” selectively. The triggers for their rewards are somewhat opaque. At times they reward when the group is highly cooperative, suggesting a desire to reinforce cooperation. At other times they also reward when contribution patterns or reward behavior among others are noisy. There is no clear evidence that they heavily reward only those who have rewarded them personally; any reciprocity is weak or at least not easily distinguished from general, light prosociality. Because individual reward targets are not visible in the transcript beyond aggregates, the specific target selection is unknown. In strategic terms, they behave like a cost-conscious, light rewarder: they are willing to sacrifice a little to support or acknowledge cooperation but are unwilling to bear substantial costs to enforce norms. They do not rely on reward as their primary tool; they mainly adjust their own contribution level.

The player is a frequent and fairly heavy user of rewards, willing to pay nontrivial costs to grant others bonus coins. They do not restrict rewards to a minimal, one‑off signal; instead, they allocate meaningful amounts in many rounds. Their rewards are not tightly “price‑optimized”: they sometimes spend enough to noticeably lower their own payoff, which indicates a willingness to sacrifice earnings to reinforce desired behavior or norms. The primary trigger for rewarding appears to be cooperative contribution rather than overall payoff level. They consistently face a group with multiple high contributors, and they continue rewarding in that environment rather than sharply discriminating only the very top cooperators. There is no evidence that they systematically target low contributors with “positive incentives” (e.g., rewarding defectors to bring them in); their spending pattern fits better with rewarding those who already behave cooperatively. Their use of rewards does not collapse when some others begin to free‑ride or when reward patterns turn chaotic. This persistence suggests an internalized preference for prosocial reinforcement or norm expression, not purely instrumental use of rewards for maximizing near‑term income.

They are a frequent and substantial user of the reward mechanism, often spending a non-trivial share of their payoff to grant rewards. They reliably employ reward across almost all rounds, including early and late phases, indicating a stable preference for positive reinforcement over self-maximization. The pattern strongly suggests that they direct rewards primarily toward high contributors and consistent cooperators, using reward as a normative signal rather than random generosity. They do not appear to systematically “buy” profit-maximizing alliances; instead, they accept reduced payoffs in order to support behaviors they deem cooperative. They continue to reward even when overall group cooperation fluctuates and even when their own net payoff in a round is relatively low, so their willingness to reward is not tightly contingent on being personally ahead. They also show no evidence of using reward to retaliate (e.g., to “crowd out” others by wasting their budgets), focusing instead on reinforcing desired behavior. Overall, they act as a pro-social rewarder who values shaping the group environment, even at a personal cost.

This player is a light and selective rewarder. Most rounds they choose not to spend on rewards at all; when they do, they spend modest amounts rather than engaging in large-scale redistribution. Their reward decisions are infrequent, suggesting they do not see themselves as a primary enforcer of cooperative norms. When they do reward, they appear to use it as a targeted signal rather than as a routine payment—likely directed toward players who contribute well or who have been important for their own earnings. They are willing to incur costs for reward even when it substantially reduces their own payoff in that round, which implies that they value influence, relationship signaling, or norm support, but only in specific circumstances. They do not display a clear pattern of “rewarding everyone who cooperates”; instead, their use of reward is sparse, strategic, and situational. There is no evidence that they use reward to deliberately subsidize low contributors toward higher future cooperation.

They are a willing but selective rewarder. They frequently spend on reward, sometimes at substantial personal cost, and sometimes not at all. This pattern suggests that they use reward as a tool rather than a fixed habit. They appear more likely to reward when their own contribution is high; they rarely combine free-riding with heavy reward spending. They focus their reward spending in rounds where cooperative norms are relatively strong, as if reinforcing an already-cooperative cluster rather than trying to “buy” cooperation in a very noisy or low-contribution environment. They tolerate significant personal payoff reduction from rewarding in some rounds, which implies that they value norm reinforcement and/or relationship signaling over immediate earnings. However, there are also many rounds where they reward minimally or not at all despite others contributing; they are not mechanically pro-social. Their reward use looks contingent on their current emotional or evaluative state about the group (e.g., satisfaction with recent interaction patterns) rather than on a strict tit-for-tat rule.

The player is a frequent and substantial user of positive reward. They routinely spend a nontrivial portion of their available payoff to reward others, often in multiple units. This occurs both when reward is commonly used by others and when some players reduce or stop rewarding, implying that they have a strong internal norm for active reinforcement rather than merely following the crowd. Their trigger for rewarding appears primarily contribution-based: they reward those who contribute high amounts to the public good and withhold (or sharply limit) reward toward chronic low contributors. They sometimes maintain reward spending even when it does not immediately pay off in personal terms, indicating that they value the act of reinforcing cooperation at the group level or maintaining a pro-social identity. They also display some responsiveness to overall reward ecology: when others around them are very erratic or use reward in ways that look wasteful or self-harming, they reduce their own spending in certain stretches, suggesting a partial strategic check on costs. Nonetheless, they never fully abandon reward; they oscillate between “heavy rewarder” and “moderate rewarder” modes rather than turning reward off. This blend points to a type who genuinely endorses positive reciprocity but is not totally blind to cost.

The player is generally reluctant to spend on reward, and when they do, they use it sparingly and inconsistently. Many rounds pass with no reward from them, even when others are contributing a lot and engaging heavily in mutual rewarding. Occasional reward expenditures appear, but not at large or systematic levels, and they do not show a stable pattern of reinforcing particular partners or consistently rewarding top contributors. This indicates that they value keeping their own payoff more than paying a personal cost to shape others’ behavior, even though the reward technology is socially efficient. Their rare reward purchases may reflect either momentary gratitude or experimentation with the reward mechanism rather than a settled strategy of incentivizing cooperation. There is no clear evidence that they use reward strategically to build coalitions or to signal norms; their main stance is to free-ride on others’ rewarding activity.

The player uses reward occasionally but not consistently, and sometimes very intensely at substantial personal cost. They are willing to spend large fractions of their payoff on reward in some rounds, then spend nothing in others. This suggests they view reward as a tactical tool rather than a standing moral obligation. They primarily use reward to reinforce high or norm‑compliant contributors and to respond to others’ rewarding patterns, but their reward behavior is noisy rather than rule‑based. There are episodes where they sacrifice much of their own earnings to distribute reward widely, implying that, in some states of the game, they temporarily prioritize influencing others’ behavior or reciprocating generosity over maximizing their own immediate payoff. At other times they abstain from rewarding entirely while continuing to accept large amounts of reward from others, which shows a willingness to free‑ride specifically on the reward mechanism itself. They do not systematically “tip” every cooperator; instead, they selectively reward in bursts, likely when they sense a cooperative coalition is worth supporting or when they feel gratitude toward recent benefactors. Their reward use is therefore best described as strategic, reactive, and inconsistent, rather than purely altruistic or purely self‑interested.

The player is a persistent, fairly generous reward user, willing to spend substantial amounts across most of the game. They regularly incur nontrivial costs to give positive payoffs to others, including in periods where they themself are not being especially favored with reward. They do not appear to condition reward tightly on marginal changes in others’ contributions; instead, their reward behavior tracks a broad pattern of supporting generally high-contributing participants. They do not use reward in a sharply strategic, targeted way (e.g., systematically rewarding new or wavering cooperators, or pivoting reward away from those who reduce contributions), and there are stretches where they either reward very little or not at all without a clear structural reason. This points to a prosocial but somewhat noisy rule: they are willing to “chip in” via reward to maintain a cooperative climate, but their targeting and timing are not finely optimized. They do not appear to use reward as a precise tool to equalize payoffs or punish quasi-defectors; instead, it functions as broad encouragement rather than calibrated control.

They never use the reward mechanism at any point, despite frequent opportunities and despite others consistently contributing at the same high level as they do. This indicates a strong reluctance to spend own resources to provide direct incentives or expressions of gratitude, at least under a one-to-one cost-benefit structure where rewarding does not increase social surplus. Their non-use of rewards does not appear retaliatory or selective; it is a blanket strategy. This is consistent with a payoff-conscious cooperative type who sees reward as individually costly and socially neutral in this implementation, and therefore unnecessary. It may also reflect a belief that stable high cooperation among peers does not require additional reinforcement, or a preference for “silent” cooperation without extra signals. Their behavior provides no evidence of using reward as a tool for alliance-building, norm enforcement, or selective encouragement.

The player never uses the available reward mechanism despite consistently high contributions from others and a highly cooperative environment. This suggests a strong preference for conserving personal resources over engaging in costly positive reciprocity; they appear to believe that explicit rewards are unnecessary when cooperative norms are already in place. They do not use rewards strategically to stabilize cooperation or to encourage specific deviators back to the norm, even when minor deviations occur. This may indicate either that they see contribution behavior itself as sufficient mutual support, or that they do not integrate the reward tool into their strategy at all. Their non-use of rewards implies low propensity to engage in second-order cooperation (paying a cost to reinforce cooperative behavior) and a tendency to rely on direct contribution choices rather than meta-level incentives. Their behavior gives no evidence of gratitude signaling through rewards, nor of targeted reinforcement, so their reward strategy is best characterized as consistently inactive or unused.

They consistently refrain from using the reward mechanism. Despite being in a highly cooperative group where every other visible player contributes at the top level for the entire game, they never spend anything to reward others. This holds even when their own contribution decisions could make them appear less generous; they still do not offset that with rewards. This indicates a strongly payoff-maximizing stance toward reward: they are not willing to sacrifice even one unit of their own payoff to reinforce or thank high contributors, even though reward is one-to-one and clearly targeted. They rely entirely on contribution choices and do not use the reward tool as a strategic lever to maintain cooperation or build allies. They also do not try to compensate others for their own free-riding periods. Their lack of rewarding cannot be explained by absence of deserving targets, since peers are reliably cooperating. The most consistent interpretation is that they prioritize preserving their own coins over any prosocial or strategic value from rewarding. Whether they conceptually see reward as ineffective, unnecessary, or simply too costly is unknown; the observed behavior aligns with a “non-rewarding, efficiency-seeking opportunist” type.

The player almost never uses the reward mechanism, despite frequent opportunities and repeated high cooperation by others. On the one occasion they do spend on reward, they simultaneously receive an equal amount of reward from someone else. Because the system is symmetric in cost and benefit, this act is net-costly and not individually profitable, so it is unlikely to be purely self-interested. However, with only a single instance and no information about the specific target, their underlying rule is uncertain. Possible interpretations include: a tentative or experimental use of the mechanism; a one-off attempt at positive reciprocity; or a weak tendency to reinforce cooperative norms. The very low frequency of reward usage indicates that, in practice, they rely primarily on direct contribution rather than on incentive engineering. They do not appear to systematically reward high contributors, to punish low contributors via withheld reward, or to structure long-run incentives. Their default is non-rewarding cooperation, with at most a minimal, sporadic use of rewards.

The player never uses the reward mechanism, even when others are clearly cooperating at high and stable levels and when rewarders are identifiable to recipients. This indicates a strong reluctance to spend personal resources on direct positive incentives or gratitude payments, even in a fully cooperative environment. Their strategy appears to be: contribute maximally, but do not pay extra to influence or thank others. This could reflect several possibilities (all with low confidence): they may view contribution itself as sufficient cooperation; they may not see additional benefit from rewarding in an already cooperative group; they may be conserving payoff; or they may be indifferent or uncomfortable with active, targeted social interventions. There is no evidence that their decision to withhold reward is punitive; it is uniformly applied and not contingent on others’ actions. They are thus best described as a “non-rewarding cooperator”: they help via public good contributions but do not invest in second-order cooperation through reward.

This player almost never uses the reward mechanism and appears highly reluctant to spend on rewarding. Across repeated opportunities, they generally choose not to transfer any of their payoff to others, despite facing multiple peers who contribute at the same maximal level as they do. They do not systematically reward high cooperators, even when those cooperators are clearly identifiable and stable. On a single occasion, they engage with the reward system in a trivial, payoff-neutral way that results in them both spending and receiving the same small amount. Given the structure of the game and the data available, the direction of that transfer is ambiguous: it may be self-rewarding if that is allowed, or a tiny reward to someone else who simultaneously rewards them. There is no clear pattern of them using reward to reinforce others’ cooperation, nor to build alliances or express gratitude. If they do reward someone else in that instance, it is minimal and does not reflect an ongoing strategy. Overall, they behave as if reward is either strategically unimportant, too costly relative to its benefit, or simply not salient. They do not appear to use it as a tool to shape behavior, to reciprocate, or to signal approval in any systematic way. Their default is effectively to ignore the reward option.

They never use rewards, despite persistent and often very high contributions by others. They ignore opportunities to positively reinforce cooperative partners or to signal gratitude or alliance. Given that rewards are relatively inefficient, this could reflect a payoff-focused calculation, but the absence of any token or symbolic reward also points to very low concern for prosocial signaling or relational strategies. They neither strategically reward to shape future behavior nor altruistically reward to acknowledge others’ generosity.

The player is selectively but repeatedly willing to reward others, despite the reward mechanism being efficiency-reducing.
