This consistent absence of reward behavior suggests that they either (a) place very low value on expressing gratitude or reinforcing cooperation at a personal cost, or (b) view the reward mechanism as too inefficient to justify use, regardless of how cooperative others are. They behave as though any positive transfer that reduces their own payoff is unacceptable, even if it might encourage beneficial group behavior. They likewise do not seem to use reward instrumentally at the end of the game (where there is no future cooperation to affect anyway), which is consistent with them not valuing reputation‑like or expressive aspects of rewarding in this anonymous, one‑shot‑per‑partner context. Overall, they are “non‑rewarding” in both normative and strategic senses: they neither repay kindness at a cost to themselves nor attempt to buy higher future contributions. Any warm‑glow or reciprocity motives are either absent or too weak to overcome their focus on retaining coins.

This player never uses the reward option. They refrain from rewarding even clearly high contributors, even when group cooperation is strong and several others appear to reward or support cooperative peers. They do not use small “symbolic” rewards, nor do they selectively reward exceptionally generous behavior. Given that rewards are relatively inefficient in this setup, their abstention may partly reflect a payoff calculus: sacrificing own coins for a smaller benefit to others is unattractive. However, their choice not to reward persists even in periods when their own payoff is high and the group behaves cooperatively, which suggests either a general aversion to spending on second-order mechanisms or a preference to express approval purely through their own contributions rather than via explicit bonuses. There is no evidence that they attempt strategic reinforcement of others’ behavior; they do not reward those who align contributions with them or those who move from very low to higher contributions. Nor do they “thank” others for high cooperation with rewards after being helped by their generosity. Gratitude, if present, is expressed only indirectly through continuing to contribute at cooperative levels. They therefore present as a no-reward type: they neither punish nor reward and appear to treat both mechanisms as dominated tools compared with simply adjusting their own contribution strategy.

The player also never uses the reward option, even though high contributions and some use of rewards by others are repeatedly observable. They do not engage in gratitude signaling toward generous group members, nor do they attempt strategic reinforcement of high contributors who are effectively subsidizing their own payoffs. Given that the reward technology is strictly payoff-inefficient (more costly than it benefits the recipient), their non-use is consistent with a narrow payoff-maximizing orientation. They show no willingness to pay to encourage future cooperation, even when they themselves free-ride heavily and clearly benefit from others’ generosity. Their stance is symmetric across punishment and reward: they avoid all costly social interventions. This suggests a stable trait of “non-interventionism under cost”: they do not try to shape others’ behavior via sanctions or incentives, and instead adapt (or simply ride along) with whatever pattern emerges.

The player never uses the costly reward option, even when others contribute very generously or when some peers appear to reward them. This suggests that they perceive the reward technology as inefficient (the cost per unit of benefit is worse than for punishment) or not worth the sacrifice of private payoff. They may also believe that high contributions are simply what is expected under the norm, and thus do not merit additional positive reinforcement. Their complete avoidance of reward while being willing to punish indicates an asymmetry: they rely on negative sanctions to discourage free riding but do not invest in positive incentives to encourage high cooperation. This points to a “punish-but-don’t-reward” enforcement style, focused more on deterring norm violations than on expressing gratitude or strategically strengthening alliances. Because they never reward, they do not visibly reciprocate rewards given to them; if others grant them bonus coins, it does not lead to a change in their use of the reward tool. Any internal sense of gratitude, if present, does not translate into costly positive sanctioning behavior in this environment.

The player is typically frugal with rewards and does not habitually use positive incentives. For most of the game, they refrain from rewarding even when others contribute very generously. This suggests that they prioritize conserving their own payoff over maintaining cooperation via costly rewards, especially given that the reward technology is efficiency-reducing. When they do reward, it is targeted and occurs only once, directed at peers who simultaneously provide very high contributions. This indicates that the trigger for rewarding is clear, above-average generosity relative to the rest of the group, not mere conformity to a moderate norm. Their reward use appears intentional rather than random: they choose exactly the players whose contributions stand out as most cooperative at that moment. Given that rewards are more expensive than the benefit they bestow, their willingness to use them at all suggests some value placed on reciprocating or reinforcing high cooperation, even when it is not individually optimal in narrow monetary terms. However, they do not continue this pattern in later opportunities, implying that they either do not see reward as an effective long-term tool or that they view that one rewarding episode as sufficient acknowledgment. Their reward behavior appears more like a one-time expression of appreciation or a brief attempt at strategic reinforcement of high-contributing peers than a systematic, ongoing policy. They do not reward consistent medium contributors, nor do they balance reward with punishment in a clear “carrot-and-stick” strategy. Overall, they are a mostly non-rewarding player with a capacity for selective positive reciprocity toward conspicuous cooperators.

This player almost never rewards; they explicitly choose no reward for most rounds despite seeing many cases where others contribute substantially. When they finally do use reward, it is targeted and limited rather than broad or proportional to contributions. Their rare use of reward appears to be a positive signal to specific co-players whose behavior currently aligns with a desired cooperative pattern. When the group is widely cooperative, they selectively reward a subset rather than the entire cooperative coalition, suggesting they value targeted reinforcement or alliance-style signaling more than generalized reciprocity. They do not seem to weigh the efficiency of reward heavily: given that reward is relatively inefficient compared to punishment, they nonetheless spend on it in at least one situation where cooperation is already high. This implies that the motive is not purely instrumental in terms of group payoff; it more likely reflects a desire to acknowledge or favor certain partners, or to experiment with whether extra encouragement will stabilize their preferred configuration. Because they pass up many opportunities to reward sustained high contributors, they are not a “gratitude maximizer” or a consistent reciprocity type. Reward is used sparingly, almost as a special tool when someone’s behavior stands out or the player wants to differentiate between co-players. Overall, they are a “selective, low-frequency rewarder”: reward is secondary in their toolbox, used mainly for occasional positive reinforcement or alliance-like gestures rather than as a primary device for sustaining cooperation.

They also never use the reward option. Even when surrounded by many consistently high contributors, and even when some others lavishly reward group members, they do not spend a single coin to positively reinforce good behavior. They likewise show no evidence of using reward strategically to build a coalition, reduce the chance of being punished, or foster reciprocity. This indicates a general reluctance to spend resources for the benefit of others, whether through positive or negative sanctions. Reward does not appear to function for them as a gratitude signal, alliance tool, or investment in future cooperation. In terms of type, they resemble a “pure free-rider on the sanctioning system”: they let others bear the cost of shaping group behavior while they simply adjust their own action levels. Their lack of reward behavior also suggests that prosocial emotions like gratitude or admiration, if present, are not strong enough (or not salient enough) to override narrow payoff considerations in this environment.

They almost never use the reward option, despite the fact that rewarding is more “efficient” than punishing in this setup. When they do receive rewards from others, they do not reciprocate via reward or visibly shift their behavior toward rewarding. This reveals a strong asymmetry: they focus on negative sanctioning rather than positive reinforcement. The absence of rewarding behavior in the presence of many high contributors suggests they are not trying to cultivate a reputation as a generous norm supporter, nor are they using rewards as a strategic tool to stabilize cooperation. Instead, they rely almost exclusively on their own high contributions to “do their part,” and on punishment to correct norm violations. Gratitude signaling through reward is weak or nonexistent; if they appreciate others’ high contributions or rewards to them, it is not expressed in their actions. In new environments, this type is therefore unlikely to invest in reward mechanisms, even when they are cost-effective, and will not typically use rewards to encourage borderline or improving contributors. Their cooperative norm is enforced primarily via their own contribution choices and occasional harsh punishment, not via positive incentives.

This player uses rewards sparingly and in a highly targeted fashion. They reward in three types of situations: initially, to someone who both cooperates modestly and engages with the sanctioning/ rewarding system; later, to a player who spends extreme amounts on prosocial sanctioning; and occasionally as a small, repeated bonus to that same highly engaged actor. The pattern suggests that they use rewards to recognize and reinforce what they see as “meta-cooperative” behavior: individuals who expend substantial resources on punishment or reward in ways that appear intended to manage the group. They are willing to spend considerably on rewarding such a player, even when that player’s own payoffs are very low, consistent with valuing their role in shaping the group more than their immediate earnings. They do not broadly reward high contributors in general, nor do they distribute rewards proportionally to contribution levels. This indicates that they are not a “fairness equalizer” who tries to top-up all strong cooperators. Instead, they display a selective “sponsor the enforcer/organizer” style of rewarding, reflecting a belief that influencing key actors is more effective than paying many small gratitude tokens. They show no signs of tit-for-tat rewarding in response to being rewarded themselves; their reward decisions do not track who has previously rewarded them. Gratitude or reciprocity toward direct benefactors is therefore weak or absent; reward is primarily strategic and role-based, not interpersonal.

This player never spends on rewards, even though reward is relatively efficient in this environment and many peers actively use it. They receive rewards in several rounds but do not reciprocate with reward spending. This implies that gratitude, if felt, does not translate into costly positive reinforcement. It may also indicate a narrow efficiency focus: they may prefer to let their high contribution itself be their cooperative act, rather than layering additional costs on top. Alternatively, they might not view targeted rewarding as necessary given that the group already sustains high contributions without their intervention. In any case, they behave as a “pure contributor” who supports the public good via their own contributions only, not via incentivizing others.

Reward is this player’s main intervention tool. They actively spend to positively reinforce high and consistent contributors and, in some cases, peers who combine solid contributions with ongoing prosocial sanctioning or rewarding activity. They repeatedly reward a subset of highly cooperative partners, indicating that they track others’ behavioral patterns over time and build a mental list of “reliable cooperators.” Their rewards are selective rather than evenly spread: they do not reward all contributors, but focus on those whose behavior either exemplifies strong contribution norms or helps stabilize cooperation through additional actions. This makes their reward use both normative (thanking good cooperators) and strategic (supporting those whose presence makes the environment better for them). They are willing to pay a nontrivial fraction of their payoff to send these positive signals, even when they themselves are being punished or facing others’ extreme sanctioning behavior. This consistency suggests that they treat reward as a tool for relationship‑like signaling in an anonymous setting: showing gratitude and encouragement to sustain a cooperative core, rather than as a short‑term payoff-maximizing choice. They do not appear to reward out of fear or bribery; instead, they reward after observing behavior they deem genuinely cooperative.

They never use rewards, despite seeing many others spend heavily to reward cooperators. This suggests they prioritize securing and preserving their own payoff over investing in long-run reciprocity through positive incentives, even when the reward technology is relatively favorable. They do not appear to “tip” high contributors or repay those who benefit them indirectly. Their giving is thus not coupled to a reinforcement strategy aimed at stabilizing cooperation via rewards. The consistent absence of rewarding behavior indicates that, for them, cooperation is its own decision problem: they contribute what seems appropriate without additionally paying to shape others’ behavior. They likely view rewarding as optional or inefficient, or they may believe that the prevailing high-contribution norm is strong enough without their intervention. Gratitude, if felt, is not expressed in costly transfers. In type terms, they are a “non-rewarding conditional cooperator”: they follow cooperative norms but do not invest extra to strengthen them.

This player also never uses reward, despite the reward mechanism being relatively efficient and heavily used by some other participants. They receive rewards multiple times yet do not reciprocate or join in rewarding high contributors. This suggests either indifference to gratitude signaling, a preference not to intervene in others’ outcomes, or a very strict cost-minimizing stance with respect to second-order actions (even prosocial ones). There is no evidence of targeted reinforcement of high contributors, coalition-building via mutual rewarding, or attempts to steer group behavior. Their strategy regarding reward is purely passive: they accept rewards but never spend to give them, even when many others do. As a type, they are a non-rewarder who decouples their own cooperation from any second-order prosocial signaling.

They never reward anyone, despite consistently high contributions from many group members and extensive reward activity by others. They do not use reward to encourage cooperators, to signal gratitude, or to build alliances or goodwill that might protect them from punishment. The reward technology is relatively efficient, but they still refrain from ever using it, suggesting that any value they place on others’ welfare, reciprocity, or strategic reputation-building is outweighed by their desire to conserve own payoff each round. They do not appear to adopt a “carrot-and-stick” strategy at all; instead, they ignore the reward channel and treat others’ rewarding behavior as irrelevant for their own actions.

This player is also very restrained with rewards. They almost never spend on rewarding, even though several others repeatedly contribute at the maximum level and some players clearly invest heavily in rewarding others. The one explicit reward they give is directed to a high contributor who aligns with the cooperative norm, and occurs in the same brief episode as their lone punishment action. This pattern suggests they conceptually support positive reinforcement of cooperation but are highly cost-conscious. They do not behave like an unconditional prosocial rewarder; they appear to treat reward as a scarce resource to be used only when cooperation is particularly salient or when they are simultaneously engaged in norm signaling via punishment. They do not systematically reward those who reward them or the group, so there is no clear gratitude or reciprocity loop in their reward use. Instead, they send a single, straightforward “approval” signal toward an obvious cooperator, then revert to keeping all remaining resources. Strategic reinforcement is minimal; if they have a reinforcement strategy at all, it is one-off and symbolic rather than sustained.

This player consistently refrains from rewarding others. They never spend coins to increase others’ payoffs, even when some peers contribute very generously or spend heavily to reward or punish. This suggests that, in their cost-benefit calculus, positive reinforcement is not worth the personal expense, even though it is technologically more efficient than punishment. Several interpretations are consistent with this pattern: they may believe cooperation can be sustained via baseline conditional contributions and punishment alone; they may view rewards as too easily misallocated (since rewarders are anonymous and could be rewarding “undeserving” players); or they may simply prioritize keeping their own payoff high and regard rewarding as a luxury. Because they do not reward even high, stable contributors, they do not signal gratitude in the payoff stage. Their notion of fairness seems more about sanctioning negative deviations than about compensating or applauding positive ones. In new environments, this type continues to rely on contribution choices and selective punishment to shape behavior, not on explicit positive reinforcement. Overall, they are a “non-rewarding enforcer”: comfortable spending to punish norm violators, but averse to spending to reward cooperators, regardless of contribution level.

This player also never uses the reward mechanism, despite the fact that rewards are relatively efficient compared to punishment and many others invest heavily in rewarding. They abstain even when they themselves receive rewards, indicating they do not reciprocate via the mechanism. Their behavior suggests they do not treat the reward tool as a means of encouraging cooperation or thanking high contributors. They maintain high contributions without using positive reinforcement, indicating they rely on their own contribution standard rather than on incentivizing others. The consistent non-use of rewards implies either a general reluctance to spend on others beyond contributions, a belief that good behavior should not require side payments, or uncertainty or disinterest about managing others’ incentives. The combination of high contributions and zero rewarding fits a “quiet cooperator” or “non-interventionist cooperator” profile: they help through their own actions but do not invest in shaping others’ behavior. There is no evidence of strategic rewarding to build alliances, attract future favors, or nudge specific players. Their stance is effectively: contribute generously to the public good, but do not spend extra to privately subsidize or steer individuals.

This player never uses reward, despite repeated exposure to others spending substantial resources on rewarding high contributors and sometimes even low contributors. They do not reward those who match or exceed their own contribution level, nor do they reciprocate when they themselves receive rewards. This indicates that they do not adopt a “positive reinforcement” strategy, even though rewards are more efficient than punishment in this environment. Their cooperative contributions therefore do not depend on using incentives to shape others’ behavior, and they forego opportunities to express gratitude or strategically encourage allies. The decision may be driven by a preference to avoid all second-stage interventions, by a narrow payoff focus at that stage, or by skepticism that small, anonymous rewards can meaningfully coordinate behavior; which of these is primary is unknown.

They show a willingness to reward, but this is even more selective than their punishment. They almost never reward in a diffuse way, even though many others contribute highly; instead, they direct rewards toward a small set of individuals at specific times. Some of these recipients contribute zero or very little in those periods, which suggests that the rewards are not strictly “thank you for contributing” signals. More likely, reward is used to support or encourage players who have been heavily punished or who they believe are being treated unfairly, or to reciprocate perceived earlier kindness from those players. They also combine reward with punishment in the same decision stage, directing opposite incentives to different individuals, consistent with a player who uses the incentive tools to shape the social landscape according to their personal judgments rather than following a simple “reward high contributors, punish low contributors” rule. They do not use rewards systematically to boost overall cooperation; instead, they deploy them in targeted interventions when motivated. This makes them an occasional, relationship- or event-driven rewarder rather than a general prosocial incentivizer.

They are willing but very selective in using rewards. They reward only a few times across the game, never using it routinely. When they do reward, it aligns with high or notably cooperative contributions, and they target multiple such contributors in one instance, suggesting they use rewards to positively reinforce desirable behavior rather than randomly. They do not reward themselves and do not seem to reward low contributors or erratic behavior, indicating some discernment about who “deserves” a bonus. The overall volume of reward is low relative to what is available, pointing toward a cautious, cost-conscious stance: they accept short-term personal cost occasionally to strengthen cooperative partners, but they are not a strong altruist or a systematic rewarder. Their initial willingness to reward a player who was sharply punished by others indicates some inclination toward supportive or compensatory gestures, but this pattern does not become a repeated theme, so whether they are motivated by empathy or simply by recognizing helpful contributors remains uncertain. They do not appear to use rewards strategically to build alliances in a targeted, repeated way, given anonymity and low frequency of use.

They use rewards frequently but more selectively than punishment. They reward players who contribute at or near full cooperation and who seem to embody the cooperative standard they themselves follow. This includes both consistently high contributors and those who increase or maintain strong contributions over time. Their rewards are small in number compared to their punishments, implying that they treat rewarding as a complementary reinforcement mechanism rather than the primary lever of norm enforcement. They are willing to pay nontrivial costs to give extra payoffs to high contributors even when it is not obviously profitable for them individually, suggesting a preference for positively reinforcing group‑beneficial behavior. There is no strong evidence that they use reward in a narrowly strategic, coalition‑building manner; rather, they seem to reward based on observable contribution levels, not on whether a player has previously rewarded or protected them. This points to a principled “support the cooperators” stance rather than personalized reciprocity or gratitude signaling. Their pattern fits a type who tries to shape the environment with both “carrot and stick,” but whose main emphasis remains punishing low contribution.

This player is also a heavy, indiscriminate rewarder at several points, but in a clearly different style from their punishment. When they choose to reward, they spread small, equal rewards broadly across almost the entire group, including both high and low contributors, and even including clear defectors. This indicates that their rewarding is not tightly conditional on contribution level. Instead, it looks like an attempt to reset group relations, express goodwill, or compensate others for earlier harshness—rather than a precise incentive scheme to differentially reinforce good contributors. They are willing to incur very large costs to fund these across-the-board bonuses, again ignoring their own payoff. In those episodes, they do not co-target with punishment; they separate “reward phases” from “punishment phases,” suggesting episodic shifts between conciliatory and punitive modes. Because they reward many actors whose contributions are poor or erratic, their reward does not reliably implement a “strong reciprocity” strategy (reward high contributors, punish low contributors). Instead, it appears more like a prosocial or even guilt-driven gesture toward the whole group, perhaps to re-stabilize cooperation or to offset previous extreme sanctions. They show no evidence of rewarding selectively based on who previously rewarded them or punished them: no clear gratitude-for-specific-benefactor pattern. Reward is group-oriented, symmetric, and sometimes includes players who harmed them earlier. This implies they are less interested in bilateral reciprocity and more in shaping or symbolically expressing a group-level climate.

This player essentially does not use the reward mechanism. Despite repeatedly observing many high contributors and having the ability to reinforce their behavior at a one‑to‑one cost, they never choose to allocate positive rewards. They also do not respond with rewards after receiving small rewards themselves, so there is no evidence of reciprocal gratitude or attempt to build alliances via positive incentives. This pattern suggests either that they see cooperation as expected baseline behavior that does not merit extra compensation, or that they prioritize conserving their own payoff over using rewards as a strategic signal. It may also indicate a belief that punishment is more appropriate or more cost‑effective than reward for shaping behavior, though their limited use of punishment after the first intervention leaves this interpretation uncertain. In new environments, they are best characterized as a strong cooperator who relies primarily on personal contribution and, at most, occasional punishment of extreme free riders, not as a player who uses rewards to manage or encourage others.

This player never uses the available reward option, even though others occasionally reward them. Their lack of rewarding is not due to an absence of cooperative sentiment (since they consistently cooperate and sometimes punish free riders), but suggests they do not see costly positive reinforcement as necessary or worthwhile. They neither reciprocate rewards they receive nor systematically reward high contributors, indicating they do not prioritize gratitude signaling or relational reciprocity in this environment. Their normative tool of choice is negative rather than positive: they rely on punishment to uphold contribution norms and seem to treat cooperation itself as the expected baseline that does not merit extra compensation. Whether they would reward in richer or more transparent environments is unknown, but in this context they behave as a “non‑rewarding enforcer”: they focus resources on correcting norm violations rather than on encouraging already‑desirable behavior.

This player uses rewards sparingly and in a targeted, almost symbolic way. They are willing to incur small costs to reward high contributors, but they do not systematically reward every cooperator. Instead, they single out specific cooperative peers for small positive reinforcement, consistent with a preference for acknowledging or encouraging good behavior rather than subsidizing it broadly. Their rewards go only to players who behave in line with a strong cooperation norm (full contributors), and they never reward low contributors. This indicates a clear alignment of rewarding behavior with pro-social performance rather than strategic attempts to buy favor from defectors or to manipulate others’ expectations. Because the rewards are low in magnitude and infrequent, they appear more like expressions of approval or norm support than calculated investments in future reciprocity. Taken together with their punishment style, this creates a “carrot and stick but light-touch” profile: they are prepared to both sanction defectors and positively reinforce cooperators, but they are economical with both tools. They appear to value maintaining a cooperative climate and signaling norms, while still keeping personal costs of enforcement and encouragement relatively low.

This player never allocates rewards, even though they themselves receive positive rewards from others in some rounds. Their behavior suggests that they do not use the reward mechanism as a means of expressing gratitude, reinforcing cooperation, or building reciprocal ties. They maintain full cooperation without engaging in positive reciprocity via the reward channel, which implies that their cooperative motivation is not contingent on being recognized or thanked. The absence of rewarding may stem from a preference for simplicity, a belief that rewards are unnecessary when cooperation is already high, or a desire to conserve resources rather than redistribute them further; which of these applies is unknown. They also do not engage in strategic rewarding—there is no evidence of attempts to selectively reward high contributors to stabilize the cooperative norm. In sum, they are a “pure contributor”: they support the public good directly through their own contributions but abstain from both punitive and rewarding side-payments.

This player shows an early, active inclination to use reward as a positive reinforcement tool, then later becomes entirely inactive in reward use. Initially, they spend nontrivial resources to reward multiple peers, including both clear cooperators and at least one clear defector. This pattern indicates that their reward strategy is not purely meritocratic. Instead, they appear to be experimenting with reward as a way to shape behavior, signal goodwill, or maintain broad group cohesion. Reward choices suggest a mix of motives: they consistently reward multiple strong contributors, implying appreciation for cooperation and a desire to encourage it. At the same time, their inclusion of a low contributor in the reward set shows that they may be attempting to draw that player into the cooperative norm through positive incentives rather than punishment. This points toward a prosocial, inclusion-oriented style that prefers “pulling in” outliers rather than ostracizing them. Their later cessation of rewards, despite continued high cooperation in the group and even after they themselves resume contributing, suggests either learning that reward is not cost-effective, fatigue with the cognitive or resource cost of distributing rewards, or a strategic shift to conserving payoff once group behavior appears stable. There is no evidence of using rewards as personal reciprocity for being rewarded by others; they reward others even when they themselves do not receive rewards. Taken together, they are a front-loaded, positively oriented social engineer: initially willing to spend to encourage cooperation and inclusion via rewards, but quick to discontinue this investment once they reassess the cost-benefit or feel that the group dynamic no longer requires active reinforcement.

The player is highly willing to reward and often spends substantial resources on positive incentives. They reliably allocate rewards to several others, frequently to individuals who appear to contribute fully or stably. This indicates a clear tendency to positively reinforce apparent cooperators. In contrast to punishment, their rewards are more aligned with straightforward reciprocity and norm support: many rewarded players are strong contributors or appear to match the cooperation standard this player favors. They also repeat rewarding some names across multiple opportunities, suggesting they are building or sustaining “alliances” with reliably cooperative others, despite anonymity of who rewards whom. They uses reward both as gratitude and as strategic reinforcement: they spend even when there is no direct, guaranteed future payoff from those individuals, implying a preference for promoting a cooperative climate rather than narrowly maximizing their own round payoff. Their willingness to invest heavily in rewards indicates strong pro-social preferences and a belief that encouraging good behavior is at least as important as punishing bad behavior. Overall, they fit a “pro-social enforcer with noisy sanctions”: a player who always cooperates, frequently rewards cooperators, and sometimes punishes in a way that reflects an attempt to regulate group behavior, though their punishment criteria remain partially unclear and possibly exploratory.

This player never allocates resources to reward others, despite being a consistent full contributor and despite facing a mix of high and low contributors in the group. They therefore do not use positive incentives to reinforce cooperative behavior or to encourage low contributors to increase their participation. Their decision to avoid rewards is stable across the game and does not respond to experiencing small incoming rewards from others. This indicates that they do not engage in observable gratitude signaling or reciprocal rewarding, at least within the cost/benefit structure provided. It is unclear whether they conceptually value reciprocity or appreciation; all that is evident is a refusal to spend personal coins to express it. The player treats rewards similarly to punishment: as a tool they simply do not use, suggesting a general preference for “contribute-only” cooperation without active social sanctioning or reinforcement. In future PGG settings, they can be modeled as relying solely on their own cooperative contributions, not on reward mechanisms, to influence group outcomes.

They are an active, consistent, and comparatively heavy user of rewards, even while personally contributing little or nothing. They routinely allocate multiple reward units to others, targeting players who contribute to the public good and occasionally extending rewards to those whose past contributions were low but have recently increased, suggesting a reinforcement strategy. This pattern implies that they treat rewarding as an investment in sustaining group cooperation that they can subsequently exploit as a freerider. They reward across a wide set of cooperative peers rather than focusing narrowly on a single partner, which aligns with a group‑level, not dyadic, orientation. They do not condition rewards tightly on perfect cooperation; instead they seem to recognize and reinforce broad participation in the public good. Their willingness to spend significant sums on rewards, even in the absence of guaranteed reciprocity, indicates a genuine prosocial or “prosocial‑instrumental” preference: they want the cooperative system to persist, but are comfortable positioning themselves as a beneficiary rather than an equal contributor. Gratitude signaling is present in their recurrent rewarding of highly cooperative players, but they do not escalate amounts dramatically, suggesting they are managing cost while still expressing approval.

This player never uses costly rewards, even though most others cooperate at high levels and several peers match their own full-cooperation pattern. They therefore do not employ rewards to reinforce good behavior, build alliances, or signal gratitude. Their cooperative strategy is thus non-relational: they contribute fully but do not attempt to shape others’ incentives beyond their own contribution. The lack of any rewarding suggests either that they (a) do not see marginal rewards as necessary when cooperation is already high, (b) place priority on preserving their own payoff rather than spending on bonuses for others, or (c) adopt a minimalist interaction style—cooperate in the main game, but ignore auxiliary mechanisms. They also do not reciprocate when they themselves receive a net-positive transfer via the sanction stage; there is no observable “gratitude” response in the form of rewarding others. This indicates that their cooperative norm is internally driven rather than sustained by reciprocity expectations. Overall, they are a pure contributor who leaves the reward channel unused, relying solely on their own consistent cooperation rather than active social reinforcement.

This player does not actively use the reward mechanism at any point, despite being surrounded mostly by high contributors and despite receiving small rewards themselves. Their inaction toward rewarding cooperators suggests several possibilities: they may believe high contribution is simply the expected baseline and not something to be incentivized; they may prioritize keeping resources rather than spending them on positive reinforcement; or they may be unsure about the strategic value of rewards in this setting. There is no observable evidence of gratitude signaling or of attempts to strengthen an existing cooperative norm via positive incentives. The asymmetry between their willingness to use punishment in a targeted way and their complete abstention from rewarding indicates a sanction profile tilted toward negative enforcement of norms rather than positive reinforcement of good behavior. Whether they conceptually approve of rewarding but judge it as inefficient, or whether they hold a principle against using rewards in such games, is unknown.

This player never uses the reward option, despite being surrounded predominantly by high contributors. This is informative: they clearly value high contribution (as shown by their own behavior) but do not spend resources to positively reinforce it. The absence of rewarding suggests one or more of the following, all with low to moderate confidence: they may view high contribution as the baseline expectation that does not merit extra compensation; they may perceive reward as an inefficient use of resources relative to passive cooperation; or they may simply be conservative with any form of costly intervention, whether positive or negative. They do accept rewards from others without visible behavioral change, but their own actions remain unaffected; there is no shift toward reciprocating reward, and no attempt to “thank” or support likely rewarders through the mechanism. This indicates that gratitude signaling via the reward channel is not part of their strategy, or at least not salient enough to override their cost-avoidant stance on rewarding. In strategic terms, they behave as a “non-rewarding cooperator”: they contribute fully but rely on others, if anyone, to implement positive incentives. They appear to separate their contribution norm (“always contribute”) from their use of costly social tools (“use sparingly, only for clear punishment when necessary”). There is no evidence that they treat reward as a tool to engineer future group behavior or to build alliances. In sum, they are willing to incur small costs to punish blatant defection but not to reward cooperation, even when cooperation is widespread. Their social influence style is thus asymmetrical: mild, targeted negative enforcement, no active positive reinforcement.

Type label: “Non-rewarder benefiting from others’ generosity” The player never spends resources to reward others, despite repeatedly being in a strongly cooperative environment and repeatedly receiving positive transfers themselves. This indicates that they do not adopt reciprocal rewarding as a strategy, even when the group contains many high contributors and some apparent altruistic rewarders. They appear to treat received rewards as windfall gains rather than as obligations to reciprocate. There is no evidence that being rewarded makes them more likely to reward others; their use of the mechanism remains at zero throughout. This suggests that gratitude, if present, does not translate into active reinforcement behavior under these incentives. They also do not use rewards strategically to shape others’ future contributions, even though the environment is one where such strategies could plausibly matter. Their absence of rewarding behavior could stem from several possibilities: they may perceive rewarding as less efficient than punishment for maintaining cooperation, may consider that contributions already sufficiently recognize cooperative behavior, or may simply prioritize conserving their own payoff over positive reciprocity. Which of these is correct is unknown. In personality terms, they lean toward “silent beneficiary” rather than “prosocial reciprocator” in the reward domain: they cooperate often, occasionally punish defectors, but do not invest in active positive reinforcement of others, even when they profit from that reinforcement themselves.

This player is willing to use reward selectively and at personal cost, but not in a systematic, rule-based way. For several rounds, they contribute fully without rewarding anyone, even when many others also contribute fully. Only later do they begin to reward, and they do so in a targeted, low-intensity manner. They reward one peer who has been contributing fully and another peer who has been repeatedly free-riding. Because rewards and punishments are anonymous, these rewards cannot function as direct relational signals to known partners; instead, they serve as generic positive reinforcement or as an expressive act (e.g., signaling approval or encouragement in an impersonal way). Their sanctioning pattern (willingness to punish but not reward) is asymmetrical: they invest against norm violators but not in favor of norm followers, consistent with a personality type that emphasizes accountability and responsibility more than gratitude signaling or strategic bonding.

This pattern suggests a “pure contributor” profile: they are willing to invest in the public good itself but not in second-order instruments like rewards or punishments, possibly due to a desire to conserve personal resources, a belief that contribution alone is sufficient signaling, or a lack of interest in actively shaping others’ incentives.

The player never uses the reward option, despite being surrounded for most of the game by high contributors and full cooperators. They never use the reward option, despite it being available every round. They never spend on rewards, despite repeated opportunities and despite receiving rewards themselves multiple times. They are very conservative with rewards. For most of the game they spend nothing on rewarding others, even when cooperation is high and others sometimes invest nontrivially in rewards.
