This single reward looks like a deliberate signal of appreciation or reciprocity rather than a systematic strategy: they do not repeat it or expand into broader rewarding later, despite continued cooperation by the group. They thus behave as an extremely sparing rewarder: willing in principle to spend on a standout ally, but not using rewards as an ongoing tool of norm reinforcement or as part of the mutual-gain “everyone reward everyone” strategy. The fact that they verbally agree to a mutual-reward idea but then do not implement it indicates that convenience, speed, or cognitive simplicity matters more to them than squeezing out small additional gains through complex coordination.

They never spend coins on rewards, even when others start experimenting with rewarding high contributors and when coalitions of reward and punishment emerge. They remain strictly focused on contribution and payoff accumulation. Their refusal to reward is consistent both when everyone cooperates smoothly and when some group members use reward strategically. This suggests they do not adopt the “prosocial rewarding” role; they seem to rely on mutual cooperation itself, rather than extra incentives, to maintain norms. Whether this is driven by simple payoff maximization, a desire to keep the game fast and simple, or disinterest in the secondary mechanism is unclear. They also do not join proposed “everyone reward everyone” schemes, reinforcing a preference for the straightforward strategy over more complex conditional reward arrangements.

They are very sparing with rewards. Across the game they use reward only once, and target a player who had previously made strongly cooperative and norm-supporting contributions (including earlier appeals to fairness and anti-free-riding in chat). The timing suggests the reward is a “thanks/recognition” move toward a high-contributing team member, not a calculated attempt to create a bilateral alliance. They do not participate in any broad “everyone reward everyone” schemes, even when such a strategy is discussed. They also do not systematically reciprocate rewards that others receive (e.g., giving to the same people others are rewarding). Instead, their single reward looks like low-frequency, selective prosocial reinforcement rather than a core part of their payoff strategy. They appear to understand that reward is individually profitable if done symmetrically by all, but choose not to invest effort or take on coordination risk; they follow the emerging consensus in chat to “just max out” contributions and avoid complicating the game with reward schemes. Overall, reward for them is a minor, symbolic tool to acknowledge or encourage good behavior, not a main pillar of their gameplay.

They use rewards selectively and in concentrated bursts, and otherwise abstain. For most rounds, they do not reward anyone, preferring to conserve points and maintain speed. On two occasions they spend heavily on rewards, accepting extremely low personal payoffs in those rounds. One episode targets a previously punished low contributor with a very large positive transfer, alongside smaller rewards to consistent cooperators; another episode spreads small rewards across several consistently cooperative and/or active participants. This pattern suggests they use rewards to morally or socially “correct” perceived imbalances: they compensate those they see as over‑punished while also recognizing reliable contributors. The willingness to sacrifice substantial payoff in a single round shows a strong pro‑social or expressive motive rather than narrow payoff maximization. However, they do not adopt systematic ongoing rewarding, and when a group strategy of mutual rewarding is proposed, they explicitly decline, focusing instead on speed and reliability; this indicates they see reward as an occasional, discretionary gesture, not a standing strategic tool.

They are willing to use costly reward and do so in a targeted, prosocial reinforcement manner rather than symmetrically or for self-gain. When they first engage heavily with the sanctioning system, they both punish non-contributors and reward specific cooperators in the same decision, despite the net move being extremely costly to themselves. They choose as reward targets players who have been consistently cooperative and, in one case, have also joined in norm enforcement. Later, after a period of turbulence, they again use reward to positively recognize cooperators, including someone who had previously defected but then returned to full cooperation. This pattern indicates they view reward as a tool to reinforce group-supportive behavior and to stabilize norms, not just as reciprocal payback for personal favors. They never attempt broad “reward everybody” schemes or self-serving exchanges; rewards are selective, norm-relevant, and coupled with punishment of defectors. Once the group stabilizes at universal cooperation, they stop using reward, likely because the norm is already secure and the marginal effect of extra coins is low relative to cost.

They use rewards infrequently and in a targeted, prosocial manner. Almost the entire game passes with them rewarding no one, even when others start to distribute rewards liberally. When they do reward, they choose individuals who have either been fully cooperative or have corrected prior non‑cooperative behavior (e.g., a previous defector who later contributes fully, and a fully cooperative partner at the very end). This pattern suggests that they view rewards as a way to acknowledge sustained or improved cooperation, not as a broad, automatic redistribution tool. The late‑game reward to a previously punished defector can be interpreted, with moderate but not high confidence, as “forgiveness plus reinforcement”: once the target reliably cooperates, they are no longer treated as an outsider and even receive positive reinforcement. Across the game, they appear more willing to rely on stable contributions and occasional punishment than to finance extensive reward schemes; rewards are a secondary, selective tool rather than the core of their strategy.

They never allocate any rewards, including during phases when others use reward frequently and when the group discusses using reward as a positive-sum strategy. Even when it is pointed out that reward can be personally profitable if everyone uses it, they decline to engage, and they also argue against the complexity and limited gain of a fully coordinated reward rotation. This suggests a clear reluctance to spend resources to benefit others, even when it might be collectively (and potentially individually) advantageous. Their stance in the reward-discussion moments is framed around coordination difficulty and marginal gain, but their actual behavior—zero rewards across the entire game—supports a deeper pattern: they do not voluntarily invest in others’ outcomes via costly reward. They also do not engage in targeted gratitude or reciprocity through rewards (for example, they never reward those who consistently contributed or those who may have spared them punishment later). This fits a “non-rewarder” type: they rely on the base PGG mechanism for cooperation and do not supplement it with additional prosocial transfers.

They never spend any of their own coins to reward others, even though they recognize and verbally endorse the idea that full cooperation benefits everyone and are aware of the reward technology. They also receive several rewards from others but do not reciprocate with outgoing rewards. When a more complex mutual reward strategy is proposed, they quickly support continuing the simple “just max contributions” strategy and label it as safer, which suggests they are risk‑averse about coordinating on more intricate reward‑based schemes and focused on minimizing time/effort overhead. This combination suggests an “enforce‑but‑don’t‑subsidize” pattern: they will spend to punish defectors to protect the cooperative baseline, but they do not invest extra to additionally top‑up cooperators. Their aversion to using rewards appears driven by efficiency and simplicity considerations rather than hostility; they never argue against rewarding on moral grounds, they simply opt out.

They never reward anyone, despite many rounds where everyone cooperates and there are obvious candidates for positive reinforcement. Even when another player points out that the reward technology is privately profitable if everyone uses it, they still abstain. When mutual rewarding is discussed, they give a candid explanation: interacting with the extra buttons would “take forever” and slow the game down. They explicitly endorse ignoring the mechanism and just continuing with the simple “max contribution” strategy. This is a rare explicit statement of their underlying preference: minimizing extra interaction cost and game duration is more important to them than extracting an additional coin via coordinated rewards. They therefore appear not to be anti‑reward in principle, but anti‑complexity and anti‑friction. In practice, this makes them a non‑rewarder, even in a setting where reward is available, profitable in expectation under full coordination, and socially encouraged by some players. Functionally, they are a “minimal‑effort cooperator” with respect to incentive mechanisms: they provide their full contribution to the public good but do not invest further coins or time into formal reward structures.

They show one notable, costly episode of prosocial rewarding: in a single round they spend a substantial portion of their payoff on distributing positive rewards to several other high‑contributing, norm‑consistent players. This occurs when the group is already highly cooperative, and they themselves are a standard cooperator, so the rewards are not compensating for personal disadvantage but appear to reflect either gratitude, relationship‑building, or a desire to reinforce good behavior. Outside of that one large reward episode, they do not reward again, even though the environment remains cooperative and multiple discussions about reward efficiency occur later. This pattern suggests that rewarding is not part of a stable, round‑by‑round strategy but rather an occasional, perhaps emotionally motivated act—possibly triggered by feeling especially positive toward some peers or by a momentary wish to “give back.” They do not use reward as a direct coordination tool (e.g., to start a formal mutual‑reward rotation) and do not propose detailed reward schemes. Their later skepticism or reluctance around complex reward coordination (preferring to abandon the idea) is consistent with a type who can be generous but prefers simple norms and does not want to manage multi‑person reward structures for marginal gains.

They almost never use rewards, despite many chances and despite others experimenting with rewarding strategies. The sole exception is one instance where they reward a fully contributing group member who had just been both punished and rewarded by others. That target is not a defector; they are part of the cooperative majority. The player’s reward in that moment thus looks like a small, selective reinforcement or solidarity gesture toward someone who suffered deductions, rather than a systematic incentive scheme. The overall pattern is that they find the basic “everyone max contributes” equilibrium sufficient and do not spend cognitive or monetary resources on building a parallel reward network, even when it would be slightly profitable if perfectly coordinated. Their rare reward use suggests they only step in when a cooperator appears unfairly treated, and even then do so minimally.

They personally never spend coins on rewarding others, despite understanding the mechanism and even pointing out that, in principle, a coordinated mutual reward scheme could be profitable. They analyze the reward technology correctly (cost vs benefit) and openly suggest it as a possible group strategy, but they ultimately decide it is too risky because it is vulnerable to non-participation by even a single player and would advantage free-riders. This indicates they treat rewarding as a second-order collective action problem and refrain from unilateral generosity when there is no guarantee of group-wide reciprocation. Their lack of rewarding despite high cooperation from others suggests they prioritize payoff optimization and risk-avoidance over expressing gratitude through the reward tool.

They never spend any resources on rewarding others, despite being repeatedly rewarded themselves. This holds in high-cooperation phases and in mixed phases with partial defection by others. Their behavior suggests that they do not treat the reward mechanism as a useful strategic tool for sustaining cooperation, nor as an avenue for expressing gratitude. They appear content to free-ride on others’ willingness to fund positive incentives: they benefit from a cooperative environment and occasional rewards without making any direct material investment in reinforcing it. Whether this is driven by indifference to the reward mechanism, a deliberate payoff-maximizing choice, or simple inertia is unknown, but functionally they behave as a non-rewarder throughout.

They almost never spend on rewards. Across the game, they only pay for a reward once, and even then at the smallest possible level. They never engage in the sustained or costly rewarding patterns seen in some peers. There is no observable pattern of using rewards to encourage high contributors, to thank particular players, or to retaliate against defectors (through omission of reward) in any targeted or strategic way. Instead, they effectively free-ride on others’ willingness to shoulder the cost of rewarding while continuing to contribute maximally themselves. This profile fits a “cooperative but reward-frugal” type: they provide the public good but avoid spending additional private resources on social incentives, treating reward as largely unnecessary or not worth the cost at the given exchange rate.

The player never spends any of their own coins on rewards, across all cooperation levels and all stages of the game. They refrain from rewarding even when others clearly contribute fully and when some group members generously reward many cooperators. This indicates a strongly self-regarding approach to the reward mechanism: they are willing to contribute to the public good itself, but not to incur individual costs for targeted positive reinforcement. There is no sign of using rewards strategically to stabilize cooperation, to reciprocate, or to express gratitude. Their non-use of reward is consistent and unresponsive to context, suggesting either that they (a) view rewards as an inefficient or unnecessary expense, (b) do not pay close attention to or fully value the reward mechanism, or (c) have a simple policy of “contribute fully, never spend on extras.” Exact motivation is unknown, but the behavior is clearly that of a non-rewarder.

The player is an extreme, persistent positive rewarder. Across most rounds they spend substantial amounts on rewards, often far more than others, and in some rounds they allocate an amount that leaves them with notably reduced net payoffs. They spread rewards widely among contributors rather than building narrow alliances; many players receive at least small positive transfers. The pattern suggests they use rewards as a broad signal of appreciation and as a mechanism to support and stabilize full cooperation, not narrowly as a tactical exchange. They do not appear to condition rewards on having been rewarded themselves in that same round; instead, they reward people who cooperated, including at times players who were earlier suspected of confusion or partial free-riding once they start contributing. Their willingness to incur very large costs for modest or no direct return indicates a strong pro-social or “altruistic rewarder” orientation, valuing group outcomes and norms of mutual support more than own material payoff.

They are extremely reluctant to spend on rewards. Across the game they almost always spend nothing on rewarding others, even when cooperation is high and when they themselves are repeatedly rewarded. The single clear exception is one instance of very small-scale rewarding, which shows they understand and can use the mechanism but choose not to deploy it as a regular strategy. Their general pattern suggests they see contribution as their main cooperative act and do not feel a strong obligation to “pay back” or to subsidize others via the reward mechanism. They do not appear to use rewards to strategically shape behavior (e.g., to encourage defectors to cooperate or to form visible alliances); instead, they essentially free-ride on others’ costly reward-giving while maintaining full contributions. The motive behind the rare instance of rewarding is unclear (low confidence: possibly a mild gesture of appreciation or conformity to emerging norms around thanking a highly generous rewarder), but it does not evolve into a sustained practice.

They never spend any coins on reward, despite frequently receiving rewards from others. This indicates a strong reluctance to incur direct costs for social enforcement, even when the group is highly cooperative and the personal cost of small rewards would be low relative to their earnings. Their non-use of reward persists across early, middle, and late stages and across both cooperative and defecting environments, suggesting it is a stable trait rather than a transient oversight. They do not differentiate between high or low contributors with reward use because they abstain entirely. This pattern is consistent with a “free-rider on enforcement”: they benefit from others’ generous reward activity, which helps stabilize cooperation, but they choose not to invest their own resources into reinforcing that norm. There is no evidence they experiment with small, targeted rewards or use rewards in a strategic manner (e.g., to encourage specific players); the mechanism appears understood but ignored.

They never spend their own coins to reward others, despite repeated opportunities and despite many others using the reward mechanism extensively. This suggests they place a higher value on retaining their own payoff than on actively reinforcing others’ cooperation via costly signals. Their willingness to contribute but not to reward implies a distinction in their mind between “doing their part” (contributing) and “using extra resources to influence outcomes” (rewarding), with the latter apparently not part of their strategy. There is no sign that they strategically reward to shape others’ behavior or reciprocate; their approach to rewards is purely passive. Motivation for non-rewarding is unclear (could be payoff-maximizing, could be uncertainty about how/why to use rewards, or a belief that cooperation is already stable and does not need reinforcement), but their consistent zero spending suggests a stable trait rather than momentary oversight.

They are extremely frugal with rewards. For most of the game they spend nothing on rewarding, despite consistently high contributions by almost everyone. Only once do they spend any reward units at all, and even then they spend very little relative to others’ heavy rewarding. Their rare use of reward follows a period in which another player has been conspicuously generous in rewarding others; their reward spending at that point plausibly targets that generosity (low confidence, because recipient identities are not reported). They never build a broad reward strategy to reinforce maximal contributors, even though rewards are costless in efficiency terms (1-to-1 transfer) and could serve as social gratitude signals. This pattern suggests that they view reward as optional and non-essential: they rely on explicit communication and their own consistent cooperation, rather than costly positive incentives, to sustain group behavior. Their limited rewarding is more consistent with a minimal, perhaps symbolic, acknowledgment than with strategic reinforcement.

They never spend any resources on rewarding others, despite being repeatedly rewarded themselves and despite a clearly cooperative group. Across stable full‑cooperation phases, they continue to use zero reward. This consistent abstention suggests that they do not see rewarding as necessary or profitable, even for sustaining cooperation that benefits them. It also suggests a preference for conserving private payoffs once contributions are made: they are willing to sacrifice via contribution, but not via an additional, voluntary reward cost. They do not engage in strategic reinforcement or “tipping” behavior toward high cooperators or generous rewarders. Whether this is due to principled frugality (“I’ve already done my part by contributing max”), confusion, or indifference to reward mechanisms is unknown, but the pattern is stable.

They are generally reluctant to spend on rewards. Across the game they spend only small amounts on rewarding others, and in many rounds they give no rewards at all, even when almost everyone is cooperating at maximum. When they do reward, it is modest and spread, which fits a limited, possibly symbolic use of reward rather than a strong strategic investment to shape behavior. Given that others are already near-universally cooperative and there is no punishment, they may see little need to spend to enforce norms. They are also a frequent recipient of small rewards from others, but they do not respond by matching that generosity on a large scale. Their reward behavior looks more like “occasional thanks or conformity to a minimal norm of acknowledging cooperators” than a deliberate tool for incentivizing group behavior.

They never spend any of their own coins on rewards, despite receiving substantial rewards from others and observing a norm where several players frequently and generously reward full contributors. This zero-reward pattern is stable across the game and holds even when rewarding would be low-risk and strongly norm-consistent. They thus free-ride not only on contributions but also on the reward mechanism itself, benefiting from others’ prosocial spending without reciprocating. Their implicit criterion appears to be: rewards are optional and personally costly, so they prefer to keep all of their payoff, letting others bear the cost of positive enforcement. This makes them a clear “reward free-rider”: cooperative enough in contributions much of the time to attract rewards, but unwilling to invest in maintaining the reward norm.

The player is a moderate, selective rewarder who clearly uses rewards but not to extremes. They are willing to incur personal cost to reward, especially when cooperation has been good but not perfect or when some players have defected. They do not adopt the very heavy, almost self‑sacrificial rewarding seen in at least one other player; instead, they spend a noticeable but contained amount across the game, including early and late phases. They never appear to use rewards punitively (e.g., withholding from defectors is the main “discipline” rather than over‑rewarding cooperators in a retaliatory sense), and they continue to reward even after others have had chances to free‑ride, suggesting a focus on reinforcing the cooperative majority rather than punishing the minority. Their pattern is consistent with a strategic reinforcement style: they are prepared to pay to “keep the group on track” but avoid excessive or flamboyant generosity.

This player is very restrained in using the reward mechanism. Across the game, they only rarely spend coins to reward others, despite receiving consistent rewards themselves from multiple group members. When they do reward, the amounts are small and not concentrated on any clear subset of partners, so there is little evidence of strong targeted reciprocity or alliance-building via rewards. Most of the time, they choose to keep their payoff rather than pay to reward, even in highly cooperative periods where nearly everyone contributes fully. Their primary tool for promoting cooperation is communication and social pressure, not costly material reinforcement. This suggests that they value efficiency in the sense of not burning payoff on low-leverage rewards, or that they may see rewards as less necessary once the group norm of full contribution is established. They do not emulate the extremely generous rewarders in the group, indicating they are not prone to “reward contagion” and instead maintain their own conservative reward strategy.

They never spend any coins on rewards, even though they repeatedly receive small amounts of reward from others. This is consistent and not sensitive to changes in group behavior: they refrain from rewarding when others defect and also when nearly everyone cooperates. There is no evidence they make targeted or symbolic rewards, even to especially generous rewarders or to players who coordinate communication. This suggests they either (a) view reward spending as inefficient or unnecessary given the already-high cooperation, (b) do not pay close attention to the reward mechanism, or (c) prioritize preserving their own payoff once they have committed to full contribution. Because they are otherwise highly cooperative, the lack of reward-giving does not seem motivated by hostility, but rather by an efficiency or “why spend extra” stance toward the costly reward mechanism.

They never spend anything on rewards despite repeatedly receiving modest rewards from others. Their payoff data show zero expenditure on rewards in all observed rounds, including those in which the group is unanimously cooperative and some players actively reward almost everyone. This indicates a consistently reward-averse stance, either because they see rewards as unnecessary, fail to attend to the reward mechanism, are aiming to maximize personal payoff, or do not feel a need to reciprocate materially. They do benefit from rewards, but do not reciprocate with their own coins even toward high-contributing or generous others. There is no evidence of selective or strategic rewarding (e.g., rewarding only cooperators, early movers, or previous rewarders); the mechanism is simply unused. Their type here is “non-rewarding cooperator”: willing to contribute to the public good but not to invest private resources to reinforce others’ behavior.

The player uses rewards sparingly and selectively. Most of the time, they choose not to spend on rewards, even when others contribute fully and even when some players are spending heavily to reward the group. On a few occasions, they do pay a small cost to reward others, especially in periods of apparent collective success and cohesion. This pattern suggests they view reward as optional positive reinforcement rather than a systematic tool: they are willing to acknowledge good group behavior but do not treat rewarding as a necessary or constant practice. They never use reward strategically to punish or exclude defectors (which is structurally impossible via reward, but they also do not withhold contributions or concentrate rewards in a punitive way). Their limited reward use points to moderate concern for signaling appreciation, but with a clear preference for preserving their own payoff over extensive rewarding.

They are consistently willing to spend on rewards, but at a moderate, bounded level. They reward in many rounds, but never at extreme, self-sacrificing levels; their spending on rewards is small compared to some hyper-generous peers. Their pattern suggests they use rewards as a tool for social reinforcement and appreciation rather than as a primary strategic lever. They do not seem to target or differentiate sharply among contributors, given that almost all others are cooperating; instead, their rewards function more like general “thank you” signals within a cooperative group. They explicitly compliment an especially generous rewarder in chat, which implies that their own reward-giving is partly motivated by gratitude norms: they like prosocial gestures and actively acknowledge them. Overall, they are positive but not extravagant in using rewards, balancing group acknowledgment with self-payoff preservation.

They are a highly willing and generous rewarder, using positive transfers in many rounds and sometimes to large sets of players. They reward both consistent high contributors and, at times, players whose contribution patterns are mixed, indicating that their reward rule is not strictly conditional on perfect cooperation. However, rewards are not completely indiscriminate: they tend to target players who are at least contributing in that round or participating in group-beneficial behavior, and they do not systematically reward chronic free-riders. Their use of reward is often broad rather than narrowly focused, suggesting a desire to reinforce a general cooperative climate rather than to craft precise bilateral relationships. They occasionally incur substantial personal cost to reward, even when their own payoff is significantly reduced as a result, showing that they prioritize positive reinforcement over individual earnings. This pattern is consistent with a “prosocial encourager” who views rewards as a tool to support group cooperation and express approval, rather than as a purely strategic investment in future payoffs.

They use reward sparingly and almost never as a primary tool. When they do reward, it is typically low-intensity and directed at multiple players rather than focused on a single clear ally. Given that the reward technology is inefficient relative to punishment, their limited use of it is consistent with payoff sensitivity. There is only weak evidence that they systematically reward high contributors; rewards appear more sporadic and sometimes coincide with rounds of broad sanctioning, suggesting a “carrot and stick” expression rather than a carefully calibrated incentive scheme. Overall, they are predominantly a punisher, with reward playing a minor, secondary role.

The player never uses the reward mechanism at any point, despite the presence of stable high cooperators and other players who consistently contribute. They do not engage in reciprocal rewarding after being rewarded themselves. Their single episode of active sanctioning uses only punishment, not reward. This indicates that they do not rely on positive incentives as a tool, either for gratitude or for strategic reinforcement of cooperative partners. It may reflect a belief that the marginal impact of reward is too low relative to its cost, a general reluctance to intervene in others’ payoffs, or a focus on their own contribution as their primary cooperative act.

This player is an active but selective rewarder, using reward mainly as a positive reinforcement tool toward consistent high contributors and perceived allies. They often grant small rewards to players who repeatedly contribute full amounts and who are not seen as destabilizing the group with extreme punishment. They also occasionally use reward to signal approval toward players who themselves reward or who are part of a cooperative core, suggesting an attempt to build or maintain a coalition of “good citizens.” Their rewards are typically modest in size—single units rather than large, expensive gestures—implying they view reward as a signal rather than as a major redistributive tool. They sometimes refrain from rewarding even when almost everyone cooperates, particularly when punishment tensions are high or when they appear to be conserving resources; this indicates they treat reward as strategic and discretionary rather than automatic. There is no clear evidence they use reward to try to rehabilitate chronic defectors; they overwhelmingly direct it toward those already behaving cooperatively.

They are willing, but very sparingly, to reward others. Across the game they give a few small rewards to different individuals, including both clear cooperators and at least one recurring low contributor. This pattern suggests they are not using rewards purely as a straightforward “pay good cooperators” tool. Their rewards look more like weak, situational positive signals: occasionally acknowledging someone who cooperates or has been heavily punished, or tentatively encouraging a marginal or stigmatized player (medium–low confidence about exact motive). They do not build a stable reward alliance with any single partner, nor do they escalate reward volume over time. Reward is thus a minor, opportunistic extension of their generally cooperative orientation, not a central element of their strategy.

The player never spends on rewards at any point, despite frequently earning high payoffs that would allow them to afford positive transfers. This holds even in periods where group cooperation is very strong and others are engaging in reciprocal or prosocial rewarding. Their lack of rewarding is stable and unambiguous. This suggests that they either regard the reward technology as inefficient (cost equals benefit, whereas punishment has a high leverage) or that their social preference is asymmetrically focused on deterring defection rather than reinforcing cooperation. Put differently, they are willing to pay to harm defectors but not to help cooperators, even when those cooperators are the ones enabling their own high payoffs. This absence of rewarding also indicates that they are not especially interested in building bilateral alliances or signaling gratitude via positive transfers; instead, they rely on contribution choices and occasional punishment as their main social instruments.

They essentially do not use the reward option. When they receive rewards, they do not reciprocate with rewards to those players, and their own outlays overwhelmingly favor punishment over positive reinforcement. This is not due to a lack of activity with the mechanism: they spend substantial amounts on punishment across the game, so the asymmetry is a genuine preference. The most plausible interpretation is that they see negative sanctions as the appropriate tool for regulating behavior, and do not consider positive incentives necessary or worthwhile, even when others are highly cooperative. They thus behave like a “pure punisher” rather than a “reciprocal rewarder.”

The player is a regular and selective rewarder, but much less intense in rewarding than in punishing. They spend on rewards for clear high contributors and for especially prosocial players, often reinforcing those who repeatedly contribute fully and sometimes also engage in prosocial sanctioning. Reward targets include consistently cooperative players and those who have rewarded or supported cooperation in others, indicating a mix of gratitude and reinforcement motives. They almost never reward low contributors, and when a previous free rider begins contributing or stops misbehaving, the player sometimes withholds further punishment but does not immediately move into rewarding them; their rewards are more clearly tied to ongoing, visible cooperation than to one-off improvements. They accept the poor cost-effectiveness of the reward technology and still use it, suggesting a preference for norm reinforcement and positive signaling over simple earnings maximization. Over time, they refine reward targets somewhat, tending to reward a small subset of the most dependable cooperators rather than distributing rewards widely.

They never issue rewards, even though they are surrounded by players who frequently use positive transfers, and even though many others reward high contributors like themselves. They therefore do not practice strategic reinforcement of cooperators or explicit gratitude toward allies. The absence of rewarding persists in high‑cooperation phases and in situations where rewarding might plausibly serve to build alliances or reciprocate prior kindness. Taken together with the lack of punishment, this suggests a “zero‑enforcement” policy: they treat the contribution stage as the only decision that matters, and they refrain from spending any resources on shaping others’ behavior, whether through carrots or sticks. This could reflect a strong payoff‑maximizing instinct with respect to the secondary mechanism, a belief that rewards have too weak an effect to justify their cost, or a generally low social engagement with the reputational/relational aspects of the game.

This player is also a frequent and substantial user of reward, although less single-mindedly than punishment. They spend notable amounts to reward others across many rounds. Their rewards are directed both to high contributors and to some low contributors, and sometimes to players who are themselves frequent punishers or rewarders. This shows that their rewards are not purely based on current-round contribution levels. The pattern suggests multiple motives: - **Positive reinforcement of perceived allies or behaviors:** They often reward players who appear cooperative or who may have treated them well (e.g., by rewarding them or refraining from punishing them). This points to conditional kindness: they use rewards to encourage or maintain alliances and to reinforce behaviors they like. - **Balancing and signaling:** They sometimes reward players who are simultaneously being heavily punished by others, and they occasionally reward players they themselves have punished in other rounds. This indicates that they use reward to fine-tune their stance toward individuals, signaling nuance (“I disapprove of some things, but I still credit you for others”) rather than treating punishment and reward as mutually exclusive. - **Low efficiency concern:** Given that here reward has much weaker leverage than punishment, a payoff-maximizer would use punishment for deterrence and rarely reward. This player nevertheless invests substantially in rewards, suggesting that they are motivated by expressive or relational concerns (gratitude, alliance-building, or fairness adjustments) more than by strict cost-benefit efficiency. Overall, their reward behavior is generous but strategic: they seem to maintain a mental map of who is “on their side” or promoting the kind of group they prefer and use rewards to support those players, even when the mechanism is relatively weak.

The player never uses the reward option. They do not reward high contributors, consistent punishers of defectors, or anyone who rewards them. This holds across cooperative and uncooperative phases of the group, and across early and late rounds. They appear unwilling to sacrifice any payoff even for low-stakes, symmetric rewards, despite the possibility of encouraging continued cooperation or building alliances. Their behavior provides no evidence of gratitude signaling, strategic reinforcement, or attempts at coalition building. Like their refusal to punish, their refusal to reward aligns with a strictly payoff-conserving, individually focused strategy that avoids all costly social interventions.

They use rewards selectively and more sparingly than punishment. When they do reward, they mainly target consistently high-contributing cooperators, particularly those who appear especially supportive of the public good or who themselves engage in rewarding others. Rewards are typically small and targeted rather than broad. They occasionally reward a player who had previously been involved in complex punishment/reward patterns, suggesting a possible attempt to reinforce cooperative or pro-social shifts in that player’s behavior. Their rewards do not track who has rewarded them personally in any simple tit-for-tat way; they are not systematically reciprocal in the reward channel. Instead, they seem oriented toward reinforcing desirable contribution patterns and perhaps signaling approval of strong cooperators or “allies of cooperation.” Compared to punishment, reward plays a secondary, supportive role in their norm-enforcement strategy: they are far more willing to spend to deter defectors than to positively reinforce cooperators, but they do use both tools when they perceive clear alignment with group cooperation.

They almost never use rewards and clearly do not rely on positive reinforcement as their primary tool. Occasional rewards appear, and when they do, they tend to go to players who: - Consistently contribute fully. - Do not obviously free-ride. - Sometimes appear to be “good citizens” relative to a low-cooperation environment. However, these reward actions are sparse and modest, especially compared to the intensity and breadth of their punishment. They do not use sustained reward campaigns to build alliances or to groom a cooperative in-group; rather, their reward use looks incidental and secondary to punishment. There is no sign that reward is used in a calculated way to offset the hostility of heavy punishment or to manage reputation. In type terms, they are a reluctant, low-frequency rewarder who sees sanctioning primarily as punishment for norm violations rather than as a balanced carrot-and-stick system.

They reward exactly once, giving a small positive transfer to a specific group member who has been consistently cooperative and socially positive in chat. This reward occurs despite the recipient not contributing in that particular round, indicating the reward is not narrowly conditional on immediate contribution but more likely reflects accumulated goodwill or appreciation of prior cooperative attitude. They do not experiment with using reward as a bargaining chip or conditional threat; it functions as positive reinforcement rather than as part of an explicit strategy of contingent reciprocity.

They never spend any resources on rewarding others, despite having several opportunities where others contribute more than them and do reward them. They forgo even cost-neutral or break-even uses of the reward mechanism (where they could potentially shape behavior without improving material payoffs), indicating a consistent reluctance to incur any personal cost for social reinforcement. This suggests either that they view the reward mechanism as strategically unnecessary, that they explicitly avoid spending on others even when they benefit from them, or that they do not fully engage with the secondary mechanism at all. Given their generally high contributions, their lack of rewarding is not due to an overall asocial or exploitative stance toward contributions alone; rather, it is specifically a refusal to expend extra resources beyond the public good decision. They appear to rely on others’ willingness to reward or cooperate without reciprocating via the reward channel, indicating a tendency toward being a “reward free rider”: they enjoy the benefits and signaling value of being rewarded but do not participate in the costly enforcement or encouragement of cooperation.

The player is willing to spend nontrivial amounts on rewards, but not consistently. Early on, they use the reward mechanism, and they do so only in contexts where peers’ contributions are high or when some peers are clearly more cooperative than others. They never use rewards as “punishment-by-omission” in an aggressive way; instead, they selectively reward apparent cooperators. Over time, their use of rewards becomes more sporadic and eventually stops for long stretches, even in fully cooperative environments. This suggests they are motivated by a desire to acknowledge or reinforce cooperative behavior, but they may learn that, in this group, cooperation is already stable and does not require continuous reinforcement. They appear to treat rewards as occasional strategic or expressive tools rather than as a standing obligation. They do not use rewards to try to “bribe” clear low contributors into contributing more. Their reward behavior is consistent with a conditional-rewarding cooperator: appreciative of cooperators and willing to incur cost, but not locked into persistent reward giving once norms are established.

The player never spends anything on rewarding others, despite multiple opportunities and despite experiencing incoming rewards themselves. Given that the reward technology is one-to-one in cost and benefit, their refusal to use it suggests that they treat the reward mechanism as unnecessary or inefficient rather than as a tool for shaping behavior. Their non-use of rewards is consistent across contexts: they withhold reward when everyone cooperates, when someone free-rides, and when they themselves have been rewarded. There is no evidence of using rewards to thank high contributors, to build alliances, or to incentivize defectors to improve. This pattern indicates an “anti-reward” or “reward-skeptical” stance: the player prefers to express their cooperative preferences through their own contributions, not by spending extra to influence others’ payoffs. It also suggests that they either do not believe that rewarding will significantly change others’ behavior, or they are unwilling to sacrifice even a neutral-cost amount to engage in positive reciprocity via the mechanism.

They never use the reward option, despite frequent universal cooperation that could be rewarded and despite witnessing others reward. This suggests that they do not use positive incentives as a tool for norm reinforcement, either because they view full cooperation as baseline and not reward-worthy, because they are very payoff-conserving regarding non-contribution expenditures, or because they conceptually reduce the game to “just contribute fully” and ignore the secondary mechanism. Given that they continue to cooperate without seeking to shape others via rewards, they appear to rely primarily on intrinsic norms and minimal chat rather than strategic incentivization.

They never use the reward option, even though others occasionally do. This may reflect a cost-conscious view that rewarding is inefficient given the unfavorable reward technology, or a general reluctance to engage in any costly interventions toward others (parallel to their non-use of punishment). There is no indication that they withhold rewards strategically (e.g., to pressure marginal cooperators); instead, their stable non-use suggests that for them, cooperating by contributing is sufficient, and they do not seek to fine-tune others’ behavior via positive incentives. Unknown whether they would ever reward in a context where the technology is more favorable, as the transcript offers no supporting counterexample.

They never use the reward option, even when others cooperate fully and maintain high contributions round after round. This is notable because they clearly value group cooperation (as seen in their own contributions and occasional positive remarks) but do not spend personal resources to materially reinforce that behavior. Their non-use spans both periods of perfect cooperation and periods where some players return to cooperation after deviating. This could indicate that they regard reward as economically inefficient, unnecessary given already-high cooperation, or simply not salient. It may also reflect a general reluctance to spend for social influence, consistent with their avoidance of both costly punishment and costly reward. They limit their prosociality to contribution decisions rather than engaging in higher-order incentive provision.

They never reward anyone, even in a consistently high-cooperation environment with multiple full contributors and some players who publicly encourage cooperation. This indicates a strong reluctance to spend on positive incentives, which is rational given the inefficient reward technology but still informative about their style: they enforce norms through their own contributions and, occasionally, punishment rather than through material gratitude or strategic positive reinforcement. Their choice suggests they either see reward as too costly/ineffective or do not feel the need to materially acknowledge good behavior when everyone is merely doing what they regard as the baseline expectation.

They never use reward, even when everyone, including themselves, is contributing fully and when others are using reward to encourage cooperation. This implies they do not invest in positive reinforcement of norms at a cost to themselves. Given that reward is relatively inefficient in this setting, their non-use is consistent with a cost-conscious or payoff-focused stance, or simply with a tendency to free-ride on others’ norm-enforcement efforts (letting others bear the reward cost while they benefit from sustained cooperation). There is no evidence of them using reward for alliance building or gratitude signaling.

They never use the reward option, even when others consistently contribute fully and the group achieves high levels of cooperation. They do not engage in positive reinforcement of cooperators, despite being themselves a consistent cooperator. This may reflect a focus on maximizing own payoff subject to always contributing fully, a perception that reward is inefficient (the reward technology is relatively weak), or a low propensity to intervene in others’ payoffs at all. Their pattern suggests an “action-only” cooperator: they contribute themselves but do not spend resources to shape others’ behavior via rewards.

This player does not use the reward mechanism at any point, even when others fully cooperate and even though they express verbal approval. Their absence of material reward is consistent across conditions: with full cooperation, with past defection that has been corrected, and when they verbally praise the group. This pattern suggests they value efficiency in their sanctioning tools: given that the formal reward technology is relatively inefficient (cost exceeds benefit delivered), they may judge it not worth using, preferring costless verbal praise. There is no evidence they strategically withhold rewards to manipulate others; rather, their behavior aligns with a norm that cooperation is expected and does not warrant costly extra payments, whereas defection may warrant costly punishment at least once.

They never use rewards, even though there is sustained high cooperation from nearly all players and multiple instances where rewarding could credibly signal approval or build alliances. This is notable given that most others match their cooperative level, but they still refrain from paying to recognize or reinforce it. This may indicate a focus on conserving own payoff when it comes to costly positive transfers, or a perception that cooperation is already stable and thus does not require extra reinforcement. Since they also avoid punishment, the broader pattern is a complete non-use of sanctioning mechanisms, not a selective rejection of only negative sanctions. Whether they conceptually approve of rewarding but judge it inefficient, or are generally averse to “interfering” with others’ payoffs, is unknown.

They very rarely reward; across the whole game they reward exactly once, and the target is a fully cooperative player. This indicates that they are willing, at least occasionally, to incur a small personal cost to provide a positive transfer to a cooperator. The reward is directed to someone already aligned with the cooperative norm, which is consistent with using reward as a form of positive reinforcement or appreciation rather than as an incentive to change behavior. However, they do not routinely reward cooperators, even when group cooperation is high and stable, so they do not appear to adopt a systematic rewarding strategy. Their default is abstaining from both punishment and reward, with a single exception that shows they recognize and are willing to support cooperative behavior in principle.

The player is very sparing with rewards and stops using them early. After a brief initial phase where they occasionally spend small amounts to reward specific others, they cease rewarding altogether and maintain this non-rewarding stance through the rest of the game, despite ongoing variation in others’ cooperation and extensive reward activity by others. They never engage in broad, systematic reinforcement of cooperators, and their total spending on reward stays low compared to heavy rewarders in the group. When they do reward, it appears selective and limited rather than generous or symbolic. There is no clear pattern of using reward to discipline defectors (not possible here) or to sustain high-contribution allies; instead, rewards look more like brief experiments or one-off acknowledgments. Once they appear to conclude that rewarding is not necessary or not beneficial for their purposes, they abandon it completely and do not re-engage, even when the environment changes (e.g., shifts in average contributions or others’ reward usage). This suggests either a payoff-conscious reluctance to spend on second-order cooperation, a belief that reward is strategically unimportant in this setting, or a preference for pure first-order cooperation (contribution) over meta-level interventions.

They never spend any coins on reward. Across diverse environments—high and low group contributions, high and low use of reward by others, early and late in the game—they consistently allocate zero to rewarding anyone. This is not because reward is unavailable or unused; several peers frequently deploy reward, sometimes at high personal cost, but this player abstains entirely. Their stance suggests either a strong payoff‑maximization motive with respect to the reward stage, a principle against using the reward mechanism, or a belief that rewarding is ineffective or unnecessary. There is no evidence that they selectively reward high contributors, allies, or rewarders; instead, their rule is simply not to reward at all. They thus function as a pure provider in the contribution stage and a pure non‑intervenor in the reward stage.

They reward others infrequently and at modest cost, compared with many peers who sometimes spend heavily on reward. Across the game, they are willing but restrained rewarders. They spend on rewards in only a minority of rounds, with relatively small total expenditures per rewarding episode. This indicates that material payoff still matters to them; they do not treat reward as a domain for large, expressive spending. When they do reward, it is generally in cooperative environments where many others are contributing. There is some evidence that they reward in contexts where group contribution is high and broadly shared, which is consistent with reinforcing cooperative equilibria rather than trying to convert defectors. There is no clear pattern of targeting frequent low contributors with rewards to lure them into cooperation; instead, their rewards appear more aligned with acknowledging cooperative norms already in place. They sometimes reward while also being rewarded, but the total flows are usually small and roughly balanced. They never engage in extreme “reward storms” (mass, high-cost rewarding) seen in a few others, suggesting they do not see reward as a performative or expressive tool. Their reward behavior is best described as cautious, low-intensity positive reinforcement, not a central instrument of their strategy.

They never spend any resources to reward others at any point, despite many opportunities and high levels of contribution by others. They also never use “symbolic” or small-cost rewards. This indicates an extremely low willingness to sacrifice own payoff even marginally to influence others’ behavior or to express approval. They also do not use the reward tool strategically to try to stabilize high contributions that benefit them; instead, they exploit whatever level of cooperation emerges at zero cost. They treat reward as dominated by keeping their own coins, even though others demonstrate a norm where cooperators are frequently rewarded. This strongly suggests they either (a) do not value social signaling at all in this environment, or (b) believe their own individual rewards will not meaningfully affect group cooperation and therefore are not worth the cost. In either case, they behave like a strictly self-regarding player with no prosocial reward motivation.

For most of the game, they never spend on rewards at all, even though they repeatedly observe others using the reward mechanism, often at substantial cost. This suggests that they are either uninterested in using rewards as a strategic or moral tool, or they place very high value on keeping their own payoff relative to any social signaling or norm-enforcement benefits. On one occasion they do use rewards, but even then they spend only a small amount, and the pattern of recipients is not clearly inferable from the aggregate summary; it may be a sparse, perhaps experimental, use of the mechanism. They do not become a steady reward-giver afterward, so this experiment does not develop into a strategy. Overall, they are best characterized as a near-non-rewarder: they free-ride on the cooperative benefits produced by others’ contribution choices but do not pay to reinforce or encourage those behaviors via rewards.

They are an active and at times heavy user of the reward mechanism, even though each unit is payoff-neutral at the individual level. They repeatedly spend nontrivial amounts on rewards, including in rounds where they themselves do not contribute, demonstrating that willingness to reward is decoupled from their own cooperation. Because detailed receiver identities are not given here, the precise targeting rule is unknown, but several consistent features appear: - They do not restrict rewards only to “high contributors”: they are willing to reward in rounds where they personally free-ride, implying that reward is not purely a reciprocity-for-cooperation tool. - They sometimes spend substantial sums on reward, which is inconsistent with a purely myopic payoff maximizer and suggests either a taste for influencing others’ behavior, a desire to shape payoff distribution, or a social preference for “doing something” in the mechanism stage. - Their reward behavior is not monotonic over time: expenditures spike in some mid and late rounds and are lower or zero in others, so they likely use reward tactically rather than as a fixed norm. Given that each reward unit has zero net private return, their repeated use indicates they care about others’ payoffs or signals, not just own income. Whether this is altruistic, alliance-seeking, or expressive behavior is unclear; at minimum, they are a “soft” strategist who uses positive incentives rather than any punitive-equivalent tools (which are absent here).

This player is a frequent and heavy user of the reward mechanism. They repeatedly pay non-trivial costs to give positive transfers across many rounds. Rewarding is not uniform: sometimes they spend nothing, sometimes they spend a modest amount, and in several instances they incur relatively high total reward costs. Overall, they adopt a “motivational” style, using rewards as a central tool rather than as an occasional gesture. The main trigger appears to be others’ cooperative or prosocial behavior, especially those who also reward or who contribute fully. Their pattern is consistent with targeted social reinforcement: they seem to single out perceived allies—high contributors and/or active rewarders—for positive feedback, rather than rewarding all cooperators indiscriminately. Their own spending sometimes exceeds the net reward they themselves receive, indicating that they are willing to incur real costs to sustain or encourage cooperation rather than merely to balance accounts. They do not appear to use reward to equalize payoffs across the group. There is no sign of systematically boosting low earners or penalizing high earners by omission. Instead, they reward in a way that is compatible with alliance-building and reciprocity: returning favors, sustaining mutual supporters, and acknowledging cooperative partners. This suggests they view the reward mechanism as a strategic relational tool rather than as a fairness-corrective instrument. Their willingness to spend on reward remains present even when it is not obviously profitable in a narrow, one-shot sense. This points to either a social preference for rewarding “good” behavior or a belief that repeated reinforcement can sustain higher cooperation over time.

The player never spends any coins on rewards. This is consistent and unconditional: they refrain from rewarding when cooperation is high, when it is mixed, and when they themselves are rewarded. This indicates either that (a) they view rewarding as unnecessary or inefficient, (b) they are strongly payoff-maximizing with respect to the costly reward mechanism, or (c) they are following a “contribute but do not use incentives” heuristic. Their refusal to reward persists even when other players invest heavily in rewarding, including clear patterns of others encouraging contributors or particular individuals. They do not join in these reward cascades. They also do not appear to selectively reward any highly cooperative or generous peer; there is no indication of targeted reciprocity through the reward channel. Given that the cost and benefit of reward are symmetric here, their zero-reward policy suggests that they do not use the reward mechanism as a tool for norm enforcement, coordination, or alliance building. Instead, they rely on direct contribution choices and ignore the extra incentive lever. In strategic terms, they behave like a “pure contributor, non-rewarder”: they are willing to incur costs to fund the public good directly but not to manipulate others’ behavior via side-payments.

This player almost never spends their own coins on rewards. Across the game, they devote only a small amount of their wealth to rewarding others, in a few isolated instances. This places them well below the many group members who use reward generously or even extravagantly. When they do reward, the pattern suggests they are responding to clear pro-social signals from others (e.g., consistent contribution and/or visible reward-giving by some players), but such actions are rare and small-scale. They do not behave like a “pro-social enforcer” or “reward leader.” Instead, they treat reward as a minor, occasional gesture rather than a strategic tool. They do not engage in systematic strategic reinforcement: they do not consistently send reward to the highest contributors, nor to specific allies, nor to shift low contributors upward. Their rewards do not clearly condition on who cooperated in that particular round versus previous rounds. This makes it unlikely that they use reward as a serious instrument to engineer future group behavior. Overall, they have a strong preference to conserve coins rather than pay for others’ welfare. Reward is not part of their core strategy; it is at best a sporadic, low-intensity supplement.

This player is an active reward user. They often spend nontrivial amounts on rewards, sometimes very large amounts, even though the reward technology is break-even. Their reward use is not parsimonious or narrowly targeted; they are willing to incur substantial cost to shift payoffs between players. The pattern suggests a mix of motives: - They frequently reward some others while continuing to free-ride, indicating a desire to maintain social ties or approval from contributors without personally bearing the contribution cost. - Their reward behavior is highly variable across rounds, from no spending to very heavy spending, indicating that they react to specific configurations of others’ actions or payoffs rather than following a rigid rule. - The largest outlays appear when they are already doing well in payoffs, consistent with using surplus to influence the payoff distribution, possibly to signal favoritism, repay perceived kindness, or support particular players. They do not appear to use rewards as a disciplined incentive system for public-good provision (e.g., consistently rewarding high contributors and withholding from low contributors in a clear way). Instead, their allocations look more like ad hoc relational or emotional responses: they are willing to pay a lot to transfer coins when they feel it is warranted, even though this does not increase total welfare.

They routinely use the reward mechanism and are willing to spend a small but persistent share of earnings to reward others. Their reward pattern is selective: they do not spread rewards widely each round, but instead give a few units to a subset of players. The triggers appear partly strategic and partly expressive. They sometimes reward well-known high cooperators and sometimes reward apparent allies or those whose actions benefit them indirectly. They also occasionally reward when they themselves defect, indicating they may be compensating for free-riding by offering small, targeted gratitude rather than by increasing contributions. The cost-benefit is balanced rather than altruistic; they rarely spend extremely large amounts and tend to keep their own payoff high, so reward is used as a low-cost signal or incentive rather than as major sacrifice. Their use of reward is consistent across early, middle, and late game, indicating a stable preference for maintaining a cooperative atmosphere through low-level positive reinforcement instead of via own contributions.

The player is very sparing with rewards and never uses them as a broad social tool. Most of the time they spend nothing on rewards, even in environments with high overall cooperation and numerous generous rewarders. When they do spend, it is in small, discrete amounts, far below what some other players are willing to invest. This indicates a strong payoff-preservation motive with respect to the reward stage, in contrast to their generosity in the contribution stage. They appear willing to reward cooperators selectively and only occasionally, suggesting that rewards are not part of a systematic strategy to sustain cooperation, but rather a low-frequency, targeted gesture. Given that they always contribute fully, everyone who also contributes fully is a potential “deserving” target, yet they reward only a tiny subset of possible recipients and never escalate to heavy, sustained rewarding. There is no evidence that they attempt to match other players’ reward intensity; they do not mirror extremely generous rewarders or join cascades of rewarding a popular target. Their reward use does not clearly track specific events like a sudden increase in someone’s contribution (since contributions are binary and constant for many players) or large prior rewards received by themselves. Overall, they treat the reward mechanism as optional and only marginally useful, preferring to support the public good through direct contribution rather than through costly, second-order incentives.

They use reward sparingly, intermittently, and never at extreme levels. They do not appear to use reward as an aggressive coordination device (for example, they do not systematically reward all full contributors in a round) nor as a strong alliance-building tool. Instead, their reward use fits a “light, occasional reinforcement” pattern—possibly expressing approval or mild reciprocity but without substantial strategic investment. They also accept that others often reward them without matching the overall generosity of heavy rewarders, suggesting they are not trying to keep up with a reciprocity norm at all costs.

They never spend any coins on rewards throughout the game, despite repeatedly seeing other players use the reward mechanism, sometimes quite heavily. This suggests that they either see reward-giving as unnecessary, inefficient, or not part of their role. They do not appear to use rewards instrumentally to shape others’ behavior or to reciprocate kindness, even when rewarding generous contributors would be straightforward. This pattern is compatible with several possible motives: a preference for keeping their own payoffs, a belief that social incentives are ineffective or not worth the effort, or a simple rule-based policy such as “always cooperate, never reward.” Which of these motives apply is unknown.

They spend on reward extremely rarely and in small amounts relative to total earnings. They never use reward as a tool to shape behavior over time; it functions more as a sporadic, low-cost gesture than as a core part of their strategy. They are willing to use the reward mechanism and at times spend very heavily on it, but their use is episodic rather than constant. In some phases they spend nothing on rewards, in others they expend substantial amounts of their payoff to distribute positive incentives. This indicates that they are capable of strong prosocial investment but do not view rewarding as an automatic default. Given the data, the primary trigger for their reward spending appears to be others’ contribution choices, not others’ generosity with rewards toward them. When they do invest heavily in rewards, it coincides with contexts where many others are also contributing fully; they seem to use rewards to support or amplify group cooperation rather than to “buy” cooperation from defectors. There is no clear pattern of them systematically rewarding low contributors to bring them in line or of punishing defectors through reward exclusion; instead, their heavy reward bursts likely go toward reinforcing evidently cooperative players. They also show that they are willing to spend more on rewards than they receive back, sometimes leaving themselves with relatively low net payoffs. This points to a willingness to sacrifice personal earnings for group-oriented signaling or reinforcement. However, these phases do not persist; they eventually return to a low-reward, pure-contribution mode, which suggests that they treat reward as a situational tool rather than a stable preference to constantly redistribute. Overall, they are a selective but sometimes very generous rewarder, using rewards mainly to support existing cooperation rather than as a continuous, finely tuned incentive system.

Reward is central to this player’s style. They repeatedly spend part of their private payoff to send positive transfers to the partner, often when both are free-riding and also on occasions when the partner contributes to the public good. These rewards are not tightly contingent on high public-good contributions; the player sometimes rewards in the absence of any partner contribution and sometimes rewards after exploitation of the partner’s cooperation. This indicates that their reward behavior is not strict conditional reciprocity but rather a general prosocial or affiliative inclination expressed through side payments, decoupled from their own contribution decision. The intensity of reward fluctuates across rounds, but the direction is consistent: they only use positive reinforcement, never mixing it with punishment. Over time, especially later in the game, they reduce the use of rewards and occasionally stop rewarding altogether, consistent with fatigue, payoff-saving, or diminished expectation of future reciprocal benefit. Overall, they are a “side-payment altruist” who prefers to use rewards rather than contributions or punishment to affect outcomes and maintain a positive relational tone.

They never spend on reward at any point, despite repeatedly receiving rewards from the partner. This holds even in periods when they briefly contribute fully; they do not accompany cooperation with positive reinforcement. Their choice pattern suggests a strict preference for keeping endowment over prosocial reinforcement. Gratitude signaling through reward is absent; they do not reward to reciprocate, to stabilize cooperation, or to soften previous punishment.

The player never spends on rewarding others at any point, despite frequent high contributions from several peers and an active use of the reward mechanism by others. They consistently accept incoming rewards but never reciprocate or initiate reward behavior. This makes them a pure non‑rewarder in this environment. There is no evidence that particularly high contributions, stable cooperators, or generous rewarders trigger any change in this pattern. Whether this reflects a principled stance against costly social tools, a belief that their individual rewards would not meaningfully affect group behavior, or simple focus on immediate earnings is unknown; what is clear is that they systematically refuse to sacrifice coins to benefit others through the reward channel.

They never spend on rewards at any point. This player almost never spends coins to reward others. Across the game, their own spending on reward is extremely low relative to both (a) the number of opportunities they have and (b) the high frequency with which other players do spend on rewards. They also do not appear to use reward to reciprocate rewards they personally receive; they are rewarded by others multiple times but do not adopt rewarding as a norm in response. Overall, they behave as a near non-rewarder: they treat the reward mechanism as mostly not worth its cost, and they do not invest in social incentives through rewards.

The player never spends any of their own coins to reward others, despite frequent opportunities to do so and despite being the recipient of repeated, often generous rewards. This indicates a strong reluctance to incur personal cost for prosocial signaling or norm enforcement, even when the technology is known and when others appear to be engaging in substantial reward-giving behavior. Their choice to abstain from rewarding persists across early, middle, and late stages, suggesting that this is a stable preference rather than an unlearned feature. This pattern is compatible with a type who sees the reward system as a “free bonus” rather than a tool to shape group behavior. This player almost never uses the reward mechanism and, when they do, they spend only a small amount and only in the very first and one of the last rounds. This player never spends on reward at any point. This is not because they themselves do not receive rewards; they do receive small positive rewards in various phases, but they do not reciprocate in kind. They do not appear to condition their contributions on having been rewarded; they continue to cooperate without “thanking” or strategically reinforcing rewarders. The player never spends any coins on rewards. Across a long interaction with many opportunities, they consistently choose not to use the reward mechanism at all. This is not due to lack of exposure to cooperative or uncooperative behavior in others; they see both high and low contributors and also see that others frequently spend on rewards. Their inaction therefore reads as a clear preference rather than oversight. This implies a strong reluctance to incur personal costs for the sake of influencing others’ behavior via positive incentives.
