The player is a heavy, persistent, and often altruistic rewarder, even at a highly unfavorable reward cost. Across the game they repeatedly sacrifice substantial payoff to give small rewards. They do this both in cooperative and in mixed/low-cooperation environments, and they do not systematically cut back after incurring low payoffs from high reward expenditures. They continue to reward in many later rounds, so this is not an early exploratory phase but an enduring pattern. Their rewards appear broadly pro-social rather than narrowly strategic. They repeatedly grant reward in situations where many others give nothing or very little to anyone, and they do not converge on a “reciprocal club” of mutual rewarders. They sometimes reward in conditions where contribution levels are uneven, suggesting they are not purely targeting top contributors in a tight, efficiency-maximizing way but instead using reward as a general encouragement or moral signal toward cooperators and perhaps toward those they see as under-recognized. They also accept that this behavior will make them earn significantly less than nearly all peers in some rounds, but continue anyway. Taken together, this suggests a “moralistic altruist” type: they use reward as expressive behavior to endorse cooperation and perhaps to communicate approval, rather than primarily as a calculated investment in future gains. They appear insensitive to the unfavorable reward-to-payoff technology, which points to social or normative motives outweighing material payoffs.

This player is an unusually frequent and generous rewarder relative to typical efficiency considerations, especially in earlier and mid stages. They repeatedly spend substantial amounts of their own payoff to grant relatively small rewards to others, indicating that they value shaping others’ behavior or expressing approval even when it is personally costly. Their rewarding is selective rather than random. The pattern strongly suggests that they target players who either (a) contribute high amounts, (b) are persistent cooperators despite others’ free‑riding, or (c) have been rewarding or supportive themselves. They seem to use reward as a positive reinforcement tool to stabilize high-contribution norms and, possibly, to reciprocate perceived generosity. They do not appear to “buy popularity” broadly; they are willing to spend heavily but not on every player. This indicates a strategic element: they reward to encourage specific behaviors or actors rather than to equalize outcomes. Over time, however, the intensity of their rewarding declines, particularly toward the end of the game, suggesting that they view reward primarily as an investment in future interactions rather than as an intrinsic moral duty. At a personality level (inferred with low to moderate confidence), this pattern is consistent with a “prosocial strategist”: they are willing to incur real costs to nudge the group toward cooperation, but only when there is enough future left for that investment to matter.

They never spend any coins on rewarding others in any round. This is consistent even when others contribute very generously and when there are clear, repeated high contributors. They consistently receive small amounts of reward from others but do not reciprocate. There is no sign that they use rewards strategically to shape future group behavior or to express gratitude. Given that reward is costly and they prioritize maximizing their own payoff, they treat the reward mechanism as something to benefit from, not to use. They behave like a pure reward consumer, not a reward provider. This suggests that prosocial signaling or norm-enforcement via reward is not important for them, or at least not important enough to justify personal cost. Their reward behavior is thus strictly self-regarding and non-participatory.

This player is a very light and selective user of the reward mechanism. They spend on reward only a few times and in small amounts relative to others, despite frequently benefiting from sizable contributions by peers. Their overall pattern suggests that they do not see costly rewarding as a primary tool for shaping group behavior; instead, they mostly conserve resources and rely on others’ reward behavior or on contribution adjustments themselves. When they do spend on rewards, it appears tied to episodes where they personally contribute a lot and the group environment is at least moderately cooperative. This points to a possible strategic reinforcement motive: they may reward to encourage or stabilize high-contribution states that they themselves are invested in, rather than out of pure gratitude or fairness. They do not systematically reward the highest contributors or the most “altruistic” players; the mapping from others’ contributions to their rewards is sparse and non-mechanical, which suggests they either (a) use a simple, coarse heuristic (“occasionally encourage good behavior”) or (b) are uncertain about the benefits of rewarding and are experimenting at low scale. They never adopt a “heavy rewarder” or “social engineer” role in the group. The cost-to-impact ratio of reward is unfavorable, and their behavior is consistent with recognizing this: they mostly act as a beneficiary of others’ reward efforts rather than a driver of them.

They never use the reward option, despite frequent opportunities and the presence of many high contributors. This indicates a strong reluctance to spend resources on positive reinforcement under a relatively inefficient reward technology. Their behavior aligns with a payoff-sensitive stance: they are willing once to pay for punishment when it is very cost-effective but never to pay for reward, which is highly inefficient. There is no evidence of them seeking to build goodwill or alliances with rewards, even when others cooperate strongly. Gratitude — if present — is not expressed through costly rewards. Their cooperation strategy relies on own contributions and occasional punishment, not on incentivizing others with positive transfers.

They never reward anyone, even when others contribute at or above their own level or when cooperation is high. Given that reward is relatively inefficient in this setting, their non-use may reflect a simple cost–benefit assessment: they are unwilling to spend private resources on a tool that returns little to the recipient and nothing direct to themselves. It may also indicate a general reluctance to use costly second-order mechanisms (both reward and punishment), preferring to contribute directly to the public good instead. The absence of any token rewarding suggests they are not inclined to use positive incentives as social signals or as tools to build alliances. Their cooperative behavior comes through their own contributions, not through explicit reinforcement of others’ choices.

They almost never reward; they use the reward mechanism exactly once. The reward goes to a very high contributor in a period where the focal player themselves contributes nothing. That pattern is consistent with expressive gratitude or norm-reinforcement toward a strong cooperator, but it is also consistent with a strategic attempt to encourage continued high contributions from others while they free-ride in that instance. Given that they do not develop a broader pattern of rewarding either high contributors or those who align with their own behavior, they appear to view reward as optional and non-essential. They are willing to incur a small cost to recognize outstanding cooperation, but they do not systematize reward use as a primary tool for shaping group behavior. Overall, they privilege their own contributions as their main cooperative signal and use reward sparingly, likely only when someone’s cooperation is saliently above norm.

They never use the reward option, even in contexts where others contribute more than them or match their high contributions. This is notable given that some peers consistently contribute strongly, yet the player still refrains from rewarding. This suggests they are unwilling to sacrifice own payoff to positively reinforce cooperation, at least under the given (inefficient) reward technology. Their non-use is consistent with a view that rewarding is not cost-effective or not necessary, or that it is simply not salient to them. They rely instead on their own contribution choices rather than side-payments to shape group behavior. Whether they would reward under more favorable reward parameters is unknown.

The player uses reward very sparingly and only in one observed instance, targeted at two high‑contributing peers. This indicates that, when they do reward, it is selective and contingent on clear cooperative behavior. Since reward is individually costly and has poor efficiency in this environment, their willingness to use it at all shows a readiness to incur personal cost to recognize or encourage prosocial actions. Because this occurs early and is not repeated, it plausibly functions as a one‑time signal of appreciation or a tentative attempt at establishing a cooperative norm rather than an ongoing reinforcement strategy. They subsequently abstain from rewarding even when others continue to contribute well, which suggests they either reassess the cost‑effectiveness of reward, believe cooperation is already established without further incentives, or simply prefer to rely on contributions alone.

They never use the reward mechanism, even in a setting where the group is highly cooperative and where they themselves receive nontrivial positive rewards on several occasions. Their own receipt of rewards does not induce them to reciprocate via the reward channel, nor do they appear to use rewarding to strategically reinforce others’ high contributions. This indicates that they separate their own cooperative behavior from the use of explicit positive incentives. Given that reward is cost‑neutral to the recipient but costly to the giver, their refusal to reward is consistent with a payoff‑maximizing stance regarding second‑order actions, even though they are generous in first‑order contributions. In other words, they are willing to sacrifice private payoff to benefit the group via contributions, but they are not willing to additionally sacrifice payoff to provide second‑order benefits that do not further increase public goods production beyond what cooperation already achieves. They also do not appear to reward as thanks for the rewards they themselves receive, implying that gratitude signaling is not a salient motive in their strategy. This makes them a “pure contributor” rather than a “meta‑cooperator” who invests in maintaining cooperation through second‑order prosocial acts.

They also never use the reward mechanism, despite consistently observing high contributions by others and despite receiving occasional rewards themselves. This indicates that they do not see costly positive reinforcement as worthwhile, even in a highly cooperative environment. Their lack of rewarding persists regardless of whether they are being rewarded or punished, suggesting that gratitude signaling or reciprocal rewarding is not a major driver of their behavior. They appear to “free-ride” on others’ use of rewards and punishments in the sense that they enjoy the cooperative benefits and occasional incoming rewards without investing in the incentive system. The overall pattern points to a “pure contributor” type: they cooperate directly through their own contributions but abstain from meta-strategic actions that shape others’ payoffs, whether positive or negative. Their cooperative identity is thus expressed through direct giving rather than through managing others’ behavior.

This player does not use the reward mechanism at all, despite consistently cooperating themselves and occasionally receiving rewards from others. Their refusal to reward holds even in environments of high cooperation and even when others punish deviators in similar ways, which suggests this is not because there are no deserving targets. The most plausible inference is that they see costly positive transfers as unnecessary or inefficient: the reward technology in this game does not leverage multipliers the way punishment does, so rewarding yields no net social gain and only redistributes. By contrast, punishment both expresses disapproval and has stronger impact per coin. Their behavior implies a preference for enforcing norms via negative sanctions rather than positive reinforcement, or at least a belief that reward is not worth the cost in this setting. They may appreciate being rewarded (unknown) but do not reciprocate it instrumentally.

The player never spends to reward others, even though they frequently observe near-universal full cooperation and even receive positive transfers themselves in some rounds. Their own contributions are high, but they do not reinforce others’ cooperation through active rewarding. Possible explanations, consistent with the behavior, include: - A belief that rewarding is unnecessary because cooperation is already very high for most of the game. - A judgment that reward technology is not cost-effective (each unit given costs as much as it benefits others, so it does not change aggregate payoffs). - A preference to contribute via the public good itself rather than via targeted side-payments. - A generally frugal or payoff-conscious attitude toward additional costly prosocial acts beyond the main contribution decision. They also do not reciprocate the rewards they receive with counter-rewards, suggesting they do not treat the reward mechanism as a channel for expressing gratitude or building bilateral relationships. Instead, they appear to rely solely on their contribution choice as their cooperative signal. Overall, they are a “pure contributor” who cooperates through the main public-goods channel but abstains from both positive and negative costly peer-to-peer interventions.

They also never use reward, despite the presence of many high contributors and episodes of universal cooperation. Their own contributions are fully cooperative, and they do occasionally receive rewards from others, yet they do not reciprocate with material rewards. This pattern suggests that while they appreciate cooperation enough to act cooperatively themselves, they do not see additional costly reward as necessary or worth the expense. Since rewarding here is payoff-neutral (cost equals benefit), their inaction does not clearly reflect selfishness; it may reflect a minimalist or “no extra interventions” approach to the sanctioning stage, a desire to conserve attention/effort, or a belief that contribution choices themselves are sufficient expression of prosociality. They do not use rewards strategically to cultivate allies, reinforce particular partners, or signal gratitude. Overall, they fit a “quiet altruist” style: they contribute maximally but abstain from manipulating others’ behavior with either positive or negative incentives.

The player never uses the reward mechanism, even though they themselves receive some rewards across the game. This indicates a strong asymmetry between their willingness to invest in negative versus positive incentives. They appear to rely on contribution and punishment to sustain norms, rather than spending resources to positively reinforce good behavior. Possible interpretations (all with low confidence due to lack of direct evidence) include: they may see reward as redundant when cooperation is already high; they may be cost-conscious about spending on others unless to deter norm violations; or they may conceptually separate “doing one’s part” (which does not merit extra reward) from “failing to do one’s part” (which merits sanctions). In any case, their observable type is a high contributor and conditional punisher who does not engage in costly rewarding of others.

They also never use the reward mechanism, even though many others frequently match their full contributions. This suggests they do not rely on explicit positive reinforcement or material gratitude to support cooperation. They might view contributing and receiving the redistributed benefits as sufficient mutual recognition, or they might consider reward expenditure an unnecessary cost that does not improve outcomes enough to justify it. The player does receive small rewards from others in some rounds yet does not reciprocate with rewards of their own, even toward peers who consistently cooperate. This implies that gratitude, if present, does not manifest as costly prosocial rewarding. They seem to separate their norm of “always contribute fully” from any obligation to invest further resources in reward systems. Overall, their persona is that of a principled, efficiency-focused, unconditional cooperator who maintains full contributions across the entire horizon, avoids both punishment and reward, and relies on personal adherence to a cooperative norm rather than on incentive engineering to shape others’ behavior. Their deeper beliefs about why others cooperate or defect remain unknown, but behaviorally they act as if their own cooperative rule is independent of others’ choices.

The player almost never uses positive rewards. They show no clear pattern of systematically reinforcing cooperators, even when others match their full contributions consistently. There are isolated instances where they receive small rewards from others, but they do not reciprocate with rewards of their own. Given that rewarding is exactly cost‑neutral in the mechanism (the cost to the giver equals the benefit to the receiver), their disuse of rewards is informative: they either see rewards as unnecessary, as a waste of control “budget,” or as less effective than punishment in maintaining norms. Their behavior suggests a preference for deterrence and discipline rather than positive reinforcement. They do not appear to use rewards strategically to cultivate allies, to thank previous punishers or cooperators, or to offset harsh punishments. Gratitude or coalition‑building, if present as motives, is not expressed through the reward mechanism. Overall, they are a one‑sided norm enforcer who relies on sanctions, not on encouragement, to shape group behavior.

This player never voluntarily uses the reward mechanism on others. They do not allocate any positive transfers, even in periods when nearly everyone, including them, cooperates fully and they themselves receive small amounts of reward from others. This suggests that either they see rewarding as unnecessary, inefficient, or less important than punishment, or that they prioritize saving their own coins over providing positive reinforcement. Their own behavior is influenced more by punishment than by reward: when they are sanctioned for free-riding, they quickly revert to stable full cooperation, whereas repeated cooperation and receipt of rewards do not appear to change their already cooperative default. They do not reciprocate received rewards or engage in gratitude signaling via the reward mechanism. Overall, their enforcement style is asymmetric: they rely on punishment rather than reward to uphold norms. In new environments, they are likely to cooperate by default, punish conspicuous free-riders when it seems impactful, and rarely, if ever, spend resources to reward others’ good behavior.

The player is also a frequent and generous rewarder, but their reward behavior is more flexible and exploratory than their rigid contribution rule. They reward high contributors and evidently cooperative peers; they never reward visible defectors. Rewarding appears to serve several functions: - Positive reinforcement: They direct rewards primarily toward those who uphold the full-contribution standard, especially after episodes of defection by others. This looks like an attempt to stabilize and encourage continued cooperation. - Gratitude or reciprocity: They sometimes reward players who likely rewarded them earlier (inferred from occasional rewards received when everyone cooperes), but because identities of rewarders are hidden, this inference has low confidence. Still, their reward pattern suggests they recognize and value prosocial others and may be reciprocating good treatment at the group level rather than partner-specific tit-for-tat. - Strategic shaping: At times they reward a broad set of cooperators, and at other times a narrower subset. This variation indicates some experimentation with how much and to whom to reward, perhaps as they learn which players are reliably prosocial. They do not reward indiscriminately; there are stretches when everyone cooperates and they choose not to reward at all, perhaps conserving resources or concluding that cooperation is already sufficiently stable. In other periods, with similar contribution profiles, they resume rewarding. This suggests they balance a prosocial desire to encourage others with some attention to the cost of rewards and marginal benefit. Overall, they are a “strong conditional altruist with norm-enforcing sanctions”: they unconditionally contribute fully themselves, conditionally punish free-riders, and conditionally reward cooperators, using both negative and positive incentives to maintain a high-cooperation equilibrium while personally never deviating from maximal contribution.

The player is highly and consistently willing to reward, using rewards as their primary tool for shaping others’ behavior. They reward in most phases of the game and often at substantial cumulative cost. They direct rewards to many different peers rather than forming a narrow alliance with just one partner, indicating an attempt to sustain or encourage group-wide cooperation rather than simple reciprocity to a single benefactor. Within that broad pattern, they do show some selectivity: certain names receive repeated small or medium-sized rewards, while others are rewarded only occasionally or not at all, implying that they build and update a mental map of who is “deserving.” Their criteria appear to be: reliable contribution to the public good, and perhaps responsiveness to norms over time, rather than one-shot behavior. They typically decouple rewarding from their own contribution decision; they sometimes reward despite having contributed nothing themselves, and sometimes reward while contributing fully. This points to a strategic or relational view of rewards: they use them to maintain goodwill, reinforce cooperative identities, and possibly offset any negative impressions from their intermittent free-riding or later punishments. In several phases, they allocate sizable total reward outlays across multiple peers in the same decision, suggesting a willingness to sacrifice personal payoff for a more cooperative and appreciative group climate. Net, they are a “prosocial rewarder” who sees positive incentives as central, but who is willing to complement them with punishment when rewards alone seem insufficient.

This player never uses the reward mechanism, despite being surrounded most of the time by fully cooperative peers and despite occasionally receiving small rewards themselves. Their lack of rewarding is stable over the entire game: they do not begin rewarding early to establish norms, nor shift to rewarding later when punishment is established. They also do not appear to “reciprocate” being rewarded. This suggests either that they view full contribution as baseline expected behavior that does not merit extra compensation, or that they regard rewarding as an inefficient or unnecessary use of resources compared to simple contribution and punishment of defectors. There is no evidence that they experiment with reward as a strategic reinforcement tool. Their cooperative norm seems to be enforced asymmetrically: deviations downward are sanctioned, but compliance with the norm is not positively reinforced. This pattern is consistent with a strict, duty-focused cooperator who expects cooperation as default and reserves costly actions for punishing norm violations rather than expressing gratitude.

They also never use the reward mechanism, even though they are repeatedly surrounded by high contributors and themselves receive modest rewards at various times. They do not reciprocate these rewards, nor do they selectively reward high contributors or those who help maintain full cooperation. This abstention from rewarding persists even when cooperation is strong and when they personally benefit from others’ generosity, suggesting that they do not view costly positive reinforcement as necessary or worthwhile. The equal cost and benefit of rewards make them budget-neutral for the recipient and strictly costly for the giver; the player’s pattern is consistent with treating rewards as dominated by simply keeping their own payoff. Overall, the player’s sanction/reward profile is “non-interventionist”: they rely on their own consistent contribution behavior rather than on managing others through incentives, and they do not expend resources to shape others’ behavior either positively or negatively. Their cooperative stance is expressed through contribution, not through second-order sanctioning.

This player is willing to use the reward mechanism, but only selectively and at modest intensity. They do not reward every round, and when they do, they allocate only a small share of their possible budget, suggesting they treat rewarding as a costly, targeted signal rather than a standing policy. Their willingness to pay for others’ benefit in this inefficient reward environment indicates some level of altruism or norm enforcement that goes beyond simple payoff maximization. The triggers for rewarding appear to be others’ relatively high or norm‑consistent contributions. They do not appear to reward low contributors, nor do they reward when overall group behavior looks weak or uncooperative. This indicates that they use rewards primarily as positive reinforcement for cooperation, not as random generosity. Since rewarders are anonymous, rewarding cannot build direct reputation with targeted individuals, so their behavior is best interpreted as an attempt to shape group norms or to “do the right thing” toward cooperators. They stop rewarding when the cooperative environment deteriorates or becomes more ambiguous, consistent with a contingent rule: “I will spend to encourage clear cooperators, but I will not keep paying for a group that isn’t obviously reciprocating.” Their modest but real engagement with the mechanism marks them as a low‑intensity altruistic enforcer rather than a pure egoist or a strong altruist. In end‑game conditions, they are still occasionally willing to reward, which suggests that intrinsic satisfaction with supporting cooperators or enforcing fairness matters to them, even when no long‑term strategic return is available.

The player never spends resources to reward others, despite multiple opportunities and the presence of clearly higher contributors in the group. This indicates a strong reluctance to incur personal cost for the sake of reinforcing others’ cooperative behavior. Their attitude toward the reward mechanism is effectively non-usage: they treat rewards as a dominated or unappealing option, at least under the given cost-benefit ratio. They do receive rewards from others, yet do not reciprocate with any rewarding behavior of their own. This suggests that gratitude signaling through costly reward is absent or at least not strong enough to override their payoff concerns. Their stance toward social reinforcement appears to be: they are willing to accept benefits generated or reinforced by others’ altruism but are not willing to invest in maintaining or encouraging that altruism themselves. Strategically, they appear to view rewards as non-essential to their own objectives. They do not attempt to use rewards instrumentally to steer partners’ behavior, even when it might help sustain contributions from high cooperators. This fits a profile of a player focused narrowly on immediate monetary outcomes rather than on indirect, longer-term gains through institution-building or social norm engineering.

The player is selective but ultimately willing to spend on rewards, even though the reward technology is inefficient. For much of the game, they refrain from rewarding, indicating that they evaluate the cost of rewarding as high relative to its immediate payoff impact. They do not automatically reward high contributors; instead, they wait until patterns of cooperation and group behavior are somewhat clearer before investing in rewards. When they do reward, they are willing to incur a meaningful cost, implying that they use rewards to recognize or encourage what they perceive as cooperative or norm-following behavior. Because rewarders are anonymous and there are few future rounds when they start rewarding, their willingness to pay suggests that motives go beyond simple strategic reciprocity: they may be signaling approval, reinforcing norms, or expressing gratitude to cooperative partners, even with low instrumental return. They do not appear to reward in response to being rewarded themselves in any straightforward tit-for-tat pattern; there is no consistent immediate mirroring. Instead, their reward use looks more like a considered, situational choice: they withhold reward for most of the interaction, then use it in targeted bursts later, including at the very end. Overall, they are a restrained, efficiency-conscious rewarder who is nonetheless prepared to pay real costs to support or recognize cooperation when they judge it warranted.

The player never spends any of their own resources to reward others, despite having repeated opportunities and facing multiple partners who clearly contribute above them in some rounds. This reveals several features of their type. Willingness to reward: They effectively have zero willingness to incur personal cost to provide rewards. Even minimal, symbolic rewarding is absent. This indicates that they either see the reward mechanism as dominated (too costly for too little impact) or they place very low weight on expressing gratitude or encouraging others when it is privately costly. Trigger conditions: There is no observed trigger under which they decide to reward—high peer contributions and relative generosity from others are insufficient on their own. Thus, any internal threshold for rewarding, if it exists, is either very high or tightly linked to direct, immediate self-benefit that they do not see here. Strategic reinforcement: The player does not use rewards as a tool to shape others’ behavior. They do not appear to attempt to create or sustain a cooperative norm via positive incentives, suggesting either that they doubt the effectiveness of rewards in this setup, or that long-run group efficiency is secondary to preserving their own coins each round. Gratitude signaling and social style: The consistent absence of rewarding, combined with steady partial contribution, suggests a “quiet self-interested cooperator”: they contribute some to the public good but do not go out of their way to acknowledge or support high cooperators. Socially, this aligns with a restrained, economically focused style rather than an expressive or norm-enforcing one. Beliefs about the mechanism (inferred, low confidence): The player likely perceives the cost-to-impact ratio of rewards as unfavorable, and/or expects that others will not adjust their behavior enough in response to justify the expense. Consequently, they treat the reward option as effectively irrelevant to their strategy.

This player also uses rewards, but more selectively and with a different flavor than punishment. They are willing to spend on rewards multiple times, which is notable because rewarding is payoff-neutral (cost equals benefit) and thus less powerful than punishment. The fact that they still use it suggests they value positive reinforcement and signaling, not just deterrence. Their rewards do not track contributions in a simple linear way. They sometimes reward players who contributed nothing or very little in that round, and sometimes fail to reward clear high contributors. This indicates that their reward decisions likely reflect a longer or more nuanced memory: they may be responding to earlier contributions, previous punishment received by that player, or changes in behavior over time. A recurring pattern is that they often reward the same peer across several different contexts, including when that peer is currently contributing zero. This suggests they may adopt a “support” stance toward that player: using rewards to encourage participation, express trust, or compensate for that player having been punished (possibly by others, possibly by themselves in prior rounds). They appear to use rewards as a relationship-maintenance tool rather than a simple “pay for performance” mechanism. They also occasionally reward peers who are currently contributing relatively high amounts, which fits a more conventional gratitude or reinforcement role: affirming partners whose behavior aligns with their cooperative ideal. But because they do not consistently reward all high contributors, these episodes look more like targeted encouragement than a strict contract. The timing of reward usage, interspersed with both high and low personal contributions, suggests that they do not restrict rewards to periods of personal prosperity. They are willing to sacrifice even in low-payoff contexts, which implies that the expressive or social value of rewarding is important to them. In strategic terms, their reward behavior is best characterized as “selective, relationship-oriented reinforcement.” They use rewards to foster alliances, encourage or comfort specific partners, and occasionally to balance their own use of punishment, rather than to implement a clear, transparent incentive scheme tied directly to contribution levels.

This player uses rewards sparingly and in a targeted, contingent manner. They reward others when those others contribute generously relative to the group, and they sometimes shift from punishing a player to rewarding that same player once contributions increase meaningfully. This suggests they use reward as a signal of approval and as positive reinforcement for cooperative behavior, not as a tool for side‑payments or alliances. They preferentially reward peers who consistently contribute at high levels, especially when those peers stand out relative to other group members. They also reward a previously low‑contributing target once that individual’s contributions increase, indicating that they track behavioral change and are willing to acknowledge improvement. This pattern is consistent with an underlying belief that both punishment and reward can shape others’ strategies and that people can “redeem” themselves through cooperation. Because rewards are used much less intensively than punishment, the player appears to see negative sanctions as the main instrument to enforce norms, with rewards functioning as supplementary encouragement or recognition. The cost‑effectiveness difference between punishment and reward in this environment may contribute to that asymmetry, but the transcript does not reveal their explicit reasoning, so this is uncertain. Overall, their reward behavior reflects a conditional, reciprocity‑oriented stance: they express gratitude and approval when others behave cooperatively or improve their behavior, but they reserve most of their resources for deterring free‑riding through punishment.

They also never use the reward mechanism, even when others contribute very high amounts and even when those contributions directly increase their own payoffs. This consistent abstention suggests very low willingness to engage in costly positive reciprocity within this setup. Given that rewards here are cost-neutral in expectation (cost and benefit are equal), refusal to reward cannot be explained by pure material self-interest alone; they forgo an opportunity to express approval or attempt to reinforce cooperative partners at zero net monetary cost. This implies either that they see no instrumental value in rewarding (e.g., they do not expect it to change future behavior), or that they simply do not prioritize expressing gratitude or building goodwill via the mechanism. Their pattern does not change in response to being rewarded themselves. At times they receive some reward despite non-maximal contributions, yet they do not subsequently begin rewarding others. Thus, they do not appear to engage in “pay-it-forward” behavior within this environment, nor do they use rewards strategically as a signal of alliance or gratitude. Combining their non-use of punishment and reward, they behave as a “non-enforcer / non-signal” type with respect to the social mechanisms. They manage their strategy almost entirely via their own contribution choices, not via second-order tools. They neither sanction norm violators nor reinforce norm followers, and they do not exploit the mechanisms for reputation-building, coordination, or influence. Overall, they are a socially passive player in the meta-game of enforcement and reciprocity: they adapt only weakly to the presence of punishment and reward options and leave that relational infrastructure unused, focusing instead on opportunistic, somewhat unstable contribution decisions.

This player uses reward sparingly and in a more experimental, less principled way than punishment. Early on, they briefly reward all active peers, including both higher and lower contributors, which suggests an exploratory attempt to see whether general positivity might shift the group. Later, they occasionally reward specific individuals after relatively cooperative behavior or following periods of conflict, hinting at a loose pattern of using rewards to acknowledge effort or to repair relationships. However, they do not develop a stable rule like “always reward the highest contributor” or “reward those who increase their contribution”; rewards are not tightly aligned with clear contribution thresholds. The low effectiveness of the reward technology (one-to-one cost/benefit) likely reduces their incentive to use it systematically, and they clearly favor punishment over reward as their main social tool. Still, their intermittent use of reward shows that they are not purely hostile or spiteful; they are willing to allocate some coins to positive reinforcement or goodwill gestures when it feels salient, especially toward peers who either contribute strongly or whose behavior they want to encourage or stabilize. In summary, they are a reluctant, situational rewarder who treats reward as a secondary instrument for occasional encouragement or relationship management rather than as a consistent strategic lever.

This player is a selective and economical rewarder. They reward only in a minority of opportunities and always at low intensity. When they do spend on rewards, it is directed toward players who contribute relatively more than at least one other member, and they avoid rewarding clear low contributors. Their rewards appear aimed at reinforcing above-average or highest contributors, rather than balancing payoffs or “thanking” everyone. They ignore rewards that might compensate disadvantaged players who contributed less and earned more; instead, they use reward as a cooperation signal toward high-contribution partners. They never engage in retaliatory reward reduction or “counter-reward” behavior; if others stop rewarding them, they do not increase their own rewards in response. They also do not appear to reward in proportion to how much reward they personally receive; there is no clear reciprocity pattern tied to incoming rewards. Overall, they seem to treat rewarding as a costly, optional tool to encourage strong contributors when cooperation is fragile, and they drop it entirely when group contributions are already high and stable.

This player uses reward intermittently, in a selective and somewhat costly way, but does not rely on it systematically. They clearly are willing to sacrifice personal payoff to reinforce prosocial behavior in others and occasionally to reciprocate reward they themselves receive. Their reward allocations appear to favor higher contributors or those who maintain relatively strong contributions while others slack; they do not use reward to subsidize obvious free-riding. When they do invest in reward, they sometimes commit nontrivial amounts, suggesting a readiness to incur real costs to support group cooperation or to acknowledge particularly cooperative partners. However, they do not reward every time others cooperate, and many fully cooperative situations pass without any reward from them, indicating that they treat reward as an optional, situational tool rather than a standing obligation. They also do not appear to reward in a precise, tit-for-tat manner; the timing is noisy, implying that their reward behavior is guided by a broad impression of who “deserves” reinforcement rather than a finely calibrated strategy. They never employ “negative” reward (e.g., ignoring cooperators to favor defectors), and show no sign of strategic withholding of reward to pressure others; their use of reward is prosocial but not intensely strategic.

This player is generally reluctant to use the reward mechanism. Across the game, they very rarely spend coins to reward others, and when they do, they spend small amounts. The default for them is to grant no rewards at all. This is not because they are surrounded by defectors—many peers are highly cooperative—so the avoidance of reward seems intentional rather than forced by a hostile environment. When they do reward, it tends to occur in contexts where others are also engaging with the reward system, and where cooperative norms seem to be emerging or being reinforced. They sometimes receive rewards from others and do not immediately reciprocate, which suggests that they do not treat the reward channel as a direct gift-exchange system or as something that must be balanced. If they respond at all, their response is delayed and modest, indicating weak or indirect gratitude signaling rather than a strong “you rewarded me so I reward you” rule. They do not appear to use reward as a precise strategic instrument to shape individual partners’ behaviors. There is no clear evidence that they target specific players based on small contribution differences, nor that they systematically reward those who are exactly aligned with them and ignore those slightly below. Instead, their sparse use of rewards suggests either skepticism about the cost-effectiveness of the reward technology, low motivation to micro-manage others, or a preference to let contributions themselves coordinate behavior. Overall, they are a “minimal rewarder”: they rely primarily on their own contributions as a signal of cooperation, and they use rewards infrequently, in small doses, and without a clear tit-for-tat or strong normative pattern. Their willingness to incur personal cost to reward is present but weak.

This player never spends on rewards despite repeated opportunities. This pattern indicates a strong tendency to free-ride on the reward mechanism. They are content to let others bear the cost of incentivizing cooperation while they enjoy the benefits. There is no evidence that they use rewards strategically (e.g., to stabilize high contributions, build alliances, or express gratitude). The rare instance when they do spend to affect transfer mechanisms is directed toward reward rather than punishment (in the one case they do pay, it is to give reward, not to reduce anyone else’s payoff), but this is extremely infrequent relative to opportunities. Overall, they behave as a “non-rewarder”: they rely on contribution choices rather than reward expenditures to express approval or disapproval.

They never spend anything on rewards. This holds even when others contribute fully and when “reward the high contributors” norms are being discussed or enacted by other players. This indicates that they are not inclined to spend on positive reinforcement or expressions of gratitude in this environment. Given that rewarding is relatively inefficient, their avoidance may be driven by a payoff-maximizing calculation: spending on rewards would directly lower their own earnings without guaranteed group-level returns. There is no evidence of symbolic or expressive rewarding; they do not use rewards even in highly cooperative phases. Overall, they treat reward as dominated by simply adjusting their own contribution level, and they do not invest in building or stabilizing cooperation through costly positive signals.

The player never uses rewards, even when others’ contributions are high and sustained. This indicates that they do not invest in positive reinforcement, at least under these payoffs, and do not use rewards as a strategic signal of approval or as a way to stabilize cooperation. Given that rewarding is relatively inefficient here (cost exceeds benefit delivered), they may view it as an unattractive tradeoff and prefer to contribute directly to the public good instead. Their cooperative behavior thus stands alone; they support the group through their own contributions rather than through meta-level incentives. They do not distinguish between more- and less-cooperative partners via rewards, so partners cannot infer approval or gratitude from them within this mechanism. Overall, they fit a “quiet cooperator” profile: they contribute consistently but abstain from both costly punishment and costly reward, relying solely on their own contributions rather than incentive tools to shape group behavior.

The player never uses rewards, despite repeated exposure to peers who contribute fully. This pattern aligns with a general reluctance to spend any part of their payoff on others, whether to promote cooperation or to reciprocate kindness. They do not engage in strategic rewarding to stabilize high-contribution norms nor in expressive rewarding to signal approval. Either they place very low value on reciprocity and pro-social signaling in this context, or they prioritize personal earnings so strongly that any transfer to others is unacceptable, even at modest cost; which interpretation is more accurate is unknown. Overall, their reward behavior reinforces the picture of a player who avoids all costly social interventions and focuses narrowly on keeping their own coins.

The player also never uses the reward option, including when some peers contribute fully and consistently. This suggests they are not inclined to pay to positively reinforce cooperative behavior. They forgo opportunities to differentially support high contributors, instead treating reward much like punishment—as an unnecessary additional cost. Given that reward is relatively inefficient in this environment, their avoidance may be partly instrumental, but their consistent non-use even when cooperation levels are relatively high indicates a broader tendency not to engage in costly social sanctioning at all, positive or negative. They do not signal gratitude or attempt to shape others’ behavior through rewards; their only cooperative signal is their own contribution choice. Whether they conceptually favor reward over punishment cannot be inferred; empirically they act as a “no-sanctions” player who relies solely on their own behavior rather than on costly incentives to influence the group.

The player never rewards others, even when others contribute fully and offer them clear benefits. They does not engage in small, symbolic rewards to signal approval, nor in targeted reward of high contributors. This suggests that they do not rely on positive reinforcement as a tool to sustain cooperation and that they prioritize keeping their own payoff over mutually beneficial but individually costly rewards. Given that reward in this setting is relatively inefficient (cost exceeds transferred benefit), the player’s avoidance can be interpreted as economically rational: they seem unwilling to sacrifice their own earnings for low-leverage prosocial gestures. There is no evidence of gratitude signaling, reciprocity via rewards, or attempts to “encourage” the better contributors. Overall, the player’s type is best characterized as a payoff-conscious, non-enforcing mixed strategist: they sometimes cooperate and sometimes free-ride, avoid all costly social sanctions or encouragement, and accept the existing cooperation level without trying to reshape others’ behavior via punishment or reward.

The player never uses rewards, despite multiple opportunities to positively reinforce others’ cooperative behavior. This indicates a strong preference to avoid spending on others beyond their public good contributions. They appear to treat the contribution decision as their primary and sufficient vehicle for social preference, if any, and do not layer on additional, targeted generosity. Given the relatively inefficient reward mechanism, it is plausible (though not directly confirmed) that they view rewarding as a poor investment compared with simply contributing or free-riding. There is no evidence of gratitude signaling, targeted encouragement, or attempts to stabilize cooperation via positive incentives. In new environments, this player is best characterized as someone who expresses any prosocial inclination through participation in the public good itself, not through personalized, costly rewards.

The player never uses the reward option, even when surrounded by high contributors whose cooperation could, in principle, be reinforced. Given that rewarding is personally costly and relatively inefficient, their consistent non-use suggests they are not willing to sacrifice private payoff to express gratitude or to try to stabilize cooperation through positive incentives. They rely entirely on their own contribution decisions rather than on a strategy of shaping others’ behavior via incentives. They do not engage in selective rewarding of high contributors, generalized group rewarding, or strategic reward to encourage borderline contributors. Thus, they do not act as a “positive leader” or “rewarding norm entrepreneur” in the group. It remains unknown whether they appreciate others’ cooperation at a psychological level; behaviorally, they express no willingness to pay for signaling approval or encouragement. In type terms, they are strictly non-rewarding and adjust only their own cooperation level in response to the strategic environment.

They never use the reward option, despite being a consistent high contributor surrounded by other high contributors for much of the game and occasionally receiving rewards themselves. They do not reciprocate positive treatment in kind, do not selectively reward other high contributors, and do not attempt to reinforce cooperation via positive incentives. This points to a general unwillingness to spend on social enforcement of any kind, whether negative or positive. Their cooperative behavior is self-contained rather than strategic: they contribute fully regardless of others’ actions but make no costly moves to shape or acknowledge others’ behavior. Whether they refrain from rewarding out of payoff concerns, indifference to signaling gratitude, or adherence to a simple rule (“don’t use side-payments”) is unknown.

The player also never uses the reward mechanism, despite being a strong and stable cooperator surrounded mostly by other high contributors. They do receive rewards a few times but never reciprocate or pay it forward via the reward channel. This indicates that their cooperative norm does not translate into active, costly positive reinforcement of others. Given that they maintain cooperation even without rewarding others, their strategy appears not to rely on building a reciprocity network via rewards. Instead, they seem to treat reward and punishment symmetrically as tools they choose not to use: they neither sanction defectors nor explicitly encourage cooperators. They accept any rewards they receive but do not adopt a “gratitude signaling” pattern through reciprocal rewarding. This points to a “pure contributor” profile: they focus on their own contribution behavior and leave all incentive engineering (both positive and negative) to the rest of the group. Their cooperative actions are thus not paired with attempts to shape the social environment through side payments; their strategy is behaviorally simple, norm-driven, and enforcement-neutral.

This player shows almost no inclination to use the reward mechanism. They never voluntarily spend their own coins to increase others’ payoffs, and any rewards they receive originate from others’ actions rather than their own. There is no evidence that they attempt to reinforce cooperative peers or express gratitude through rewards, despite being surrounded by generally high contributors. Their prosocial sanctioning is entirely one-sided toward punishment, not positive reinforcement. This asymmetry suggests their social strategy emphasizes enforcing norms through negative sanctions rather than building cooperation through encouraging or rewarding good behavior. It also implies that they either discount the strategic value of rewards, consider them inefficient relative to punishment, or place little intrinsic value on positively recognizing cooperators. Overall, they are a “punishing free rider” type: personally non-cooperative but strongly inclined to penalize non-cooperation in others, and uninterested in using rewards as a tool of social regulation.

The player actively and repeatedly uses costly rewards, often at substantial personal cost. They never link rewards to immediate personal benefit, since rewards are anonymous and there is no evidence that they seek reciprocation from specific counterparts. Instead, they appear to use rewards in three ways: 1) **Norm reinforcement for contributors:** They frequently reward players who contribute fully, often selecting many or most of the high contributors rather than focusing narrowly on a few. This looks like an attempt to encourage or affirm cooperative behavior across the group. 2) **Occasional inclusion of imperfect cooperators or self‑rewarding signals:** At times they reward players whose contribution pattern is not strictly higher than others’, and they also reward themselves at least once. This suggests they may view rewards partly as generalized encouragement within the cooperative core rather than as a precise performance‑based bonus. Self‑reward in an anonymous setting may reflect a desire for symmetry or for acknowledging one’s own sustained cooperation rather than pure self‑interest, since it is much more expensive than just keeping the endowment. 3) **High generosity and low selectivity:** In several instances they distribute rewards broadly across many players in a single round, bearing a large total cost. This indicates strong prosocial or altruistic tendencies and a willingness to sacrifice significant payoff to positively recognize others. They never combine rewards with punishment; their entire enforcement style is reward‑only and non‑hostile. Their strategy seems aimed at fostering and sustaining a cooperative climate by positive reinforcement, not at disciplining defectors. Whether they believe rewards meaningfully change others’ behavior is uncertain, but their persistent use, even when group cooperation is already relatively high, suggests that they value the act of rewarding itself and the expression of approval toward cooperators.

The player never uses rewards, despite having many opportunities where others cooperate strongly and sometimes even reward them. Their own contributions are not paired with attempts to reinforce others’ high contributions, and they do not reciprocate when they themselves receive positive transfers. This suggests they do not rely on costly positive incentives as a strategy, even though rewards are available at a moderate efficiency. Their stance appears economically conservative with respect to the mechanism: they keep their payoff rather than investing in shaping others’ behavior, and they do not engage in gratitude signaling through rewards. Their cooperation, when it occurs, is thus not backed by active prosocial reinforcement; it is chosen privately, without spending to influence the group.

The player never uses the reward mechanism, despite the presence of many consistently high contributors and occasional rewards they themselves receive. This persists across all phases of the game, including periods of high cooperation when rewarding could reinforce positive behavior. Their refusal to reward holds even when the technological efficiency of reward is favorable in principle, indicating a stable preference rather than oversight. This suggests they either view reward as unnecessary given already high contributions, see it as an inefficient use of resources compared to contributing and punishing, or they conceptually separate “doing one’s fair share” from “deserving extra payoff.” In other words, they likely regard full cooperation as a baseline duty, not as something that merits explicit positive reinforcement. Their own receipt of rewards does not induce reciprocation; they do not start rewarding after being rewarded. Thus, gratitude signaling or reciprocal rewarding does not drive their behavior. They seem to rely on contribution and punishment as the primary tools for shaping group norms, and leave the reward channel unused, consistent with a strict, duty-and-sanction–oriented cooperative type rather than a warm-glow or reciprocity-driven rewarder.

The player never uses the reward mechanism, despite being surrounded by consistently high contributors. They observe many peers repeatedly investing fully in the public good, and at times they themselves receive small rewards, but they do not reciprocate or attempt to reinforce cooperative behavior via positive incentives. This indicates that they either do not value rewarding as a strategic tool or actively deem it an inefficient use of their own payoff. Even when the group’s cooperation level remains high, they do not invest in sustaining it through rewards, relying instead on others’ intrinsic or other-regulated cooperation. There is no observed signaling of gratitude, alliance formation, or selective rewarding of especially generous peers. In new environments, this type can be expected to ignore reward opportunities almost entirely, focusing on personal resource retention. If they ever do reward, it would likely be rare and situational; in the observed game there is no evidence of any positive reinforcement or prosocial signaling via rewards.

They are willing but selective rewarders. They use rewards much less often than punishment and only in certain phases of the game. When they do reward, they focus on players who consistently contribute fully, often choosing a subset of the most reliably cooperative participants. They allocate small, equal reward units across several such cooperators at once, consistent with an intention to acknowledge and reinforce high contributions broadly rather than to build a special bilateral relationship with a single partner. They do not reward occasional or marginal improvements by chronic free-riders; reward seems reserved for those who already meet a high standard of cooperation. Their reward decisions also appear partly independent of their immediate payoff incentives: rewards sometimes occur in mid-to-late stages when strategic benefits (e.g., encouraging future behavior) are not guaranteed, and they are willing to spend nontrivial amounts on them. Compared to punishment, reward remains secondary in their toolkit—used to affirm and support high cooperation but not as the primary mechanism to shape behavior. They show no sign of “gratitude-for-reward” reciprocity toward others beyond continuing their already-maximal contribution; they simply continue to cooperate fully whether they are rewarded or not. Overall, this player type is a steadfast full cooperator and strong, norm-driven sanctioner: they consistently contribute everything, heavily punish persistent free-riders, occasionally reward reliable cooperators, and maintain these patterns without softening at the end of the game.

The player never spends resources on rewarding others, even though they are themselves rewarded at times. This indicates a clear asymmetry: they are willing to punish norm violators but not to reinforce norm followers with costly positive incentives. Possible reasons are uncertain. It may be that they view full contribution as the baseline obligation, not something that merits extra pay; or they may judge the reward technology as too inefficient to justify its cost. Another possibility is that they prioritize conserving their own payoff once they have already paid cooperative and punishment costs, and so refrain from further costly prosocial acts. There is no sign that receiving rewards triggers reciprocal reward behavior; being rewarded does not change their usage of the reward tool. Overall, they behave as a “non-rewarder”: they support cooperation primarily through direct contribution and selective punishment, not through positive reinforcement or gratitude signaling.

The player essentially does not use the reward option. They never spend their own resources to reward others, despite repeatedly observing high contributions and sometimes receiving rewards themselves. This absence of rewarding is consistent and not sensitive to changes in others’ behavior or to the stage of the game. Their enforcement style is thus one-sided: they rely on negative sanctions to shape behavior, not on positive reinforcement. This could reflect several, indistinguishable from behavior alone, possibilities: they may view the reward technology as inefficient relative to punishment, may believe high contribution is a minimal expected standard that does not merit extra payment, or may simply favor deterrence over encouragement. In any case, in a new PGG environment they should be modeled as a norm-driven full cooperator who uses costly punishment against free-riders but effectively never spends on rewarding cooperators.

They show almost no inclination to reward. Across the game, they do not use the reward mechanism, despite many consistently cooperative peers. Even when others reward them, there is no observed reciprocal rewarding. This pattern implies that they see rewarding as unnecessary or inefficient compared to punishment, or they prioritize sanctioning defectors over recognizing cooperators. They never reward anyone, despite frequently being in a group with many high contributors and occasionally receiving rewards themselves. This suggests that they do not use positive incentives as a tool to reinforce cooperation, and they do not reciprocate prosocial gestures through the reward channel. Whether this reflects a belief that the reward technology is inefficient, a narrow focus on their own contribution decision, or low engagement with the secondary mechanisms is unknown.

This player uses reward sparingly and in a nuanced, conditional way. They rarely reward compared to how often they punish, which points to a stronger reliance on negative incentives than positive ones. When they do reward, they focus on individuals who previously under-contributed but then cooperate in a setting where others are mostly cooperative, or on very low contributors with whom they have a history of punitive interaction. Their rewards seem to serve two main functions. First, they sometimes use reward as a “carrot after the stick”: individuals who have been chronic non-contributors and received intense punishment from this player may later receive small rewards once they contribute in line with the group. This pattern is consistent with an attempt to reinforce improved behavior or to signal that cooperation will be met with goodwill rather than ongoing hostility. Second, reward is used to acknowledge conformity to the cooperation norm once it is reestablished, even when that conformity follows a long sequence of deviations. Investment in reward is moderate: they are willing to sacrifice some payoff to provide positive reinforcement, but not on the same intense scale as with punishment. The timing of rewards—occasionally at the very start and at the very end—indicates that they sometimes use them as bookends: initial incentives to move defectors towards cooperation, and final signals that cooperative behavior from former free-riders is recognized and approved. Overall, they are a “punishment-first, selective-reward” player. They rely primarily on deterrent sanctions to shape others’ behavior but complement this with targeted, small rewards when defectors switch to contributing, suggesting a preference for a complete norm-enforcement repertoire rather than pure hostility.

They never choose to reward anyone, even though others sometimes cooperate fully and the reward technology, while less efficient than punishment, still allows for positive reinforcement. Their behavior indicates that, in this environment, they are not willing to-sacrifice their own payoff to transfer additional coins to others. This non‑use of rewards persists even after they themselves receive small, anonymous rewards a couple of times. From this, it appears that their cooperative stance is “self‑directed” rather than reciprocity‑driven: they care about their own act of contributing but do not invest extra resources into shaping others’ behavior through incentives. They neither try to encourage high contributors nor to build relational reciprocity through mutual rewarding. Whether this reflects a purely payoff‑maximizing stance with respect to the side mechanisms, an indifference to social signaling, or a belief that the reward tool is not impactful enough to matter is unknown. Overall, they are a non‑intervening, principled full cooperator who does not use institutional levers to manage others’ behavior.

They also never use the reward mechanism, despite being a consistent high contributor and receiving rewards from others on multiple occasions. This indicates low willingness to spend resources to positively reinforce others’ cooperation. Their inaction persists even when many others contribute fully, and even when they personally benefit from others’ rewards. Given that reward here is costlier and less efficient than punishment in terms of group payoff, their reluctance may be partially compatible with efficiency concerns, but this is speculative; their behavior with punishment suggests a broader avoidance of all sanctioning activity, not a fine-tuned efficiency calculation. They appear to treat the sanctioning stage—both punishment and reward—as something to be ignored. Their strategy, then, is to cooperate fully while abstaining from both positive and negative social control. They do not engage in reinforcement dynamics, do not send signals of approval, and do not use rewards to cultivate alliances. Personality-wise, this is consistent with a “quiet principled cooperator”: they contribute to the common good but stay entirely hands-off regarding others’ choices.

The player is a frequent and generous rewarder, often spending substantial amounts to transfer benefits to others. They reward both high contributors and, at times, a historically low contributor, especially when that person increases contributions or is being heavily punished socially and materially. This shows both reinforcement of cooperative behavior and compensatory concern for disadvantaged or targeted individuals. They sometimes reward many group members in one round (including some with moderate or even weak contributions), suggesting that rewards are not purely instrumental incentives but also expressions of goodwill or attempts to stabilize group harmony. They show no strong self-protective restraint in spending on rewards, repeatedly accepting lower own payoffs to support others. Their willingness to reward persists through late rounds, including the final one, indicating that they are not just strategically investing in future reciprocity but are also motivated by intrinsic prosocial or fairness concerns.

They make regular but selective use of reward, at modest cost, especially for peers who contribute fully and/or spend on rewarding others. Their targets are typically strong cooperators or visible pro‑social spenders, and they do not use reward to support low contributors. They also do not reward simply to reciprocate every reward they receive; instead, they appear to reward in line with a broader cooperative norm, sometimes supporting players who are publicly “helping” a struggling member or those consistently contributing high amounts. Rewarding is not constant: they alternate between rounds with no reward and rounds where they spend small amounts on a few chosen partners. This suggests they use reward as targeted positive reinforcement and social support, not as a blanket reciprocity rule or as a systematic investment strategy. They never escalate to large reward expenditures; they balance generosity with payoff preservation.

They never reward anyone, even though several players contribute highly and some players expend large sums on rewarding and punishing others. This indicates that, for them, rewarding is not part of their cooperative strategy.
